With Minni sitting in the big blind with a large stack (19325), entering the pot with this weak holding risks unnecessary confrontation against a well-positioned opponent. I should wait for better opportunities in position or with stronger holdings.

I am on the button with K3s. In a 4-handed game, this is a reasonable hand to open for a raise to exert pressure on the blinds and take advantage of my position. I will raise to 300, which is a standard 3x open, to either win the blinds immediately or play a pot in position if called.

Preflop from the small blind facing a button raise to 300 (needing 250 more to call into a 450 pot). Held 6s7h, offsuit connectors with mediocre playability. While pot odds are decent (~2.8:1 effective), this hand performs poorly out of position postflop against a wide button opening range. Straight potential exists but requires perfect conditions, and implied odds are limited by stacks and multiway potential with BB behind. Folding preserves stack for premium spots in a tournament setting where survival and selective aggression maximize EV over 150 hands. Button steals often, but defending trash OOP leads to tough decisions.

Flash opened to 300 from the button. I’m in the big blind with 4♦8♥. The pot is 450; calling 200 more (total 300) gives me roughly 31% pot-equity requirement. 4-8 offsuit is poorly connected, largely dominated by a typical BTN opening range, and has very poor post‑flop equity/realization out of position. A tiny 3-bet to the min (500) has low fold equity and will often be called by better hands; a larger bluff isn’t attractive with this holding. Preserve chips and fold — this is not a profitable spot to defend or bluff.

In a 4-handed game, UTG is effectively the Cutoff position. Queen-Jack offsuit is a standard opening hand in this position. My stack size of 7900 (79 BB) is healthy, allowing me to play aggressively. I will open for 3x the big blind, which has been my consistent sizing in previous hands, to put pressure on the blinds and potentially take the pot down early or play in position post-flop.

Preflop on the button with A2s (suited Ace-Deuce). Flash raised to 300 from UTG in a 4-handed game, which is an early position open and likely decently strong but not nutted given the sizing (3x). A2s plays well multiway postflop due to suit and ace blocker, and I have position on everyone. Pot odds are favorable: calling 300 into 450 gives about 2.5:1 implied odds, and my hand has good equity (roughly 35-45% vs. a typical UTG range) with straight/flush potential. SB and BB can act, potentially creating a multiway pot where position shines. Folding is too tight on button; 3-betting A2s as a bluff is possible but risky with deep stacks and uncertain fold equity against Flash, who has shown aggression. Calling maintains flexibility and pot control.

Fold. 3♦6♥ is a very weak offsuit hand with almost no playability out of position. Flash opened UTG to 300 (a relatively strong range) and the Button called, so this is at least a three-way pot. Calling 250 into a 750 pot requires ~25% equity to break even preflop; 3‑6o won't have that vs two calling ranges and will struggle to realize equity postflop. A min‑raise/squeeze here would be a speculative bluff against big stacks and an UTG opener — not worth risking chips. Preserve the stack and wait for a better spot.

I'm in the Big Blind with 4d5d, facing a 200 chip call. Flash raised UTG to 300, Elon called on the button, and Minni folded from the SB. 

4d5d is one of the weakest hands in poker. While it contains the wheel (A-2-3-4-5) straight potential, that's unlikely to materialize, and this hand has almost no current value. I'm out of position for the entire hand, and calling 200 more chips would put me in a difficult spot post-flop in a 3-way pot where both opponents have already shown strength.

With a 5250 stack and 150 hands remaining, I should preserve chips for premium hands and favorable positions rather than playing a dominated hand out of position. The math doesn't support calling here - I'm relying on improving to a made hand, which is unlikely against two opponents who've indicated strength.

Folding is the mathematically correct and strategically sound play.

As the pre-flop aggressor, I'm continuing with a standard continuation bet on this relatively coordinated but low-card board. My Qh Js holds two overcards and a backdoor straight draw. By betting 400 into a 750 pot, I aim to fold out hands like small pocket pairs, A-high, or other broadway cards that missed. This sizing maintains pressure while allowing me to reassess if I face resistance from Elon, who is in position.

Flop hits me with bottom pair (2h pairing the 2s) but with a weak ace kicker against Flash's strong UTG raising range. Board has two spades (9s 2s), introducing flush draws, and possible straight draws (T7). Flash's 400 bet (1/3 pot) into 1150 is standard continuation, representing overpairs (TT+), sets (88/22/99), two-pairs (98/82), or strong draws. My equity is low (~25-30% vs. typical range) with no real draw (backdoor hearts at best). Calling commits to multi-street defense out of position relatively (though button), and raising is bluff without fold equity history. Deep stacks (70bb), but folding preserves stack for better spots; pre-flop call was speculative for position/multiway, but heads-up flop bet folds out. Standard fold.

Qh5c is a trash hand offsuit from UTG in a 4-handed game. It has poor playability, low equity against typical raising ranges, and is easily dominated by better queens. No position advantage, and with deep stacks, better to wait for stronger holdings to maximize EV over the tournament. Standard fold to avoid spew.

Button with K♣J♣ (Kh Jc) — a strong, playable hand in position. I want initiative and fold equity versus the blinds, so I’ll open instead of limp. Min-raising to 200 is too small (invites callers/3-bets); a 3x sizing to 300 gives decent fold equity, narrows ranges, and still leaves deep stacks to manoeuvre postflop. If Claude (SB) or Flash (BB) 3-bet I’ll re-evaluate (often fold or proceed cautiously out of position); if called I’ll use position to extract value or bluff as appropriate. Raise to 300.

Folding K6o from small blind against button raise. This is a very weak hand that plays poorly out of position. While Minni is aggressive, I shouldn't burn chips on marginal hands with 52 big blinds. K6o is unlikely to have sufficient equity post-flop, and I'll be in an uncomfortable position. Folding preserves my stack for better opportunities.

I am in the Big Blind with K2o. Minni, the chip leader on the Button, has raised to 300. While Minni is opening frequently, K2o is a very weak hand to defend with out of position. The pot odds are about 2.25:1, but the lack of playability and poor position make this a fold.

UTG with 3♦2♣ offsuit — a junk hand with almost no post‑flop playability and I’ll be out of position against multiple deep stacks (Claude 5,200; Flash 8,200; Elon 7,025). The required call (100) or a min‑raise to 200 might be cheap relative to my stack, but limp‑calling or opening with 3‑2o UTG is negative EV: I’ll often be dominated and forced to play difficult pots multiway. Preserve chips and wait for a better spot in position or with a real hand. Fold.

Folding 6c 3c (trash hand) on the button. Despite positional advantage, this hand lacks showdown value and post-flop playability. Preserving stack for better spots is the correct strategy with 52 BB remaining in a 4-handed tournament at hand 27/150.

I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Deuce offsuit. Both the UTG and Button have folded, leaving only the Big Blind, Elon, to act. While this is a blind-vs-blind situation where I could theoretically steal, Queen-Deuce is a very weak hand that plays poorly out of position. Elon has shown enough aggression in previous hands that I'd rather not invest chips into a pot where I am likely to be behind or forced to play a difficult post-flop game. Folding is the most prudent play here.

As6o (Ace-Six offsuit) is a weak hand that doesn't meet the threshold for opening from UTG, the earliest position. While the pot offers 2.5:1 odds on the 100 call, several factors support folding:

1. **Position**: UTG is the worst position pre-flop with full action remaining. Calling weak hands from early position is a long-term losing strategy.

2. **Hand Quality**: As6o has limited playability even with an ace - the six kicker is very weak, leading to marginal situations post-flop.

3. **Stack Consideration**: At 5200 chips (52BB), I'm the short stack at the table. I should preserve chips for premium hands where I have better equity and position.

4. **Opponent Strength**: Minni (19325) is the chip leader and has been aggressive; Elon and Flash both have healthy stacks. In a 4-way pot from early position with a weak hand, the equity situation becomes difficult.

Folding keeps my chip stack intact for better opportunities with stronger holdings in improved positions.

I am on the button with Queen-Eight offsuit. In a 4-player game, this is a marginal hand. Claude has folded UTG. While opening from the button is standard with a wide range, Queen-Eight offsuit is at the bottom of that range. Given that Minni (the Big Blind) has a very large stack and has shown aggression in previous hands, I expect to be called or re-raised frequently. I'll choose to fold here and wait for a better spot to utilize my position, as this hand doesn't play well post-flop if dominated.

Preflop in SB, folded to me heads-up vs BB Minni. QJo offsuit is a strong SB opening range hand with good equity against a typical BB defending range (~43-45% equity vs loose calls). Playable postflop for top pair/top kicker potential or straight draws. Raising small takes initiative, has fold equity on blinds (pot 150, risk 250 post-SB for ~2.5bb raise), and builds pot when ahead. Calling is too passive OOP, folding trashy. Minni aggressive postflop but deep stacks allow maneuvering. Standard small raise to 3bb.

I have pocket sixes (6♠6♣) in the big blind facing a single SB open to 300 (Elon). Stacks are deep (me ~193bb, Elon ~68bb), so set‑mining has good implied odds. Calling 200 more (300 total committed) keeps the pot under control out of position and allows me to realize equity or hit a set; a small 3‑bet is an option but would bloat the pot OOP and invite 4‑bets. Plan: call to see the flop; if I hit a set or a very favorable texture I will build the pot, otherwise proceed cautiously versus significant aggression.

Preflop raise with QJo from SB was standard to take initiative OOP with a playable broadway hand. Flop Ac5c7d completely misses my range entirely - no pair, no draws, queen-high with wrong suits. This board favors Minni's BB calling range heavily (Ax, pocket pairs, suited connectors like 65s/68s/34s for straights/flushes). As first to act OOP with air and minimal showdown value, betting is -EV: risks stack without equity, no fold equity vs calling station tendencies from history (e.g., Hand 2, Hand 14). Checking allows pot control, realizes equity when behind (she checks back weaker hands), and sets up cheap fold to her frequent continuation bets (observed aggression postflop). Deep stacks but no need to bluff here - save barrels for better spots. Clear check-fold.

Check. I called preflop as a set‑mining play with 6♠6♣; the flop A♣‑5♣‑7♦ is unfavorable — I’m an underpair to likely Ace-containing portions of Elon’s raising range and there are two clubs on board (I only have one club backdoor). Betting OOP with just a pair here would bloat the pot and put me in tough spots vs raises or big turns. With deep stacks I prefer pot control and to realize equity — check to see a free turn. Plan: if I hit a set I’ll build the pot; if Elon fires small on the turn I can call to continue set‑mining but will fold to substantial aggression unless I improve.

Turn board Ac5c7d6h still completely misses my QJo - queen-high with no pair, no flush draw, no straight draw (gutshot at best unlikely). Minni checked flop behind, indicating possible weakness or trap, but her BB calling range vs SB open hits this board hard (Aces, sets, two-pair combos like A7/A6/76/54, flush draws, open-enders like 48/89/34). As OOP first to act with pure air and minimal showdown value, betting commits further without fold equity against her history of calling stations (e.g., multi-street calls in Hand 2, Hand 14) and risks getting raised off entirely. Checking realizes equity for free (she may check back worse), controls pot, and allows cheap river decision or fold to her frequent bets. Deep stacks but no profitable bluff spot here - save for better dynamics. Clear check, prepared to fold river unless check-check.

I turned a set (6♠6♣ on Ac–5c–7d–6h). Elon checked both flop and turn, so now is the time to start building the pot and charge A‑x and club draws rather than giving a free river. I’ll make a value/protection bet of ~2/3 pot: 400 into 600 to extract calls from worse A‑highs and draws while denying equity. If he check‑raises large I’ll re‑evaluate (likely call small/medium raises, be cautious vs polarized overbets), but on this street I want to build value with my made hand.

Facing a 400 bet into 1000 on the turn board Ac5c7d6h with QdJs (queen-high, no pair, no draws, gutshot unlikely). Preflop as SB raiser with QJo, missed flop entirely (checked), checked turn expecting value or fold equity from Minni, but she bets after checking flop behind, which strengthens her range significantly: likely Ax (esp A7/A6/A5), two-pair combos (76/54/A6), sets, or turned pairs/improved draws (e.g., 86/46 for straights, flush draws betting). My queen-high has minimal showdown value OOP vs her BB calling range that hits this board hard. Pot odds ~3.5:1 require ~22% equity, but I have <15% vs value-heavy range; even vs polarized, history shows Minni calls stations (Hand 2 multi-street calls, Hand 14 deep calls) but here her check-flop-then-bet line polarizes to value over bluffs. Raising has no fold equity (deep stacks, her aggression), calling bloats pot OOP with air for river disaster. Standard fold preserves stack for better spots; deep effective stacks (my 6875 >13bb pot) allow waiting. Previous plan was check-fold river to bet.

I am dealt 2c 3h in the UTG position. This is the weakest possible starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Given the position and the negative expected value of playing such a hand, there is no strategic reason to enter the pot. Folding is the only viable professional play here.

65h is a strong suited connector on the button in an unraised pot after UTG fold. Limping (calling 100) is optimal to see a cheap flop in position, likely multiway given opponents' tendencies to call from blinds. This maximizes implied odds against deep-stacked SB Minni and allows postflop playability where flush/straight draws shine. Folding is too tight; raising adds unnecessary variance preflop with a speculative hand that prefers multiway pots.

I have A♥T♣ in the small blind facing a limp from the button and the big blind. This is a strong, playable hand that plays well postflop but is harder to realize value out of multiway and out of position. Calling the 50 would be passive and likely leave me in a 3‑way pot out of position. A min-raise (200) is too small — it invites loose defenders. I want a clear initiative/isolation vs the limp while keeping the size reasonable relative to deep stacks. Raising to 400 (total) is large enough to fold out many speculative hands and isolate the button or get heads-up vs the BB, but not so large as to overcommit. If called, I can c-bet and leverage my IP advantage vs a single opponent; if both fold I pick up the pot immediately.

Folding Ts6s from the big blind against Minni's raise. This is a weak hand with poor equity out of position. Despite Minni's aggressive tendencies, Ts6s doesn't have sufficient strength to justify calling 300 chips and building a pot out of position. Pot odds are 2:1 which isn't adequate for this holding. Preserving stack for better spots is the disciplined play.

65h remains a strong speculative hand on the button facing a SB raise after my limp. Minni's raise range is wide (SB vs button limper), including many suited connectors, broadways, Ax suited that I dominate or have equity against. Calling 300 more (4.4% of stack) into a 900 pot offers excellent implied odds deep vs her 19k stack, especially with position heads-up. Postflop playability is superb for flush/straight draws, wraps, etc. Folding is too tight; 3-betting adds variance without clear edge as she calls wide and I'm not strong enough for value. History shows Minni aggressive but callable in position.

I raised preflop and am heads-up with A♥T♣ on T♥ 4♠ 7♠ — top pair with the best kicker on a two‑spade board. This is a straightforward continuation-bet spot: I want value from worse tens, second pairs and medium pocket pairs while charging spade draws and denying free cards. Elon callers from the button have mixed ranges (pairs, broadways, spade draws) and can call a sizable bet. I’ll bet ~2/3 pot to both protect and extract value: 600 into the 900 pot. If he folds I take it down; if he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn; if he check‑raises big I’ll proceed cautiously given his shown aggression, folding to extreme sizing or calling/raising depending on sizing and reads.

Flop board Th4s7s with 65h gives me an open-ended straight draw (3 or 8 makes the straight: 3-4-5-6-7 or 4-5-6-7-8), plus backdoor heart flush draw (one heart on board) and backdoor spade flush potential. Strong speculative playability deep-stacked with position on SB Minni. Pot 1500, calling 600 (pot odds ~22%, need ~28% equity to 2100 pot) is excellent as my equity is 35-45% against her wide SB c-bet range (overpairs, top pair, weaker pairs, spade draws, floats) based on history of aggressive betting but frequent calls/folds. Raising risks unnecessary variance vs her deep stack and calling range; folding is too tight given implied odds (~18k stack) and position for turn control/realizing equity. History (e.g., Hand 2,14,17) shows Minni bets wide post OOP raise but vulnerable to draws in position.

I hold top pair with the best kicker (A♥T♣) on T♥ 4♠ 7♠ 9♦. Heads-up pot is 2,100 after I c-bet 600 on the flop and Elon called — his calling range contains worse tens, second pairs, underpairs and single-spade/connector draws. The 9♦ on the turn only improves a small slice of his range (9x, some straights) and doesn’t meaningfully complete spade flushes (only a river spade plus double-spade hole cards would). I’m still ahead of a large portion of his calling range and want to extract value while denying free cards, so I’ll continue with a 2/3-pot turn bet to charge worse tens, 7s/4s and underpairs and price out backdoor equity. If he calls I’ll re-evaluate the river; if he check-raises small I will likely call and proceed cautiously on the river, but I will respect a large polarized shove and fold enough of my range to avoid costly calls.

Turn board Th4s7s9d with 65h gives an open-ended straight draw (8 outs: any 3 or 8 for 3-4-5-6-7 or 4-5-6-7-8 straight), backdoor heart flush draw, and overall strong playability. Pot 3500, Minni's 1400 bet (~40% pot) from SB OOP after leading flop fits her aggressive history (wide c-betting ranges including pairs, draws, Ax suited, broadways). My equity is ~32-40% vs her range (GTO sims show favorable), pot odds 1400/4900=28.6% excellent. Deep effective stacks (my 5875 ->4475 behind, her 17k) provide huge implied odds for river realization in position. Calling maintains pot control, induces bluffs/value from worse (e.g., T,7, spades draws), exploits her tendency to overbet wide but fold weaker rivers (as in hands 14,17,28). Raising semi-bluff has fold equity but risks variance vs her calling station tendencies and stacks her deep when drawing; folding surrenders too much equity/implied. Optimal to call IP for max EV.

I have top pair with the best kicker (A♥T♣) on T♥ 4♠ 7♠ 9♦ Kc. The pot is 4,900 and Elon (button) has passively called my flop (600) and turn (1,400) bets. His line is more consistent with stubborn one-pair hands (weaker tens), 9x, underpairs and some missed spade/connector draws than a K-heavy range, so I expect more worse hands in his calling range than river-K hands that now beat me. I want to extract value from those worse tens/9x/medium pairs while keeping the pot controllable against the possible Kx. A ~half-pot value bet does that: large enough to get value from worse made hands and charge thin calls, but not so large that I become pot-committed to a single raise. I’ll bet 2,500 chips — if he folds I take it down, if he calls I often have the best hand, and if he jams a polarized shove I’ll re-evaluate but give significant credit to a very strong hand and avoid automatically spewing chips.

River Kc bricks my open-ended straight draw completely (missed 3 or 8), leaving 65h with absolute air—no pair, no flush draw, no straight—on a coordinated board (Th4s7s9dKc) ripe for straights (e.g., 68 for 6-7-8-9-T), two-pair combos (T7,79, etc.), top pairs (TK,K9), and spade flushes/draws. Minni's aggressive triple-barrel line OOP after raising my limp (preflop raise, flop 600 into ~1100, turn 1400 into ~2700, river 2500 into ~5300 pre-call) polarizes her range heavily to value (Tx+, 97s+, sets, straights) given her history of leading strong boards and extracting value (e.g., Hands 14,17,20,28 where she bets multi-street with pairs/top ends). Bluff % low as positionally disadvantaged and board favors her initiative. Pot odds 2500/9900 ≈25% require 32-40% equity vs value-heavy range (actual <15% equity), and calling commits ~56% stack (4475→1975) into massive reverse implied odds (she pays off only if worse air, unlikely). Raising all-in (4475) has poor fold equity vs her deep stack/calling station tendencies (e.g., Hand 2 call down); pure spew. Folding preserves 4475 stack (top 25% of field?) for 121 hands left, optimal discipline with speculative hand that whiffed despite great pre/triple-call play.

9d Ts is a marginal offsuit connector in early position (UTG) with 4 players. Standard play is to fold preflop to avoid playing out of position multiway with a hand that doesn't play well postflop. My stack is ~45BB, pot is small, no need to speculate. Opponents are deep/aggressive, better to wait for better spots.

On the button with Q♠5♣ — a marginal, non-suited queen. There are two players to act (small blind Claude and big blind Flash) who have shown willingness to defend and be aggressive, so open-stealing with this hand is risky and likely to get called or 3‑bet. I prefer to use my positional advantage and see a flop cheaply. Calling 100 keeps the pot small, preserves my deep stack, and lets me exploit post‑flop if I hit (top pair or a good draw). I will fold to a significant preflop 3‑bet and play cautiously postflop unless I pick up real equity.

Calling to complete the small blind. A2o gets excellent 5:1 pot odds (50 to win 250). Even though this is a weak hand, folding away my 100 SB investment for just 50 more is poor bankroll management. The ace has value and I'm likely seeing a flop in a multi-way pot. Pot odds mathematically justify the call.

I am in the Big Blind with K4o. The Button (Minni) has limped and the Small Blind (Claude) has completed. There is no need to raise here and build a pot out of position with a marginal hand like King-four offsuit. Checking allows me to see a free flop and realize my equity without additional risk.

Checking with A2o on a 9-2-7 board. I have the weakest possible pair (pair of 2s) with a poor kicker. Being first to act from the small blind out of position with a marginal hand, checking allows me to see how Minni and Flash respond before committing more chips. This is safer than betting a weak hand that's unlikely to have the best of it. I can reassess on the turn based on their actions.

In a limped pot with three players and holding King-high on a board of 9-2-7 with a heart draw, I have some marginal showdown value but my hand is not strong enough to bet for value. Checking is the most prudent play to see the turn for free and keep the pot small, especially since the small blind has already checked to me.

Flop: 9c 2h 7h. I hold Qs5c — no pair, no heart draw, just queen‑high. Pot is small (300) and both blinds checked, leaving me in position against two opponents. In a multiway spot with a weak hand I don't have much fold equity — a c‑bet is unlikely to fold out both callers and will just turn into a bloated pot OOP. I prefer pot control and to use my positional leverage to see a turn card for free. Plan: check and re‑evaluate on the turn; call small bets for pot odds if villains look wide, but fold to sizeable protection bets or raises unless I improve (or get a favourable runout to credibly bluff).

On the turn with A2o, I have a pair of deuces with an ace kicker—a very weak holding. The board is 9c-2h-7h-4s with no draws.

Key considerations:
1. Hand strength: My pair of 2s is vulnerable and likely behind many hands Minni and Flash could have
2. Position: I'm out of position (SB) with action first, which is a disadvantage
3. Board texture: The 4 on the turn doesn't help my hand and could have completed draws or paired someone else
4. Pot dynamics: Everyone checked the flop, suggesting weak holdings across the board, but the turn brings new information
5. Stack management: With 5000 chips, I shouldn't be gambling with marginal holdings

Betting this weak hand risks:
- Getting check-raised by a stronger hand
- Calling down and losing more chips in a losing situation
- Bloating the pot when I'm likely behind

Checking allows me to:
- See the river cheaply with minimal commitment
- Gather information on how opponents respond
- Preserve chips for stronger hands later in the tournament (hand 30 of 150)

This is a clear check. I'm playing pot control with a weak hand from out of position.

On the turn, I have improved to bottom pair with a King kicker. The pot remains small as it was limped pre-flop and checked around on the flop. Claude has checked again. While I have improved, a pair of fours is still quite weak and vulnerable to any 7, 9, or better. Betting here might force out complete air but will likely be called by any better hand or draw. Checking is a safe way to continue controlling the pot size and reach a showdown with my marginal hand.

I check. Holding Q♠5♣ on 9c 2h 7h 4s in a three‑way pot I have no pair and no heart draw; my fold equity is negligible multiway and both blinds have shown willingness to call. The pot is small (300) relative to our stack, so betting here to steal is low EV — it will often be called or raised by better hands and just bloats the pot out of position. Pot control and seeing a free river is the highest EV line: check and re‑evaluate on the river. If I face a small river bet (cheap) I can call to try and catch or realize showdown; I will fold to sizable protection bets/raises unless I improve (Q or 5) or get a clear read to bluff.

River is complete (9c-2h-7h-4s-Jh). I hold A-2o, giving me a pair of deuces with an ace kicker—an extremely weak hand. 

Key factors:
1. **Hand strength**: Pair of 2s is likely behind most reasonable holdings that reached showdown. The Jack on the river is unlikely to have helped me and could have made trips, pairs, or two pairs for opponents.

2. **Action pattern**: All three players checked flop and turn, indicating cautious play and weak holdings across the board. However, the river Jack changes the dynamic.

3. **Risk-reward**: Betting this weak hand risks:
   - Getting called by stronger hands (9x, Jx, 7x, 4x, any overcards with better kickers)
   - Getting check-raised on the river with a marginal holding
   - Losing additional chips with a losing hand

4. **Stack preservation**: With 5000 chips and 120 hands remaining, preserving chips for premium hands is critical.

5. **Position & information**: Checking allows me to see showdown cheaply or win the pot if everyone else checks. If either opponent bets, I can fold and save chips.

Best line is to check and go to showdown. This is pot control with a weak hand—no need to create unnecessary losses.

I have bottom pair (fours) with a King kicker. The board has a completed flush draw and several cards higher than my pair (Jack, Nine, Seven). Since the pot was limped pre-flop and checked through the flop and turn, it is possible my pair of fours is currently the best hand, but it is too weak to bet for value. Checking allows me to reach a showdown for free if the Button also checks, or to evaluate a small bet from an opponent without having committed more chips to a very small pot.

Check. I'm in a three‑way pot with Qs5c and have no pair, no heart draw and essentially zero showdown value on 9c 2h 7h 4s Jh. Both opponents checked the turn and river, which makes a multiway bluff here very low EV — I need both players to fold and the pot is tiny (300) relative to my stack. I preserve chips and take the free showdown; if someone now bets a trivial amount I might call for pot odds, but I'll fold to any meaningful river protection bet or raise.

I'm UTG in a 4-handed game so opening ranges should be quite wide. J♦9♠ is playable heads-up and postflop with these deep stacks. Pot is 150, to-call 100 and min-raise is 200, but I want to take the initiative and avoid a multiway limp. I size to a standard 3x BB (300 total) to get fold equity, define ranges, and keep options postflop. Plan: c-bet favorable textures, play cautiously if I miss and face heavy action. Against a small 3-bet I'll often continue; I'll fold to large 4-bet shoves from the shorter stacks.

A2o (Ace-Deuce offsuit) is one of the weakest hands in poker. While I have the advantage of position on the button, several factors make folding the correct play:

1. **Poor hand strength**: A2o ranks near the bottom of the hand spectrum. Even when I hit an ace, I'm vulnerable to kicker problems against an opponent who raised from UTG.

2. **Minni's raising pattern**: Looking at her recent hands, she's shown consistent aggression and strength, particularly from early positions. Her raise from UTG suggests a legitimate hand.

3. **Unfavorable dynamics**: The pot is 450 with 300 to call, but postflop I'd need to hit to proceed with confidence. The risk-reward isn't favorable - I'm risking 300 chips now with a weak hand that flops poorly most of the time.

4. **Stack considerations**: At 5000 chips, I'm not desperate yet but should avoid marginal spots. I should preserve chips for stronger hands where I have better equity.

Flash 3‑bet preflop and barreled flop and turn, so his continuing range is weighted toward Ax, strong broadways and some bluffs. I hold QcJh — second/top pair with a decent kicker, but the board has an Ace, a paired trey and the river 4♦ completes more diamond/board texture, so my hand is vulnerable to many better holdings (A‑x, sets/full houses, and completed backdoor combos). Flash checked the river, which could be weakness or a trap, but this is a dangerous card to bluff on and value is thin (I only get called by worse Qx or weak pairs, and get raised/called by better hands). I’ll pot‑control and take showdown: check.

I have top pair, top kicker with Ace-King. The river card, the Four of Diamonds, completes a potential diamond flush draw. Minni has check-called my bets on both the flop and the turn. While it is possible she is holding a weaker Ace like AJ or AT, the risk of her having backed into a flush or holding a slow-played two pair (AQ) makes checking the safest and most logical play. By checking back, I realize my hand's showdown value and win the pot against her weaker range without risking my remaining stack to a check-raise.

On the button with A♥7♦ this is a clear open-raise spot. I have position on both blinds, an ace-blocker, and a deep stack (21975) so I want to take the initiative and fold out weaker holdings while building pots when I have advantage. Min raise is 200 but I size to a standard ~3x the BB (300) — big enough to apply pressure to the blinds (SB 13150, BB 4725) without bloating the pot unnecessarily. If I face a 3-bet I'll re-evaluate based on sizing and opponent; for now open to 300 to steal and play postflop in position.

I am in the Small Blind with 9s 7h. The Button (Minni) has opened for 3x (300). While the pot odds to call are roughly 2.8:1, 97 offsuit is a very weak hand to play out of position against a big-stack aggressive player. Additionally, Elon in the Big Blind is relatively short-stacked (~47 BBs) and could potentially squeeze or jam. Given the 150-hand structure and the risk of being outplayed post-flop with a marginal hand, the most prudent professional decision is to fold and wait for a more advantageous situation.

Preflop in the big blind facing a button raise to 300 after SB folds. Pot is 450, need 200 more to call (pot odds ~3.25:1). Hand is Ts4s, suited with straight potential. Button range is wide (aggressive player from history, frequent raises). Standard BB defense vs BTN open includes suited connectors/gappers like T4s for playability (flush draws, wheel straights, gutshots). Folding is too tight; 3-betting light risky with short stack and hand not suited for it. Calling cheaply to realize equity heads-up, control pot OOP, and use position postflop. Stack 4725 effective ~47BB, good for speculative hands.

Flop is Td 6c 6h, paired board with top pair (Ts) but extremely weak 4 kicker OOP vs aggressive button raiser (Minni). Preflop call with Ts4s was standard speculative defense. No strong draws (backdoor spade FD at best). Vulnerable to better Ts (TJ+), overpairs, sets (66), or two-pair combos in her wide button range. Leading OOP risks too much into likely check-raises or calls; better to check for pot control, realize equity OOP, induce bluffs/check-behinds from her missed draws/air, and fold to aggression if warranted (poor kicker limits calling big bets). Her history shows frequent aggression, so check exploits her betting range while preserving stack ~46BB deep.

I opened from the button to 300 and the BB just called. Flop T♦6♣6♥ — I have A♥7♦: no pair, no real draw. The board is paired and heavily favors any pocket pair (they now have two‑pair/trips/quads), so my hand has limited showdown value. That said I’m first to act in position and can use fold equity to take this pot down or keep it small if called. I’ll make a standard small continuation bet (~1/3 pot = 225) — big enough to fold miss‑hands and overcards, but small enough not to bloat the pot against the many pocket pairs that dominate me. Plan: if he folds I pick up the pot; if he calls I proceed cautiously (check blanks, value-bet/raise only on an Ace or a very safe card); if he check‑raises big I’ll assume a very strong hand and fold.

Facing a small 1/3 pot continuation bet (225 into 650) on a paired 6s board with top pair (Ts) and weak 4 kicker OOP vs aggressive button raiser Minni with wide range. Pot odds ~3.9:1 are excellent for calling with decent showdown equity against her bluffs, Ax, KQo+, weaker pairs, and missed draws from suited connectors. Weak kicker is a concern (vulnerable to T9+, 66, T6), but bet sizing too small to fold top pair; raising unprofitable as hand plays poorly post-raise and OOP. Calling preserves stack (only 5% of 45BB), controls pot size, realizes equity heads-up, induces further bluffs, and positions to fold turn if she shows strength while hitting sets/straights less likely than air/value. Her history of frequent small bets aligns with balanced C-bet; standard BB defense continues here.

Heads-up on turn, OOP with top pair (Ts) and weak 4 kicker on paired 66 board with Qh turn bringing potential heart flush draws and broadway blockers. Pot 1100 (~11BB), my stack 4300 (~43BB effective vs Minni's deep stack). Preflop speculative call with Ts4s was fine vs wide button range; flop call of small c-bet realized equity cheaply while controlling pot. Turn Qh is a neutral card for my hand (no improvement beyond backdoor spades), but improves some draws Axh, KQh in her range and possible two-pair Q6, QT. Betting OOP thin value risks check-raises from better hands (T9+, 66, T6, Q6) or floats; unprofitable as hand plays poorly post-raise. Checking exploits her aggression (history of frequent small bets/raises postflop, e.g., Hands 2,9,14,17,20,29,37), induces bluffs/missed draws (KJ, suited connectors, Ax), allows cheap showdown with equity vs worse pairs/bluffs, and preserves stack to fold to strength if shown. Positionally sound BB defense continues; pot control prioritizes survival in tournament with 112 hands left.

I opened, c‑bet small on the flop and Elon (BB) called — his flop calling range is heavy with pocket pairs (66, 77–99, TT), some tens and a few floats. The turn Q♥ is a blank for A♥7♦: it doesn’t pair me but it helps a lot of hands in his range (Qx, TQ, a slow‑played 66). I only have ace‑high (with a heart blocker), so my showdown value is poor and my fold equity is limited against a player who just called a flop c‑bet. Betting here would be a large bluff into a range that will often call or can shove, which risks a big chunk of the effective stack for little equity. I’ll control the pot and use position — check behind on the turn. If he leads into me I’ll fold to significant aggression; if he checks back I’ll re‑evaluate the river (look to value only with an Ace or bluff selectively on a very favourable runout).

River Th pairs the board Tens, giving me trip Tens (Td, Th, Ts) with 4s kicker—a monster hand on this paired 6s board with heart flush possible. Previously pot-controlled OOP with marginal top pair weak kicker vs aggressive button Minni (wide range: Ax, KQ, suited connectors, pairs), calling small flop c-bet and both checking turn (her check-behind suggests medium strength like pair/A-high/draw rather than sets/overpairs). Now ~4:1 pot odds irrelevant as first to act; betting extracts max value from her worse hands (Qx, 6x, Tx weak, two-pair Q6/T6 unlikely, bluffs/missed draws/flush draws that don't beat trips). Avoid check-back losing value or check-raise OOP vulnerability. Bet ~2/3 pot (700 into 1100) balances value vs her calling range (one pair+, flush draws), induces calls from Q-high/T-high/6x while oversized for pure bluffs; protects vs unlikely full houses (66/T6). Deep effective stacks (43BB), but tournament stage favors value-betting strong hands vs her history of calling stations/aggression (Hands 2,14,20,29,37). Positions perfectly for thin value OOP.

Board: T♦ 6♣ 6♥ | Q♥ | T♥ — I have A♥7♦: no pair, only ace-high. I c-bet a small 1/3 pot on the flop and Elon called, which on this texture is dominated by made hands (66, TT, 77–99, Qx, QT/full-house possibilities). He checks the turn and then fires a smallish river bet of 700 into a 1800 pot (pot would be 2500 after his bet). Calling 700 to win 2500 gives ~21.7% required equity — my A‑high is only ahead of rare bluffs or unpaired floaters and behind the bulk of his flop‑calling range. Raising would be a pure bluff with little fold equity against a player who called the flop and now bets the river; it also needlessly risks chips vs hands that already beat me. With a deep stack and plenty of future spots, the +EV play here is to fold and conserve chips rather than chase a slim chance.

I am on the button with 7-6 offsuit in a three-handed game. While the button usually opens a wide range, 7-6 offsuit is quite weak, especially against Minni in the big blind who has shown a high level of aggression and a tendency to defend or lead out. Elon in the small blind has a relatively short stack and might look to shove over an open. Given the current dynamics and the fact that I want to preserve my stack for better opportunities, folding this marginal hand is the most prudent play.

8c5s is a trash hand: offsuit, gapped, low cards, poor playability and equity. In small blind vs big blind after button fold, completing 50 into 200 (post-call pot) offers pot odds but commits me OOP post-flop with a hand unlikely to make strong hands or realize equity well. My stack (5350, ~35BB) is playable; folding 0.5BB preserves chips for better spots. No read suggests Minni's BB range is exploitable enough to justify limping. Raising is -EV with this hand. Standard fold.

Preflop on the button with 7s5h offsuit, a weak speculative hand with poor playability and low equity against calling ranges. Facing large stacks in blinds who have shown aggression in history. No need to risk chips with trash when better spots will come on button. Folding preserves stack for premium opportunities in tournament.

Heads-up against Flash with Jh4h in the small blind. Stacks: me 21,450, Flash 13,050 (effective ~13,050). Pot is 150 and I need to put in 50 more (min raise to 200 if I want to raise). J4 suited is marginal but has flush/backdoor potential and reasonable equity heads-up. Calling 50 gets me to a 200 pot (post-call), so I’m getting ~25% pot odds — cheap enough to see a flop and use postflop play to extract value or fold if I miss and face heavy action. Raising to 200 with this hand is unnecessary and unnecessarily bloats the pot with a weak holding. I’ll call to see a flop, then proceed cautiously: fold to large turn/river aggression without improvement, value/cbet when I connect, and use pot control with marginal hands.

I am in the Big Blind with Jd 7h. Elon folded on the button, and Minni (Small Blind) just completed the blind. Since I can see a free flop with a mediocre hand and I will have position post-flop, there is no need to raise and inflate the pot. Checking is the most standard and safe play here.

I limped preflop to see a cheap flop with Jh4h. On 8s-9c-6c I have no pair and no realistic draw (backdoor hearts flush is impossible with only two cards to come). Flash defended a wide BB range preflop, so his range contains many pairs and club draws that dominate me. Pot is tiny (200) and there’s little to gain by bluffing into a willing caller—betting just bloats the pot with a hand that has almost no equity. I’ll check and exercise pot control: see how he reacts on the turn and fold to meaningful aggression. If he checks back I get a free card; if he leads a small probe I may call selectively depending on sizing and reads, but I’ll fold to a larger bet.

I have an open-ended straight draw (5 or 10 completes it) on a board of 8s-9c-6c. I am in position against Minni in a limped pot. Minni checked to me. While I could bet to try and take the pot down now or build it up for when I hit, checking back allows me to see the turn for free and realize my equity without the risk of being check-raised off my draw. Given the low pot size and my drawing hand, checking is the most prudent play.

I hold Jh4h on 8s-9c-6c-5d — no pair, no heart draw (backdoor gone), only a gutshot to a 7 (4 outs ≈ 8–9% equity to hit the river). Pot is small (200) and Flash checked the flop, showing passive, call-down tendencies. Betting here would be a pure bluff with very low fold equity and is likely to be called by any pair or a club draw. Also, calling a probe is incorrect: e.g. a 100 bet into 200 requires ~25% equity to call (100 into a 400 pot after a call), well above my ~8–9% chance. I’ll exercise pot control — check and fold to significant turn aggression. If Flash checks back we get a free river to potentially hit a J/7 or attempt a river bluff on a favourable card.

On a board of 8s-9c-6c-5d, I have made a straight (5-6-7-8-9) using the 7 in my hand. While a 10-7 would make a higher straight (6-7-8-9-10), my hand is still extremely strong, especially in a limped pot where Minni has checked twice. I need to bet now to extract value from one-pair or two-pair hands and to protect against the club flush draw. Betting 150 into a 200-chip pot is a strong value bet that is likely to get called by weaker made hands.

I checked the turn with J♥4♥ intending to fold to meaningful turn aggression. Flash leads 150 into a 350 pot — calling 150 would commit me to a pot where I need ~30% equity to break even (150/(350+150)=30%). My clean outs are very limited: three Jacks and three fours (up to ~6 outs ≈13% raw), plus four 7s that usually make a board straight (often a chop). Many of those outs will still lose or chop versus a sensible calling/raising range. Flash checked the flop and now bets the turn fairly aggressively, so his range is weighted to made pairs or draws that have me crushed. Raising would be reckless with essentially no fold equity. For these pot-odds and range reasons I fold and preserve my stack for better spots.

A♠T♠ on the button in 3-handed is a clear open — great position, strong hand, and we can both steal the blinds and play postflop with initiative. Standard sizing here is ~3x the BB (300 total): big enough to apply pressure and isolate the short-stacked big blind (Elon, 5,250) or fold out weaker SB defense, but not so large that we overcommit preflop. If Elon shoves, we have decent equity and can call; if Flash defends we play postflop in position. Raise to 300 to open the pot and take control.

I am in the Small Blind with 4h 6h. Minni, the chip leader, has opened for 300 from the Button. While 4-6 suited has some speculative value, playing it out of position against an aggressive chip leader is generally a losing play. Furthermore, Elon in the Big Blind has a stack size that makes him a candidate for a 3-bet squeeze. I'll fold to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

T6o is a weak offsuit hand with poor post-flop playability out of position against a button raise from an aggressive opponent like Minni, who frequently raises to steal blinds. Pot odds are 450:200 (2.25:1, requiring ~31% equity), and while equity vs. a loose button range might be around 35% heads-up, the positional disadvantage and Minni's large stack (21100) make continuing risky. My stack is 5250 (52.5BB), not desperate for chips. Folding preserves stack for better spots in this tournament.

I am on the button with 3d 4c, which is one of the weakest possible hands in poker. In a three-handed game, while playing a wider range is generally encouraged, this hand lacks both high-card value and connectivity. Given Minni's large stack and active play style, trying to steal the blinds here is unnecessarily risky. The most disciplined and standard professional move is to fold and wait for a better spot.

Td2s is a trash hand with no connectivity, suitedness, or high card strength. In the small blind facing a big blind with a massive stack (21k), playing out of position post-flop would be disastrous even if I complete the small blind cheaply. Pot odds are about 3:1 for calling 50 more into 200, but the hand has zero equity realization potential against any defense range. Folding preserves my 5200 stack for better spots in this 3-handed dynamic where Minni has shown aggression but I need playable hands to engage OOP.

3-handed on the button with 73 suited, a speculative hand with good playability and suited potential for flush draws/straight draws postflop. SB limped (likely weak range), BB defends wide. Excellent position to realize equity. Raising standard size for initiative, fold equity (potentially take down 150 dead money immediately ~23% implied odds), or see favorable flop multiway/cheap. Raising 3x BB (300 total) balances steal/value from limped pot without overcommitting short stack. Folding too tight, calling too passive/induces multiway pots OOP.

We’re in the small blind with Tc4h facing a standard button open to 300. This hand is weak and highly dominated by the button’s likely opening range (broadways, Axs, many tens). Postflop we’d be out of position (we act first) and the big blind (Flash) often defends, so calling 250 risks a multiway, OOP flop with very poor playability and little equity. A min-3bet to 500 as a bluff is thin here — little fold equity against a short-stacked, aggressive button and it invites action from the big blind. Given stack sizes and table dynamics, the +50 already posted isn’t worth gambling further with T4o. Fold and wait for a better spot.

In the Big Blind, I am facing a button raise to 300 from Elon. My hand, 9h 5s, is quite weak and does not play well post-flop, especially out of position. Elon has been active and has a relatively short stack compared to the table leader, but there is no strategic reason to defend this specific hand against a 3x open. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here to preserve my stack.

Button with 9♠7♥ — a marginal but playable hand in 3-handed play. My stack (~215bb) is big relative to the blinds and opponents (SB ~130bb, BB ~52bb), so I want to take the initiative and use position. I open to 300 (standard 3x BB sizing) to generate fold equity and either steal the blinds or play postflop in position. If I face a sizable 3-bet I'll fold this hand; if called I'll c-bet favorable flops and exercise pot control on wet boards.

I am in the Small Blind with 9d 3d. Minni has raised to 300 from the Button. While the hand is suited, 93s is far too weak to defend out of position against a 3x open, especially with a short stack in the Big Blind who could potentially shove. Folding is the standard and most prudent play here to preserve my stack.

67s is a suited connector with good playability, standard BB defend vs BTN raise. Pot odds are favorable (calling 200 into 450 pot, ~3.25:1). Minni's BTN range is wide, giving us decent equity (~35-40% vs typical open range). Heads-up postflop OOP is manageable with this hand due to straight/flush potential. Folding too tight, 3-betting not strong enough. Call to realize equity and control pot size.

Flop: 6d Js Kh rainbow board. We hold 67s for bottom pair with a 7 kicker, suited spade (backdoor flush draw with Js on board, backdoor straight potential). Heads-up OOP vs aggressive BTN range after our standard defend. Board hits villain's wide range (KJ, Kx, Jx, Ax draws, bluffs) but also our hand has showdown value and playability. Pot 650, deep stacks. Betting risks folding out better equity realizations or getting raised off hand; thin value at best. Checking standard: induces bluffs from wide c-bet range (~50-70%), allows pot control, realizes equity on favorable turns/rivers (spade, 5/8 for str, 7 for trips). Will call small/medium c-bets (~300-500) with ~25-30% equity vs polarized range, fold to large overbets or raise aggression showing strength.

I opened preflop and have position; the flop K♥ J♠ 6♦ is fairly dry and favors my raising range. With 9♠7♥ I have no pair and only a backdoor straight, so my fold equity as the preflop aggressor is my primary weapon. A half‑pot c-bet (~50% of 650 = 325) should fold a lot of Elon’s weaker calling range (A‑high, Q‑high, tiny pairs) while keeping the pot manageable if he calls with Jx/Kx or a pair. Plan: bet 325 to take the pot now or get a cheap turn; if he calls I’ll proceed cautiously (check‑control vs small turns, look to barrel selective turns like 8/10 that give me added story or equity) and be prepared to fold to substantial aggression since I’m behind to most of his calling range.

Bottom pair with a mediocre 7 kicker on a K-J-6 rainbow board. Heads-up OOP vs BTN aggressor after standard defend preflop. Villain's c-bet of 325 into ~650 pot (~50% pot) is standard from a wide polarized range including Kx, Jx, Ax draws, gutshots, and bluffs. We have ~30-35% equity vs that range (ahead of bluffs/draws, live vs some Jx, crushed by Kx/sets). Folding is too tight given pot odds (calling 325 into 975 ~3:1 effective, needing ~25% equity which we have) and playability (backdoor spade flush draw, gutshot potential on 4/5/8 turns). Raising is a bluff with no real blockers or strong equity; induces folds from worse but gets snapped by better. Calling realizes equity, controls pot size, induces further bluffs from missed draws on later streets, and keeps us in position management while deep-stacked (5050 effective). Plan: Call, then evaluate turn based on board texture and villain sizing.

Heads-up OOP on turn with bottom pair (6s), 7 kicker, and a gutshot straight draw (needing 5 for 4-5-6-7). Board K-J-6-4 rainbow-ish but now two diamonds, increasing draw possibilities (diamond flush draws active). Pot 1300, villain's BTN range after preflop raise and flop c-bet (325 into 650, standard ~50% pot) is polarized: value (Kx, Jx, sets, two-pair) + bluffs/draws (Ax, QTs, suited connectors). We have ~30-35% equity vs her range (beats bluffs/draws, some Jx, live vs weaker Kx sometimes). After calling flop c-bet, continuing aggression risks folding out equity; thin value bet gets called by better or raised. OOP pot control essential: check induces bluffs/checks from missed draws, realizes equity cheaply to river, maintains fold equity if she bets big later. Plan: Check, call small-medium bets (~400-700) on river if priced (~25%+ equity needed), fold to overbets. Deep stacks favor playing postflop carefully with playable hand history shows Minni turns missed draws into bluffs often.

Check. I c‑bet the flop and Elon’s call narrowed his range to Kx, Jx, medium pairs and some A/Q‑high hands (plus the occasional two‑card diamond combo). The 4♦ on the turn kills my backdoor straight — with 9♠7♥ I have no pair and only a few weak pair-outs to the river — so I have very little equity versus his likely calling range. The pot is already 1,300 and Elon is relatively short, so I don’t want to bloat the pot with a thin bluff that will frequently be called by a K/J or turned into a tough fold decision facing large pressure. Checking controls the pot, keeps his bluffs in, and lets me re‑evaluate the river. Plan: if he fires a small turn bet (≈≤50% pot) I’ll call to see the river; if he jams or exerts large pressure I’ll fold; if he checks back I’ll decide on a river bluff/value based on the card (prefer bluffing only on believable runouts).

River pairs the jack, dramatically improving our hand from weak pair of sixes to strong two pair (jacks and sixes, with king kicker). Board: Jd Js 6d Kh 4d (paired jacks, diamond flush possible, straight draws unlikely). Villain's BTN range (preflop raise, flop c-bet ~50% pot, turn check-behind) is polarized post-turn check: value like Kx (superior two pair), Jx (trips), sets/two-pair combos, diamond flushes/draws; bluffs/missed draws (Ax, broadway blockers, gutshots). We crush Ax, Q-high, weaker pairs/bluffs (~40-50% of range), have ~35-45% equity overall (behind nuts, live vs some Kx/Jx). OOP river action after villain's turn check (caps very strong hands somewhat, as monsters might bet turn), betting extracts value from worse (Ax calls, some missed K-high), denies equity to draws/checks-behind, and protects vs her potential bluff-raise. Check risks free showdown or her thin value bet we call; bet applies pressure. Size 850 (~2/3 pot) balances value (gets called 1-2 streets by worse) vs fold equity from better, leaves fold button if raised (villain deep but history shows aggression without trap tendencies). Maximizes EV in deep-stack spot, consistent with history where Minni calls stations rivers (e.g., Hand 2).

Pot before the river bet = 2150. Elon bets 850; calling would cost me 850 to win a total pot of 3850 (2150+850+850), so I need ~22.1% equity to break even. I have 9♠7♥ — no pair, no meaningful showdown value. Elon called my flop c‑bet and checked the turn, a line that heavily weights Kx, Jx and medium pairs; the river J♦ actually helps that range (and gives trips/full houses to any Jack combinations) and creates some diamond/flush possibilities. A small river stab like 850 is exactly the size a player uses to get thin value or induce calls, not a size that convinces me he’s bluffing >22% of the time. Raising as a bluff is too risky (he can shove, I’m out of story if called). With virtually no equity and good pot odds required, folding is the correct, low-variance play.

In 3-handed play, J8o is a standard opening hand from the button to put pressure on the blinds. Elon in the small blind is relatively short-stacked (around 60BB), which makes him more likely to play 'push or fold', while Minni in the big blind has been active. Raising to 3x the big blind is consistent with my previous opening sizes and allows me to take control of the pot early. If I face significant resistance from either player, I can evaluate based on their sizing, but J8o is strong enough to attempt a steal here.

3d4h is a complete trash hand with no high card value, poor connectivity, and offsuit. Facing a button raise from Flash, who has been aggressive in steals from late position throughout the history (e.g., Hands 4,5,22,24,25,33,36), folding from the small blind is standard. Pot odds to call 250 into ~700 (post-call, assuming Minni folds) offer ~5:1, but my hand has terrible equity (~25-30% vs. a loose button range) and I'm out of position post-flop with a short-ish stack (60BB). No reason to defend this speculative hand when better spots exist in a long tournament (45/150 hands). Preserve stack for premium hands.

Facing a button open to 300 from the button with the small blind folded, I have 2♦4♦ in the big blind. This is a weak but suited hand with reasonable postflop playability and good implied-odds potential vs a wide open-raising range. Calling costs me 200 more (total commitment 300) into a 450 pot, giving ~30.8% break-even equity — acceptable against a wide button range and trivial relative to my deep stack (20.8k). I don’t want to 3-bet bluff with such a raggy hand; calling keeps the pot reasonable and lets me realize equity on flops. Plan: see the flop, fold to heavy action on dry boards, and pursue draws or small-ball value when appropriate.

I called preflop to see a flop, but K♥ J♦ 7♣ is a dry, high-card board and my 2♦4♦ has no pair and only a very weak backdoor diamond draw (would need running diamonds). I’m out of position and don’t want to build the pot with almost no showdown equity. I’ll check to control the pot and fold to any meaningful continuation bet; if Flash checks back I’ll re-evaluate on the turn only if I pick up a diamond or a card that improves my equity significantly.

I have middle pair (Jacks) with an 8 kicker on a board of Kh-Jd-7c. While I have showdown value, the board is somewhat coordinated with straight draws (QT, T9). Checking back here is a standard play in position to control the pot size and realize my equity. It protects me from a potential check-raise and allows me to see a turn card for free. My hand is currently ahead of most of the Big Blind's folding/drawing range but behind Kings and better Jacks, making a check the most prudent way to navigate this street.

I picked up the turn diamond (T♦) and now hold 2♦4♦ with four diamonds on the board (J♦, T♦ + my two), so I have a single-card flush draw to the river (9 outs ≈ 9/46 ≈ 19.6%). Pot is 650 and it’s checked to me — checking gives me a free card but cedes all fold equity. A small semi-bluff here is attractive: a bet of 200 into 650 gives a called pot of 1050 and my break-even hit rate if called is 200/1050 ≈ 19.0%, slightly below my ~19.6% raw chance to make the flush, so this size is roughly +EV even if always called, and it also folds out many one-pair/high-card hands (Kx, Qx, worse Jx) now. Plan: bet small as a semi-bluff to fold out weaker holdings and build the pot if I hit. If I get called, I’ll evaluate the river — value-bet a completed flush, fold to strong action if I miss (or consider a targeted bluff vs obvious weakness). I will fold to a large check-raise on the turn.

On a board of Kh-Jd-7c-Td, I have second pair (Jacks) with an 8 kicker. After checking back the flop to control the pot, Minni leads out with a small bet of 200 into a 650 pot on the turn. This represents about 30% of the pot. While the Ten is a scary card that completes several straights (Q9, 98) and improves hands like JT or KT, the price to call is extremely favorable (nearly 5:1 odds). My hand still has significant showdown value against bluffs, weaker pairs (like Tx or 7x), or straight draws that Minni might be betting. Folding here would be over-folding to small bets. I will call to see the river and re-evaluate based on the final board and Minni's action.

I missed my flush — 2♦4♦ did not improve on the river (board K♥ J♦ 7♣ T♦ T♣). I have no pair and essentially zero showdown value. Flash was the preflop raiser and check‑called my 200 turn semi‑bluff, which looks like a relatively sturdy calling range (Kx, Jx, or possibly a T that turned into trips on the river). That line is not a clear sign of weakness, so my fold equity is low and a river bluff would need a high fold rate I likely don't have. With two players and only 1,050 in the pot, the prudent, EV‑positive line is to check and concede the hand to any value bet — check to see if Flash gives up or to avoid paying when behind.

On the river, the board pairs the Ten (Kh-Jd-7c-Td-Tc). My final hand is two pair, Jacks and Tens, with a King kicker (using the Kh on the board). Minni led for a small amount on the turn and has now checked the river. By checking back, I guarantee a showdown with a hand that has significant value. Most of Minni's Jx range will result in a split pot because the board's King and pair of Tens act as the kickers. I am behind any King (Kx) or any Ten (Tx), but I beat 7x, pocket pairs like 88/99, or pure bluffs. There is little value in betting as better hands won't fold and weaker hands are unlikely to call. Checking is the most standard and safe play to realize my equity.

Preflop on the button with Qc2d offsuit, a very weak hand lacking connectivity, high card strength, or suitedness. Pot is 150 (blinds), to call 100. Even in a multiway limped pot with position, Q2o has poor equity against calling ranges from blinds and terrible postflop playability. Raising risks getting called by better hands or dominated; limping is passive and invites multiway pots where this hand struggles. Folding preserves chips for superior spots, as opponents show aggression in history but blinds can defend wide. Stack of 5975 allows patience with 104 hands left.

We're heads-up after Elon folded; Q9o is a perfectly reasonable opener in the small blind against a single big blind. With deep stacks and only one opponent, I want to take the initiative and apply pressure rather than limp/call. A standard 2x BB open (to 200 total) keeps the pot manageable, gives fold equity, and lets me play postflop with position/initiative. Plan: if Flash 3-bets large I can fold; if he calls I'll c-bet favorable textures and look for top pair, backdoor draws, or float spots. Calling 50 is too passive and folding is too weak, so I raise to 200.

I am in the Big Blind with Ac4c, which is a strong hand in 3-handed play, especially against a Small Blind raise. Since I have position on Minni and a hand with good equity and nut potential (flush), I will 3-bet to take control of the pot and potentially take it down pre-flop. A raise to 600 is a standard sizing here.

Flash 3‑bet to 600 after my 200 open. Q♠9♦ is too weak to 4‑bet for value and a small 4‑bet bluff is unnecessary here — I have position after the flop in heads‑up play and deep effective stacks. Calling 400 into a pot of 800 (~33% equity required) is reasonable against a likely wide 3‑bet range at this sizing. Plan: call to 600 total, see a flop with position, c‑bet when I connect or have good equity, and control/float otherwise — fold to extreme aggression on very unfavorable runouts.

I opened to 200 preflop, Flash 3‑bet to 600 and I flat called; pot is 1,200. Flop 5c‑3h‑4h is a dry, low board that misses a large chunk of a typical 3‑bet value range (AK/AQ/QQ+), so with position and no pair I want to leverage initiative and take the pot down frequently while keeping the size controllable. A small continuation bet (~1/3 pot) gives good fold equity against his missed broadway-heavy hands and keeps the pot manageable if I face resistance. Plan: bet 400 to apply pressure; if he folds I win the pot, if he calls I’ll float and re‑evaluate on the turn, and if he check‑raises big I will fold (I have no pair and limited equity).

I have middle pair (fours) with the nut Ace kicker, a gutshot straight draw to the wheel (any deuce), and a backdoor nut flush draw on a 5-3-4 board. Minni's lead of 400 into a 1200 pot (a 'donk bet') is relatively small, representing about 33% of the pot. While I could raise to regain the initiative, calling allows me to realize my significant equity and keep the pot manageable. If I hit a 2, an Ace, or a club on the turn, my hand becomes much stronger. Calling also keeps Minni's bluffs in her range.

Betting again on the turn as a pure bluff is thin — I have Q9o with no pair, no hearts and I didn’t improve on 6♦. The flop bet got called, which narrows Flash’s range to many hands that now have decent equity (heart draws like A♥x/K♥x, small pairs, some 5/4/3 holdings, or loose broadway hands). The 3-4-5-6 runout is more coordinated and actually increases the number of hands that will continue against a turn barrel, so my fold equity is low. With deep stacks and a 2,000 pot, I prefer pot control and to realize showdown equity rather than bluff into a range that calls with many draws or better. Plan: check to keep the pot manageable, fold to heavy aggression (check-raise / large lead), and evaluate a river decision based on his action — call small bets if he shows weakness, but save chips vs big pressure.

The turn 6d completes a four-to-a-straight board (3-4-5-6). I have a pair of fours and an open-ended straight draw (any 2 or 7 improves me to a straight), along with my Ace overcard. Minni's check after donking the flop suggests she is either pot-controlling a marginal hand or is afraid of the straight. Since the board is extremely coordinated and I have significant equity, checking back is the best play to realize my equity for free and avoid being blown off the hand by a check-raise. I can evaluate the river based on what card falls.

Final board 5♣-3♥-4♥-6♦-2♥ makes a 2-3-4-5-6 straight on board. I hold Q♠9♦ with no hearts — at showdown I’m just the board and will only beat opponents who misread or muck; I lose to any 7 (3-4-5-6-7) or any two‑heart combo that makes a flush. Flash 3‑bet preflop and called my flop c‑bet, then checked the turn — a line consistent with broadway hands, medium pairs, and missed hearts more than with lots of 7s or double‑heart combos. Checking to showdown usually just splits/loses; a small river stab pressures a wide portion of his range that missed and will fold. I’ll make a modest bluff (~1/3 pot) to fold out missed broadways/pocket pairs while keeping the sizing manageable — if he raises I’ll fold (no heart/7), if he calls we go to showdown. Bet 700 (≈1/3 pot).

The river 2h completes a 6-high straight (2-3-4-5-6) on the board. This means that both players currently hold at least a straight. Minni's bet of 700 into the 2000 pot is a relatively small sizing (35% pot). While Minni's flop donk-bet and turn check could indicate she has a 7 (giving her a 7-high straight) or a heart flush, it's also very common for players to bet when the board is a straight to try and force a fold from a chopping hand. Given the pot odds (3.8 to 1), I must call here to realize a potential chop, as folding a straight on the board is rarely correct against an active opponent in this configuration. Raising would be unwise as it turns a chopping hand into a bluff against a range that does contain some 7s and flushes.

With JJ on the button in a 3-handed game this is a clear open. I want to take the initiative, isolate one or both blinds and build a pot with a strong but vulnerable pocket pair. Table dynamics show regular 300 opens from the button, and stacks are deep (I ~204BB, Flash ~135BB, Elon ~59BB), so a 3x BB open is standard: big enough to fold out weaker hands and get value from calling ranges, but not so large that I bloat the pot unnecessarily or invite light 3-bets. I will raise to 300 total. If I face a sizable 3-bet I will re-evaluate (call or fold depending on sizing and opponent), but for now open to 300 to maximize EV and keep postflop playability.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Five offsuit (Ad 5h). Minni, the chip leader, has raised to 300 from the Button. In 3-handed play, Ace-high hands are generally strong enough to continue against a wide button opening range. While 3-betting is an option to exert pressure, Minni has shown a tendency to call 3-bets (as seen in Hand 46). Calling allows me to keep the pot manageable while playing out of position, although it does give Elon in the Big Blind a good price to enter or squeeze. I'll call the 250 to see a flop and evaluate my relative strength based on the board texture.

K7d is a suited king with good playability and blockers. In the BB, facing a button raise to 300 and SB flat, the pot is 700 and I need to call 200 more, offering excellent pot odds of about 3.5:1 (22% equity needed). Minni's button opens are likely wide given her frequent raising history, and Flash's SB call suggests a speculative hand. My hand has solid equity against these ranges (~35-40% vs typical button open + SB defend), plus strong implied odds if I hit flush/draws or top pair. Folding is too tight deepstacked; raising is unnecessary without premium. Plan to call, see flop, and play aggressively if connected.

On a board of Ks-7c-7s, I am first to act with Ace-high. This board texture favors the pre-flop aggressor's range (Minni) which includes many Kings. Since the board is paired and I have one overcard to the pair of sevens but am behind any King or pocket pair, checking is the most prudent play. I'll check to see how the button (Minni) and big blind (Elon) react to the board before committing more chips with just Ace-high.

Flopped a massive full house with 777KK (sevens full of kings), one of the best possible hands on this paired board. Only realistically beaten by pocket kings (kings full), which is unlikely given preflop action: Minni's wide button raise and Flash's speculative SB defend. Flash checked postflop, suggesting weakness, but Minni can have Kx, 7x, spade flush draws, or overpairs. Betting for thin value and to protect against draws while building the pot. 2/3 pot size (600) balances value from worse hands (trips, two-pair draws) and fold equity from junk, while keeping my stack considerations in mind (5675 effective). Avoid checking to pot control as this is too strong; raising unnecessary since no bet yet. Plan to continue value betting turn if appropriate.

I opened to 300 preflop with JJ. The flop K♠ 7♣ 7♠ is hostile — any K or any 7 (a set) beats me, but my Jacks still have decent equity vs missed broadways and many marginal hands. Elon (BB) puts out a relatively small bet (600 into a ~1500 pot), which is consistent with a wide c-bet range (top pair, small pairs, bluffs). With position and a hand that’s strong but vulnerable, I want pot control and the ability to re-evaluate on the turn. Raising here would bloat the pot and can get me in trouble if I’m behind to Ks or a set; calling keeps worse hands and bluffs in, preserves fold equity on later streets, and lets me make a more informed decision on the turn. I will call the 600.

On a board of Ks-7c-7s, Elon (Big Blind) has bet 600 into a 900 pot, and Minni (Button) has called. My Ace-high (Ad-5h) is currently very weak in this multi-way pot. The bet and call suggest that at least one of my opponents has a piece of the board (likely a King or a Seven) or a flush draw. I have no draw myself, and even if an Ace comes on the turn, it might not be the best hand if someone holds a Seven or a King with a better kicker. Given the strength shown by both opponents and my lack of equity, folding is the most disciplined and correct play here.

Kings full of sevens remains the nuts on this turn card (9s), as only pocket 77 for quads is realistically superior, which is extremely unlikely given Minni's button raise and flop call range (wide, including Kx, 7x, draws, Ax). The 9s adds straight draws (T8) and completes some spade flushes, but board spades are Ks/7s/9s, so flush draws need two more spades, and my Kd blocks KQs spades. Minni's flop call after my 600 bet suggests strength like top pair Kx, trips 77/K7/set mining, or draws. Heads-up deep with pot 2100 and my stack 5075, a 2/3 pot bet (1400) extracts value from her top pair, two-pair, weaker full houses (unlikely), and draws while denying equity to floats/draws. Checking risks giving free cards to draws or induces bluffs I can raise, but value betting maximizes EV in positionally disadvantaged spot (acting first). Consistent with flop line to build pot with monsters.

Call 1400. I have an overpair (J♣J♦) to K♠-7♣-7♠-9♠ — strong vs a lot of Elon's continuing range but vulnerable to Kx, a 7, or a completed spade flush. Elon's line (BB flat preflop, small c-bet on the flop, another small-ish turn bet) is range-heavy and includes many Kx value hands plus bluffs/semi-bluffs. Raising here risks bloating the pot and getting committed when I'm behind to Ks/flushes; folding surrenders too much equity because I still beat many of his bluffs. Pot odds are favorable: calling 1,400 into a pot that will be ~6,300 after my call requires ≈22% equity, which JJ should have vs his betting range. I’ll call to keep position, control the pot, and re-evaluate on the river (check/call small–medium bets, be prepared to fold to big river overbets if the board makes obvious stronger hands).

River 6d is a relatively blank card that doesn't complete any strong draws beyond possible spade flushes (which Kd/7d partially blocks top spade combos like KQs) or minor straight possibilities, but my sevens full of kings remains near-nuts—only beaten by runner-runner quads (77) or unlikely better full houses. Minni's lines (button open, flop call into paired board, turn call of 1400 into growing pot) suggest she can have Kx (top pair/good kicker), 7x sets/trips, two-pair combos, or stubborn draws. Pot is 4900 with my effective stack 3675 OOP; shoving all-in extracts maximum value from her calling range while denying any fold equity loss on a check (she might bluff or value bet thinner). Consistent with prior value-heavy line, this polarizes and builds EV deepstacked. No check-raise incentive as villain rarely bluffs enough here.

Fold. Elon is effectively all-in for 3,675 into a current pot of 8,575 — calling 3,675 would contest a 12,250 pot, so I need about 3,675/12,250 ≈ 30% equity to make a break-even call. His line (BB call preflop, small flop c‑bet, turn raise on the 9♠, now a large river shove) is exactly what I expect from hands that beat my JJ: a turned spade flush (he needed two spades in hand to make the flush on the 9♠), turn‑improved two‑pair (K9), or a set/fuller boat range. My Jacks only beat pure air/missed overcards and a small subset of bluffs — not enough to justify a ~30% equity requirement given how polarised this shove looks. I also can’t raise (he’s all‑in). Preserve stack and wait for a clearer spot — fold.

In a three-handed game, the button opening range is traditionally very wide. King-three offsuit, while not a premium hand, is strong enough to open for a raise from this position. By raising, I am putting pressure on the blinds to fold and asserting control over the pot. If called, I will have the advantage of being in position for the remainder of the hand. Given the stack sizes and the current dynamics, a standard 2.5x open is appropriate here.

Preflop in SB with Qs5c offsuit, a weak hand with poor playability out of position. Facing a button raise from Flash to 250 (200 to call into 400 pot), getting about 2:1 but multiway pot likely with BB Minni behind, who defends wide based on history. Equity is low against a wide button range, dominated by better Qx, and no strong speculative value. Folding preserves stack for better spots in tournament with 102 hands left.

Facing a button open to 250 in 3‑handed with K♣3♦ in the big blind. Stacks are deep (~130bb effective) and the immediate pot odds to call 150 into a 400 pot are excellent (~3.7:1, need ~21% equity). K3o is too weak for a value 3‑bet and, out of position, a light 3‑bet loses fold equity and creates tough post‑flop spots; folding is overly tight vs a wide BTN opening range. I’ll defend and call to see a flop (total contribution 250), planning to proceed only with clear equity (paired king with a usable kicker, strong draws) and to keep the pot controlled out of position otherwise.

I have top pair (K) with a very weak kicker (K♣3♦) on K♥5♥5♦. Flash opened preflop and therefore has a wide range that includes many Kx, some 5x/sets, pocket pairs and overcards — so while I have real equity, many of his continuing hands beat me (55, Kx with a better kicker, K5). Stacks are deep and I am out of position, so I want to control the size of the pot rather than build a big pot OOP with a marginal kicker. Checking keeps worse K’s and bluffs in the pot and lets Flash bluff or c‑bet thin; if he fires a small c‑bet I’ll call and reevaluate on the turn, but I will be prepared to fold to a large sizing or a check‑raise that reads like trips/two pair. If he checks back I can take a free card or make a measured turn bet on a favorable card. For these reasons I’ll check and keep the pot under control.

On a board of Kh-5h-5d, I have flopped top pair with a King in my hand. While my kicker (3h) is weak, top pair in a heads-up pot is generally a strong hand. Minni checked to me after calling from the Big Blind pre-flop. I will place a continuation bet of approximately half the pot (250 chips) to extract value from flush draws, middle pairs (66-QQ), or Ace-high hands, and to protect my hand against potential overcards or drawing hands. If I am raised, I will have to proceed with caution given my weak kicker.

Call 250. I have top pair with a very weak kicker (K♣3♦) on K♥5♥5♦. Flash opened wide from the button, and his small flop bet (250 into the pot) looks like a standard c-bet that contains many bluffs and worse Kx hands. With deep stacks and out of position I want to keep the pot controlled — raising OOP with a marginal kicker is committing and creates difficult spots against a wide continuing range. Calling realizes equity, keeps bluffs/worse Ks in the pot, and lets me reevaluate on the turn: surrender to large turn aggression, call small single-barrels depending on texture, or extract value if he checks back or gives a weak turn card.

Check. I have top pair (K♣3♦) with a very weak kicker on K♥–5♥–5♦–6♥ and I’m out of position to the button who opened wide and fired a small flop c‑bet. His continuing range contains a lot of worse Kx and bluffs, but also hands that currently beat me (Kx with better kickers, two‑pair/trips and some combos that are now a flush). The 6♥ puts three hearts on board, so any two‑heart holdings are already a made flush — a protection bet here won’t fold many of the hands that beat me and only bloats the pot OOP. With deep stacks and a marginal kicker I prefer pot control: check and let him reveal his strength. Plan: if Flash checks behind I get a free river and can make a small value shot on a safe runout; if he fires a small turn barrel (~≤1/3 pot, ~350) I’ll call and reevaluate on the river; if he barrels large (>~1/2 pot) or check‑raises, I’ll fold to preserve stack. Checking keeps bluffs and worse Kx in and avoids committing to a difficult multi‑street spot OOP.
