The turn 6h is a dangerous card as it completes the heart flush. While I still have top pair (Kings), my kicker (3h) is weak, and the board is now pairing 5s and potentially flushes. Minni's check-call on the flop suggests she has a piece of the board or a draw. By checking back, I control the pot size and ensure I see a river card without being blown off my hand by a check-raise from a 5, a flush, or a better King. This is a classic 'pot control' spot with a marginal made hand.

I have top pair (K♣3♦) but a very weak kicker on Kh–5h–5d–6h–As. Villain opened wide from the button, c‑bet a small flop sizing and then checked the turn — a line consistent with a wide range (misses, worse Ks, small pairs, some 5x/sets and occasional bluffs). The river Ace is a dangerous card: it improves Ax and A5 combos that now beat me and doesn't create many additional calling candidates for a river value bet. There are very few hands worse than mine that will call a river bet, while many stronger hands (A‑x, 5‑x, made flushes) will happily call or raise. With deep stacks and being out of position, pot control and taking a free showdown is the highest EV line here. I will check and see a showdown (and only continue if he gives a clear thin bet I can beat).

The river card is an Ace, which is an overcard to my pair of Kings. While I have a decent hand with Kings and Fives, Minni has played the hand passively, check-calling the flop and checking the turn and river. The Ace on the river is a scare card that could have easily improved her range if she was holding an Ace-high draw or simply a weak Ace. Additionally, any King she might have would likely result in a chop since the board's Ace and Six are higher than my Three kicker. Betting here is unlikely to get called by a worse hand and could potentially face a check-raise from a 5, a flush, or a newly improved pair of Aces. Checking back is the most prudent play to reach showdown and minimize risk.

78s is a strong speculative hand on the button with position on both opponents in a limped pot (SB completed, BB checked). Suited connectors play well post-flop in position, with straight and flush potential. Opponents have shown loose calling tendencies from history (Minni often raises/calls light, Flash aggressive). Raising isolates against wide limping/checking ranges, builds the pot for implied odds, and takes initiative. Standard sizing: 3x BB into 150 pot, raising to 300 total (200 more). Minni deep, Flash medium stack; this pressures BB and induces calls from worse.

I’m in the small blind with 9♠7♣ facing a button open to 300 (pot 450) — I must call 250 to continue. 9-7 offsuit is a weak one-gapper that plays poorly out of position, and calling here likely creates a multiway OOP pot against the opener and the big blind. A minimum reraise to 500 is too light and 3-betting this hand is low-EV without a specific read. With deep stacks I can afford to be selective; preserving chips and waiting for a better OOP spot or a suited connector/pair is preferable. Fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am in the Big Blind holding Queen-Deuce offsuit. The Button (Elon) has raised to 300. While we are playing 3-handed and ranges are naturally wider, Q2o is a very weak hand with poor playability post-flop, especially when out of position. Given that I have a healthy stack and it is still relatively early in the tournament, there is no need to defend with such a marginal hand against a standard 3x open.

Button with QcTs — a playable broadway hand with good playability and positional advantage. With only the two blinds to act and deep stacks, limp/calling is suboptimal (gives up initiative and fold equity). I want to open and take the lead; a standard 3x BB open (300 total) applies pressure to the SB and BB, narrows the field, and keeps post‑flop options open without committing too many chips. If I face a 3‑bet I’ll re-evaluate based on sizing and opponent tendencies, but standard open-raise is best here.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Four offsuit. Minni, on the Button, has raised to 300. This is a standard opening range for a Button player. A4o is a decent hand to defend the Small Blind, especially against a wide Button range. While I could 3-bet as a bluff using the Ace blocker, calling allows me to see a flop with a hand that has some showdown value and potential, while keeping the pot manageable against the chip leader. Elon in the Big Blind might come along, but I'm getting a good price (250 to call into a pot of 450).

Pocket Tens is a premium hand, strong enough to 3-bet preflop for value and to thin the field against a likely wide button raise from Minni and a speculative SB call from Flash. Button opens are wide in position, and SB calls often include suited connectors or broadways. Raising takes initiative, builds the pot OOP where we have postflop advantage with a strong pair, and our stack depth supports it. Sizing to 1200 (4x the open) is standard for isolation while denying equity to worse hands. Calling is passive OOP multiway; folding is too tight.

I opened to 300 on the button with QcTs; SB (Flash) called and Elon (BB) 3‑bet to 1,200. Calling costs me 900 more (1200 total) into a 1,800 pot — a small price to see a flop in position with a playable broadway hand. With the SB already in the pot a 4‑bet bluff is unattractive and folding is too nitty; I’ll call to keep pot control and use my positional advantage. Post‑flop I will continue aggressively with top pair/strong draws and fold to heavy pressure on dry/brick textures where I miss.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Four offsuit. After I initially called Minni's button raise, Elon in the Big Blind executed a significant squeeze to 1,200. Minni then called that raise. A4o is a very weak hand to take into a three-way, bloated pot while out of position against both opponents. Elon's squeeze range likely dominates my Ace-high, and if I hit an Ace, I will often be out-kicked. Calling 900 more into a 2,700 pot offers decent odds, but the lack of playability and the high probability of being dominated make folding the most disciplined and profitable play for my tournament longevity.

Pocket Tens is an overpair on a 9-5-2 rainbow flop with one spade draw. Minni's BTN calling range vs my BB 3-bet includes Ax suited/spade hands, lower pairs, suited connectors that hit draws (e.g., 76s for OESD/spade combo, 43s straight draw). This is a strong value spot OOP: bet to protect against the spade draw and any straight draws, build the pot, and deny equity to floats. Her deep stack allows value extraction from worse hands like 88-66, AJ+. Checking risks a free card to draws or induces bluffs/check-raises, but betting maintains initiative. Sizing to ~2/3 pot (1800) balances value from pairs/draws while keeping SPR manageable (~2.5:1 post-bet) for turn control. History shows Minni calls bets with medium strength/draws.

Heads‑up vs Elon after his preflop 3‑bet and SB (Flash) folded. I have QcTs on 9♦‑2♠‑5♠ — two overcards and a backdoor spade/runner‑runner possibilities, plus position. Elon’s 1,800 bet into the ~2,700 pot (~67% c‑bet) represents a mix of strong made hands and continuation bets from a 3‑bettor, so I’m behind to his value range but have decent equity vs a wide/continuation range. Calling 1,800 is cheap here: I’m getting roughly 28.6% to break even (1800 into a 4,500 pot, calling builds a 6,300 pot), and with position I can realize equity, see a turn, and control pot size. A raise to 3,600 risks getting jammed or called by better hands and is unnecessary with only overcards and a weak backdoor draw. Plan: call to see the turn; if I pair Q/T, pick up a good draw, or see a favorable runout I’ll apply pressure; if I miss and face heavy turn aggression I’ll fold.

Pocket Tens remains an overpair to the 9 but is now crushed by any Ax that Minni holds, which is a significant portion of her calling range versus our preflop 3-bet and flop bet (e.g., AQo+, Axs, especially since the flop was low and drawable). The Ac turn card hits her range hard: top pair for AJ+, two-pair combos like A9/A5/A2 rare but possible, or strong draws improving. The board has spade draw (still live) and now an Ace, reducing our equity significantly (~20-30% vs her range). Pot is 6300 with our stack at 5675, making us pot-committed if checked and facing a bet, but value betting is thin—no worse hands call a bet of meaningful size (e.g., bluffs/draws likely check/fold), and we block sets/TT+. Bluffing all-in risks her having Ax and snapping; better to check and realize equity: pot control OOP, induce bluffs from missed draws (spades, gutshots like 43/76), or showdown vs worse like 88-JJ (which downgrade post-Ace). History shows Minni barrels draws/medium strength but folds to aggression when behind (e.g., Hand 14 river fold, Hand 9 fold); checking keeps SPR low (~1:1) and exploits her aggression. Preflop/flop lines built max value already; check preserves stack vs her likely Ax.

I called the flop to see the turn and I missed — the Ace on the turn significantly improves Elon’s range (top pair, two pair, sets) and takes away much of my showdown equity. He checked the turn after an aggressive flop line, which can be pot-control with a real hand or a trap, so betting now would risk a check-raise or shove from better hands and would be an unnecessary commitment with only two overcards and a backdoor runner-runner spade chance. In position I should keep the pot controllable and see the river for free; that preserves stack, lets me realize equity, and keeps river bluffing options open if he shows weakness again. Check behind and re-evaluate on the river.

River Queen of Hearts changes little: no flush completes (spades draw missed), pairs the board for AQo or Q9s in range, but doesn't improve our TT significantly. Board now A-Q-9-5-2 rainbow, TT has showdown value against her BTN calling range vs 3bet/flop bet (missed spades like JTs/KsQs/76s for gutshots/OESD, mid pairs 88-JJ that downgraded post-Ace). Her turn check after our check suggests caution—not trapping monsters (sets unlikely without raise), possibly weak Ax (AJo/A9o slowplaying unlikely OOP vs our line), draws, or floats. Value betting thin: Ax snap calls/raises ~5.5k shove, better hands crush; bluffs unlikely vs our perceived strength (3bet/flop bet). Bluffing ourselves poor—blocks our own value, stacks committed anyway. Checking best: OOP pot control, induces bluffs from <1:1 SPR (~0.9:1), showdowns profitably vs worse (history: Minni checks weak/rivers folds weak like H14/9/44). Preflop/flop extracted max value; preserve stack vs her Ax-heavy range (~60% equity loss post-Ace). If bet, fold/call off; check realizes ~35-45% equity overall.

I check. Line and stack dynamics say this is a pot‑control, showdown decision. I picked up a rivered Q (QcTs) — top pair with a mediocre kicker — after calling Elon's preflop 3‑bet and flop c‑bet. Elon checked the Ace turn and checked the river as well; that line is consistent with either an Ace/pot‑control hand or a missed c‑bet/air. Against a 3‑bettor his range still contains many A‑combos and strong pairs that beat my Q, and a river bet will largely get called by those A hands or better (or turned into a check‑raise jam), while folding out only the hands I already beat. Pot is 6,300 and both stacks are deep enough that I don’t want to inflate the pot with a marginal hand. Checking behind preserves stack and realizes my showdown equity versus his wide/controlled checking range — the higher EV line here is to take it down at showdown rather than risk a bluff or getting called by better.

I am on the button with AQo, which is a very strong hand in a three-handed game. I will make a standard raise to 300 to build the pot and take control of the hand with position. Both Elon (SB) and Minni (BB) have shown various levels of aggression, so I want to define my hand strength early.

43o is one of the worst hands in poker, completely uncoordinated with poor playability and low equity against a button raise range. Facing a raise to 300 (2.5BB), calling 250 more from SB offers insufficient pot odds considering the multiway potential with BB behind and dismal postflop prospects. Folding preserves my 56BB stack for superior opportunities. History shows Flash is active on button, but this hand isn't worth defending.

Facing a button open to 300 while I’m in the big blind with 7♠8♠. Pot is 450 and I need to add 200 (I already have 100 in). Pot odds on the call are ~31% (200 into a 650 pot after my call). 7♠8♠ is a strong defending hand vs a wide button opening range – good equity and excellent implied odds with both stacks deep (I’m ~213BB, Flash ~126BB). I’m out of position, so 3-betting with a speculative hand isn’t ideal; I want to keep the pot manageable and realize equity on flops where I can play aggressively. Folding is too tight vs a single raise from the button. I’ll call the 200 more and see a flop, keeping options to c-bet, check/raise, or fold depending on the texture and opponent’s actions.

I called preflop with 7s8s to see a flop and I hit a marginal made hand: 6h Qd 8c gives me second pair (pair of 8s) with a weak 7 kicker. This is a heads-up pot and the button opened wide, so Flash will c-bet a large portion of his range. Out of position with a medium-strength hand, I want pot control and information rather than barreling or raising. Checking keeps worse hands and bluffs in and lets me fold to firm aggression or call reasonable continuation bets. Plan: check now; if Flash fires a small-to-medium c-bet (~<1/2 pot) I will call to see the turn and re-evaluate. If he checks back I can take a stab on the turn or lead for thin value depending on the card. I avoid leading/raising OOP with a marginal pair to prevent bloating the pot when likely dominated by Qx or better.

I have flopped top pair with the best possible kicker (TPTK) on a relativamente dry board of 6h-Qd-8c. Minni checked to me after calling my pre-flop raise. I should bet here to extract value from weaker Queens, middle pairs, or straight draws like 79 or 9T, while also protecting my hand. A bet of around 60% of the pot is appropriate in this situation.

I called preflop with 7♠8♠ and checked the flop. The board (6♥ Q♦ 8♣) gives me middle pair (8s) with a weak 7 kicker. Flash’s 400 into a 1050 pot (~38% pot) is a small probe-size that fits a wide c-bet range from the button (many Qx, broadways, weaker pairs and a lot of bluffs). Calling 400 requires ~28% equity (400 into a 1450 pot after my call) — my hand has significantly more equity vs a wide c-bet range and also has straight outs (5/9) to improve. Out of position I want to control the pot size and keep worse hands and bluffs in, so a check-call is the correct, low-variance line. Plan: call the flop to see the turn; if Flash barrels again small I’ll often call and re-evaluate on the river, but I’ll fold to large, polarized aggression on later streets if I don’t improve. I won’t check-raise OOP with a marginal second pair.

Turn: 6♥ Q♦ 8♣ 8♦. I hold 7♠8♠ — trips (three eights). Flash opened wide from the button and c-bet a small amount on the flop, so his range is heavy in Qx, broadways, single-pair hands and bluffs. With trips I want value and protection (there are a few two-card diamond combos and Qx hands that will call) and I don’t want to give a free river card. Betting also denies equity to hands that can improve and extracts from top-pair/medium-strength hands that will call. I’ll make a sizable but not pot-committing bet (~2/3 pot) to charge worse hands while keeping calls from Qx and similar holdings — 1,000 chips is the right balance. If Flash raises big I’ll strongly consider he has a full house/quads (or a polarized raise) and will re-evaluate/cautiously fold; if he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the river for further value.

Minni has led out on the turn after the board paired the eight. While this lead could represent trip eights, it could also be a semi-bluff with a straight draw (like 79 or 9T), a protection bet with a weaker Queen, or an attempt to take control of the pot on a 'scare' card. In a three-handed game, Top Pair with an Ace kicker is too strong to fold to a single lead. However, raising here is risky as it might isolate me against trips or better hands while forcing out her bluffs. Calling is the most balanced play, allowing me to keep her range wide and utilize my position to see how the river develops.

I hold 7♠8♠ and the river (6♥ Q♦ 8♣ 8♦ 9♣) gives me trips (three 8s). Against Flash’s line (button open, small c‑bet on the flop, call on my 1,000 turn bet) his continuing range is heavy with Qx, single pair/9x hands and some bluffs. Very few holdings beat me on the river — realistic full‑house combos are QQ and Q8 (quads are essentially impossible given card removal). Checking surrenders thin value; betting extracts from hands I beat (Qx, 9x, second‑pair hands and bluffs) while still charging the few draws and worse pairs. I want a size that keeps Qx and worse in the pot but doesn’t overcommit me to the small number of full‑house combos, so I’ll bet a solid, non‑committal amount: 2,000 chips (~55–65% of the pot). If Flash raises large (polarized sizing), I’ll assign him a full‑house/polarized range and fold; I’ll consider calling smaller raises for value if the sizing suggests bluffs are present.

Minni has taken an aggressive line by leading into me on both the turn and the river after I was the pre-flop and flop aggressor. This line often indicates a strong hand, specifically trip eights or a straight (JT). However, in a three-handed game, Top Pair Top Kicker (AQ) is near the top of my range and is a very strong hand to fold, especially given the pot odds (calling 2000 to win a total pot of 7450). Minni could be betting a weaker Queen for value/protection or occasionally bluffing with missed draws. While there is a risk she has me beat, I must call to remain unexploitable and because my hand has significant showdown value against her overall range.

Preflop on the button in an unraised pot (pot 150, to call 100 likely SB limp). Q4s is a strong suited hand with good position, playability, and flush/straight potential. Raising steals dead money from blinds/SB limp, builds pot OOP if called, or takes it down pre. Standard button raise sizing of 3x BB (300 total). My stack ~56BB allows aggression to maximize EV over tournament.

Facing a button open to 300 from my small blind with 5♥8♠ (offsuit). This is a weak, dominated hand and I’ll be out of position postflop against both opponents. Calling 250 into a 450 pot requires roughly 36% equity to break even, which 5-8o doesn’t have in this spot, and the big blind still acts (risking further action/3-bets). A squeeze/3-bet could be considered with a stronger or more suited holding, but with this hand it’s too speculative. Preserve my deep stack and wait for a better situation — fold.

I am holding 8d 3s in the Big Blind. Elon raised to 300 from the Button. This is a very weak hand that plays poorly out of position. Although I am getting decent pot odds to call (200 to win a total pot of 450), 8-3 offsuit is near the bottom of my range and is not worth defending against a button open, even with the discount. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I’m on the button with T6o (Th6c), pot is 150 and it’s only the blinds to act. This is a classic steal spot — my hand is marginal but playable in position and I have a big stack edge. I’ll open a standard-sized raise to 300 (≈3x BB) to apply pressure, deny correct odds to the blinds, and keep the initiative post‑flop. If either blind 3-bets big or shoves, I’ll fold this hand and preserve my deep stack; if called I’ll use position to see favorable flops and control the pot.

In a 3-handed game, while ranges are wider, King-Six offsuit is a marginal hand to defend from the Small Blind against a 3x Button raise from Minni. Calling would put me out of position for the rest of the hand against the chip leader, and I run the risk of Elon in the Big Blind squeezing or shoving over my call. Given the current stack sizes and my position, folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

In the big blind facing a button raise to 300 from Minni, who has been aggressive in previous hands with frequent raises. My hand 2s5s is a suited connector with good playability, potential for straights and flush draws. Pot odds are excellent: calling 200 more into 450 (pot will be 650), requiring minimal equity. Standard BB defense vs wide button range includes low suited connectors. Will realize equity out of position and look to stack off with strong draws or made hands postflop. Folding too tight, 3-betting not suitable for this hand.

On the flop of Kc Ts 5d, I have middle pair (fives) with a weak deuce kicker and backdoor spade flush draw (three spades total). The board is dry rainbow, but king-high is scary. As the pre-flop caller OOP against an aggressive button raiser like Minni (wide range including many overcards, broadways, Ax), checking is standard to pot control, induce bluffs from her missed draws/overcards, and realize equity multi-way if checked through (though heads-up). My stack is 5475 into 650 pot (~8.4 SPR), so I have fold equity if I check-raise later, but betting out risks folding out better or worse hands unnecessarily. No strong enough value or semi-bluff spot to lead; check to see her action and play post-flop dynamically.

I have top pair (Ten) with a weak 6 kicker on a K♣ T♠ 5♦ rainbow flop and I’m in position. Opponent (Elon) checked after calling preflop from the big blind — his range is wide (Kx, Tx, middle pairs, some floaters and small pairs). This hand is strong enough to bet for value and to take the initiative: I want to get value from worse tens, small pairs (55, 66, 77, 88, 99), and floats, while denying free cards that could improve overcards or give villain a cheap turn. There are no significant flush draws, so protection isn’t urgent but extracting value is important. I’ll make a medium-sized c-bet (~½–⅔ pot) to price calls from worse hands but not overcommit if I face resistance. If Elon raises big I’ll assume a very strong King or better ten and will fold given my weak kicker; if he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and proceed cautiously. Bet 400.

Facing a continuation bet of 400 into a 1050 pot (~38% pot) from the aggressive button raiser Minni, who frequently raises wide preflop and c-bets flops. My hand is middle pair (fives) with a poor deuce kicker, providing showdown value against her bluff-heavy range including missed Ax, broadway overcards (QJ, KJ light, AJ), and some weaker pairs or draws. No straight or flush draw, but backdoor spade potential. Pot odds excellent: 400 to win 1450 (~27.6% equity needed), and range equity likely 35-45% given history of Minni's wide play (e.g., Hands 17,20,29 where she bets light). OOP but effective SPR post-call ~3.5 (5075 / 1450), allowing check-fold turns that don't improve or check-raise strong draws/improvers. Folding here is too tight as BB defender realizing thin value/equity; raising risks bloating pot OOP with marginal hand vulnerable to better pairs/Tx/Kx. Call to pot control, induce further bluffs, and play postflop dynamically as per flop plan.

On the turn 7h, the board is Kc Ts 5d 7h, and I hold middle pair (5s) with a terrible 2s kicker and a backdoor spade draw. No new draws completed for me, but the 7 opens some straight draws (e.g., 86, 98 suited) and pairs up possible hands. As OOP with a marginal showdown holding after calling flop c-bet from wide button range (Minni's frequent light aggression seen in hands like 14,17,29,47), betting risks isolating better (Kx, Tx, sets) or folding out worse bluffs/draws/A-high. Pot is 1450 with SPR ~3.5; checking pot-controls, induces bluffs from her overcards/missed draws (AJ, QJ, K9 types), allows check-fold to big bets if polarized, or check-call smaller for value realization. Consistent with flop plan of realizing equity OOP dynamically against her bluff-heavy continuing range. Folding equity unnecessary here as defender.

I have top pair (T♥6♣) with a weak kicker. I c-bet the flop and Elon called — his calling range is wide (Kx, Tx, middle pairs, floats). The 7♥ on the turn is a blank that doesn’t change many hands in his range, so this is a good spot to extract value and deny a free river. I’ll make a turn bet around half to two‑thirds of the pot to get calls from worse tens, small pairs, and floating hands while not bloating the pot if I face resistance. Size: 900 into a 1,450 pot (~62%). If Elon check‑raises big I’ll give him credit for sets/Kx/strong lines and fold; if he simply calls I’ll re‑evaluate the river and look to get thin value on safe runouts or pot‑control against aggression.

Facing a large turn bet of 900 into 1450 (~62% pot) from aggressive button Minni after I checked. My middle pair of fives with deuce kicker (5s2s) has decent showdown value against her bluff-heavy range (A-high, broadway overcards, missed draws like QJ, AJ, K9), as seen in her history of light aggression (e.g., Hands 14,17,20,29). However, it's vulnerable to her value range (Kx, Tx, sets, two-pair). Pot odds require ~38% equity (900/(900+2350)), and while I likely have 30-40% vs her continuing range, OOP with SPR dropping to ~1.3 post-call (4175/3250), river play becomes committed and exploitable to further value/overbets. No strong draw or improvement on turn 7h (possible gutshots for villain but not me). Consistent with pre-turn plan of check-folding unimproved turns to large polarized bets to pot control and avoid bloating OOP with marginal hand. Folding preserves stack for better spots in tournament with 3 players left, maximizing EV long-term over spew equity realization.

I am on the button with 9h 4h. While this is a suited hand, 9-4 is quite weak. Looking at the table dynamics, Minni (Big Blind) is the chip leader and has been playing very aggressively, frequently defending and raising. Elon (Small Blind) is relatively short-stacked at 50BB. Opening such a weak hand from the button into an aggressive big blind is likely to lead to a difficult post-flop situation or a 3-bet that I cannot call. I will fold and wait for a better spot to commit chips.

Preflop in SB with BTN folded, facing only BB. Unopened pot, so wide opening range from SB vs BB is standard (50%+ hands). Kd4d is a suited king, strong enough to open: king blocker reduces villain's premium pairs, suited for flush/straight potential, and decent high card equity. Raising realizes fold equity while building pot OOP with initiative. Standard size 3x BB to 300 given blinds 50/100 and villain tendencies to call wide but fold sometimes. Limp too passive, fold too tight with position advantage.

Elon opened to 300 from the small blind; I’m in the big blind with 6♦8♣. I’m facing 200 more to call into ~400, which is a good price to defend with a connected, playable hand heads-up. I’m out of position so I don’t want to 3-bet light and inflate the pot OOP — a raise here would commit chips without a strong hand. With deep effective stacks this hand has reasonable implied odds to hit straights/pairs, so I’ll call the 200 more (300 total including my BB) to see a flop and then fold to heavy aggression if I miss or proceed when I pick up equity.

Preflop SB raise with Kd4d suited was standard wide opening range OOP vs BB for fold equity and initiative. Flop Js Ac 3h is an ace-high board with rainbow suits, hitting villain's wide BB calling range hard (Ax, Jx, pocket pairs, broadways). My hand is complete air: king-high with no pair, no straight/flush draw (4d Kd vs board no diamonds). No showdown value and poor equity (~25% vs villain's range). As preflop aggressor OOP, c-betting air risks getting called/raised by better hands while villain checks back worse; fold equity present but Minni calls frequently per history (e.g., Hands 2,14,17,20). Checking realizes equity, keeps pot small, and allows villain to bluff into me if checking weak. Bet sizing inefficient with deep stacks (mine 4775/600 pot ~8x); prefer check-fold to any bet.

I defended preflop with 6♦8♣ and the flop comes J♠ A♣ 3♥. I have no pair, no heart backdoor and virtually no meaningful straight prospects — just a few low pair outs. Elon was the preflop raiser and checked the flop, which could be either pot-control with an Ace or weakness; either way I’m out of position with a weak hand and very little fold equity. Betting here would just bloat the pot OOP with junk. I’ll check to control the pot and realize my equity. Plan: check behind; if Elon fires a small probe on the turn I can call to see another card cheaply, but I’ll fold to significant pressure since this hand rarely improves enough to beat strong holdings.

Turn board pairs the jack (Js Jh Ac 3h), creating two-pair and set possibilities that heavily favor Minni's BB calling range (Ax, Jx, pairs 22-JJ, broadways, suited connectors). Kd4d remains pure king-high air with no pair, draw, or meaningful showdown value—loses to any improved hand or ace. Preflop raise and flop check kept pot small (600) OOP, which is ideal for airball. Now first to act, betting offers low fold equity against Minni's sticky calling history (e.g., Hands 2,14,17,20,29,35,47,51 where she calls multi-street aggression frequently, even light). Thin value bet impossible; bluff bet risks calls from better and stacks up poorly (4775 into 600 ~8bb pot). Checking realizes equity by inducing bluffs/value bets we can fold or hero-call cheaply if small, controls pot, and avoids bloating with no equity. Standard check OOP on scary paired board with nothing.

I defended preflop with 6♦8♣ and both players checked the flop and turn (turn pairing the J). I have no pair, no meaningful draws and I’m out of position versus the preflop raiser. Betting OOP here with air just bloats the pot and is unlikely to fold better hands (A‑x, Jx, pocket pairs) — Elon’s checks look like pot control with an Ace or a slow/medium pair. With deep stacks I’d prefer to control the pot and realize my equity rather than bluff into potential strength. I’ll check behind on the turn, see a free river and re-evaluate there; fold to significant river aggression unless it’s a very small probe bet I can call for pot odds.

River board Js Ac 3h Jh Ah pairs both A and J, massively favoring villain's BB defending range (Ax becomes aces full/up, Jx becomes full house or two-pair, pairs/sets improve, heart flush draw hits). Kd4d is pure king-high air with zero showdown value vs any pair+ (loses to A,K,Q,J,T,x pairs, any flush draw that hit hearts). Preflop raise + multi-street checks OOP rep our range as missed draws/pocket pairs that didn't connect, capping us weakly. Betting any size (even small 33% pot) has abysmal fold equity vs Minni's sticky history (calls multi-street in 2,14,17,20,29,35,47,51, often light/overpairs); she'll hero-call aces-up/full house value and fold equity insufficient to justify bluff (pot odds require ~60% folds, unrealistic). Checking realizes equity: induces value bluffs from Q-high/J-high/missed draws (her check-back flop/turn suggests medium strength, not nuts), pot control perfect with 4775 effective into 600 (tiny), avoids spew OOP. Hero-fold to bet if she fires.

Board is Ac Ah Js Jh 3h — the board itself is two pair (Aces and Jacks). I hold 6♦8♣ with no pair, no meaningful draw and essentially zero showdown value beyond the board. Elon (the preflop raiser) checked flop, turn and river, a line consistent with either a controlled Ace/Jx showdown hand or complete weakness; either way a bluff out of position into one opponent with a short-to-medium stack is unlikely to fold enough better hands (any Ace or Jack, most pocket pairs) and is just bloat. With pot = 600 and no bet to call, the least-variance, highest EV line is to check and take the free showdown rather than fire a bluff that will often be called by better. I check behind and preserve my stack.

3-handed play on the button with blinds totaling 150 in the pot. K5o is a playable opening hand from this position due to position advantage and king blocker against opponents' calling ranges. Opponents have shown aggression but also folding tendencies preflop and postflop in previous hands. Standard open-raise size around 3x the big blind to build pot or take it down immediately. Raising to 300 total commits chips effectively without overcommitting our 5375 stack early. Folding is too tight 3-handed; limping invites multiway pots OOP for them.

Elon opened to 300 from the button. I’m in the small blind with Jh8c — a marginal but playable hand — and it only costs me 250 more to see a flop. With a deep stack (25.5k) and two opponents still to act, folding is too tight for the price; a small 3-bet as a bluff is unnecessary and would bloat the pot out of position and invite action from the big blind. I’ll call to defend my blind, see a flop, and proceed cautiously out of position: continue with top pair/strong draws, fold to heavy pressure on dry boards, and be prepared to fold if Elon shows significant aggression given his shorter stack.

I am in the Big Blind holding King-Five of spades. Elon raised from the button to 300, and Minni called from the small blind. I need to call 200 more to see a flop in a pot that will be 900. With suited King-high, I have sufficient pot odds (4.5 to 1) and equity to justify a call, especially since I close the action pre-flop.

Flop: 9h 7s 3h. I hold Jh8c — no pair, only jack‑high with a very weak backdoor heart draw (need both turn+river hearts; ~4% to make a flush). This is a three‑way pot and I’m out of position, so barreling here as a bluff is low EV — the board is dry enough for a c‑bet, but multiway pots punish bluffs and I don’t want to bloat the pot with essentially air. I’ll check to control the pot and see how Elon/Flash act. Plan: fold to significant pressure (roughly anything near pot or larger) since I have minimal equity; call a small probe (around ≤1/3 pot) to see the turn if priced well, and only continue if I pick up a pair or a clear draw.

I have King-high on a 9-7-3 flop with two hearts. While I have a backdoor spade flush draw and one overcard, I do not have a made hand. Minni checked, and Elon, the pre-flop aggressor, is still to act. Checking is the most standard and prudent play here to see how the action develops without committing more chips with a weak hand.

Flop is 9h7s3h, a somewhat drawy board with two hearts and possible straight draws, but relatively uncoordinated. Both opponents checked, which is a sign of weakness, especially multiway after my preflop raise. I have position on the button and range advantage as the preflop raiser, including strong hands that hit this board like sets, two-pair, or overpairs. My Kh5d is a bluff candidate with king-high, backdoor flush draw via the Kh blocker, and decent fold equity against their likely weak checking ranges (missed draws, weak pairs, or air). Betting around 2/3 pot (600 into 900) balances my range by denying equity to draws, protecting against turn improvements, and extracting value from worse that might call (e.g., 8x, 6x, weak 9x). Checking back risks giving free cards to draws or allowing them to realize equity cheaply. Pot odds and stack sizes support a bet without overcommitting (effective stacks allow for post-flop play). Opponents have shown folding tendencies to aggression postflop in similar spots historically.

I checked the dry flop (9♥7♠3♥) with J♥8♣ and Elon fired a ~2/3-pot bet (600 into 900). I have no pair, only one overcard (J) and a backdoor flush that needs both turn+river hearts (very low equity). To profitably call I need roughly 600/(1500+600) ≈ 28.6% equity; my realistic equity here (multiway) is well under that (single‑pair outs + backdoor ≪ needed). I’m out of position and Flash is still to act behind, which makes calling for bluff‑catching even worse. This is a clear fold — preserve my deep stack for better spots and avoid getting barrellied off later streets.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Five of spades on a Nine-Seven-Trey flop with two hearts. Elon, the pre-flop aggressor, has bet 600 into a 900 pot (a 2/3 pot bet) after Minni checked and then folded. While I have a King as an overcard and a backdoor spade flush draw, I do not have a made hand or a significant draw. The pot odds are 2.5 to 1, requiring roughly 28.5% equity to continue. Against a standard continuation-betting range that includes pairs and flush draws, my King-high has insufficient equity and poor playability out of position. I will fold to preserve my stack.

Button with Qd5s — marginal but playable due to position and a queen blocker. Pot is 150 and it’s folded to me (to call 100). With two opponents behind (SB Flash and BB Elon) and deep stacks, I want to take the initiative and apply fold equity rather than limp. I’ll open to a standard 3xBB (300 total): bigger than the min-raise to pick up the blinds more often and keep my range strong/consistent with the table. Plan: if both call I play postflop in position and c-bet favorable flops; if I face a sizable 3-bet I’ll fold this weak kicker queen hand; if a small isolation occurs I may continue selectively. Raise to 300 to steal/define the pot.

I am in the Small Blind with pocket threes. Minni, on the button, has raised to 300. With a pocket pair in a three-handed game, this is a standard calling situation to set-mine or see if the flop is favorable. The price is 250 more to call into a pot of 450, giving me roughly 2.8:1 odds. While pocket threes are vulnerable post-flop without improvement, folding is too tight here, and 3-betting might invite a four-bet or put me in a bloated pot out of position against the chip leader. I will call and see the flop.

Preflop in the big blind facing a button raise to 300 after SB call. My hand J2o is extremely weak: offsuit, gapped, poor high card value, and terrible playability out of position post-flop. The pot has 700 with 200 to call, laying about 3.5:1, but equity against a button opening range (which is wide but still crushes J2o) is low (~25-30% vs typical range), and implied odds are poor OOP with this hand that rarely improves to a winner. Opponents have large stacks, but folding trash preserves my 5875 stack for better spots. No reason to speculate here in a tournament spot aiming to maximize stack over 150 hands.

I have flopped a set of threes, which is a monster hand on this board (2d 3c 9c). My goal is to maximize value from Minni, who was the pre-flop raiser. Checking is the standard play here to allow her to continuation-bet with her range, including bluffs, 9x hands, or overpairs. I will likely check-raise or check-call depending on her bet size and the texture of the turn, but for now, checking is the best way to induce action.

I raised preflop so my range is strong here; the flop (2d-3c-9c) is very dry and Flash checked, showing weakness or a draw. I have Qd5s — an overcard to the board and a decent blocker to some Qx combos, but no pair or real draw. A standard continuation bet puts pressure on his wide calling range and folds lots of air; I’ll size modestly to ~40% pot to get folds while keeping the pot manageable if I’m called. If he check-raises big I’ll give this up without improvement; if he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and barrel selectively on favorable cards.

I have flopped a set of threes on a 9-3-2 board with two clubs. This is a monster hand and near the top of my range. My initial check was to induce a continuation bet from Minni, the pre-flop aggressor. Now that she has bet 300 into the 700 pot, I will check-raise to 900. This accomplishes three things: it builds the pot while I have a significant equity advantage, it protects my hand against the flush draw on the board, and it extracts value from Minni's potential 9x hands, overpairs (like TT-AA), or club draws. Given Minni's large stack and aggressive history, she is likely to call a raise with a wide variety of hands.

I c‑bet a dry 2♦-3♣-9♣ flop for 300 and got a large check‑raise to 900 (pot now 1,900; I must call 600). I have Q♦5♠ — no pair, no club draw, only 6 clean outs (3 queens + 3 fives) → ~24–25% to pair by the river. Flash’s check‑raise sizing polarizes his range toward strong made hands (sets: 22/33/99, top pair 9x, overpairs TT+ and the occasional value hand) plus some club‑draw bluffs. Versus that polarized range my realized equity is likely worse than the pot‑odds requirement, and raising as a bluff here is thin (I don’t block enough of his bluffs and would be risking a large pot out of line with my holding). I’ll preserve my stack and wait for a better, less dominated spot — fold.

I am on the button with pocket deuces. In a three-handed game, any pocket pair is a strong opening hand, especially from the button. My goal is to either win the blinds immediately or play a pot in position. I will raise to 300, which is consistent with my previous opening sizes and puts pressure on Elon and Minni.

Hand is 2s4h, a complete trash hand with no high card value, connectivity, or suitedness. Facing a button raise from Flash, who has been aggressive in recent hands (e.g., raising preflop and betting flops). I'm out of position in the small blind against two opponents. Stack is 5825 (58BB), pot 450, calling 250 gives poor implied odds for such a weak hand. No reason to defend this; folding preserves stack for better spots.

Facing a button open to 300 with 4♦6♣ in the big blind. This is a weak, off‑suit, low connector — not a hand I want to 3‑bet with. Calling 200 more into a 450 pot requires roughly 200/(450+200) ≈ 30.8% equity, and vs a wide BTN opening range this is borderline but acceptable given the cheap price and deep stacks. A min‑reraise to 500 gains little fold equity and puts me OOP postflop with a poor holding, so I’ll defend the blind and see a flop. Plan: fold to large turn/river aggression if I miss; continue only with a pair, two‑pair, strong draw, or a favorable runout.

I defended the big blind preflop with 4♦6♣ but this flop (8♥9♥K♥) is exactly the kind of miss I planned to fold to aggression on. I have no pair and no heart — only tiny two‑card backdoor straight outs — and I’m out of position versus the button who opened, a range that contains lots of hearts and Kx. Bluffing OOP with no equity is poor and bloats the pot against a deeper stack. I’ll check to control the pot and realize my (very small) equity. I intend to fold to any meaningful bet on the flop; if Flash checks back I’ll reevaluate on the turn and continue only with a pair, a strong draw, or a favorable runout.

The flop is extremely coordinated with three hearts and three cards in the mid-to-high range (8, 9, K). My pocket deuces are currently very weak, and although I hold the 2 of hearts, it is the lowest possible heart for a flush draw. Minni, having called from the big blind, can easily have a King, a better pocket pair, or a variety of hearts in her range. By checking back, I control the size of the pot and see a free turn card, avoiding a potential check-raise on a board where I have very little equity and would be forced to fold.

I defended the big blind with 4♦6♣ and planned to fold to aggression if I missed. On the turn (8♥ 9♥ K♥ J♣) I have no pair, no heart, and no realistic straight draw — effectively zero meaningful equity. Flash (BTN) checked the flop and is deep enough to call or raise if I try a bluff; betting OOP with this garbage is a poor use of chips. I’ll check to control the pot and realize my equity, folding to any bet on the turn. Only a pair/strong draw or an extremely favorable river card would change that plan.

The board is extremely connected (8h 9h Kh Jc), and my pocket deuces are currently very weak. While I have the 2 of hearts, it is the lowest possible heart and offers minimal equity. Minni has checked twice, suggesting she may not have a strong hand, but this board texture likely hits a significant portion of her Big Blind defending range (straights, flushes, or high cards). Since I have very little showdown value and betting is unlikely to fold out better hands on such a wet board, I will check behind to see a free river card and keep the pot small.

I have nothing (4♦6♣) and missed the entire board. Flash opened preflop but then checked flop and turn, which is a sign of weakness/pot control rather than a value line with a made flush or big pair. The river paired the board (9s), which helps some of his blocking hands but also makes a thin bluff more credible. Betting a small-to-medium size preserves fold equity while keeping my cost low: 300 into a 650 pot (≈31.6% required fold frequency) is a cheap attempt to steal the pot given his passive line. If he folds I pick up the 650; if he calls I lose a modest amount. Plan: make a half-pot bluff (300). If he raises, I’ll fold — no showdown value.

The board runout (8h 9h Kh Jc 9c) is extremely dangerous, with multiple straights and a flush possible. Since all cards on the board are higher than my pocket deuces, my hand is effectively just the best five cards on the board (9-9-K-J-8). Minni, having defended from the big blind, is very likely to have at least an Ace or a Queen in her hand, which would give her a better kicker than the 8 on the board, or she may have a piece of the board itself. Her 300 bet on the river is a small value bet or an attempt to win the pot away from a potential chop. Given I have no showdown value and almost any hand she holds beats me, I will fold.

3-handed on the button with 34 suited, a speculative hand with good implied odds, straight and flush potential. Position is excellent for playing postflop. Pot has 150 (likely blinds with SB fold), facing to-call 100 into it offers great odds, but limping is suboptimal. Standard play is to raise for value/isolation/steal, taking initiative. Raise to 3x BB (300 total) is standard sizing for button open or vs limp/check. Opponents deep, especially Minni, but initiative and position outweigh hand strength. Past history shows aggression rewarded.

I’m in the small blind with Tc2h facing a button open to 300. The pot is 450 and it costs me 250 to call, so I’d need roughly 250/(450+250) ≈ 35.7% equity to break even. T2o has very poor equity against a button opening range and I’ll be out of position postflop with the big blind yet to act. Raising to 500 would be a light bluff with a weak hand that risks bloating the pot and inviting action from Elon (≈55bb) or the BB. With a healthy stack and a marginal holding in a tough position, the correct play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Nine of Spades, a strong hand in a three-handed game. Elon raised from the Button, which is a wide range. I have the option to 3-bet for value and protection, but calling allows me to play a pot in position relative to the raiser (post-flop) or simply see a flop with a hand that has good equity and playability without bloating the pot against a stack that is roughly 55 big blinds deep. Given the tournament stage, I'll call the raise and see the flop.

I have top pair with an Ace and a Nine kicker on a board of Ah-4h-6c. As the Big Blind in a heads-up pot against the pre-flop raiser (Elon on the Button), the standard play is to check to the aggressor to see their reaction. My hand is strong, but not necessarily a monster, so I want to control the pot size while potentially inducing a continuation bet from Elon's wider button range or protecting against draws if the action escalates.

Flop of Ah4h6c is coordinated with two hearts and gutshot possibilities (e.g., 2-5, 5-7). I have bottom pair (4s) with a deuce kicker, giving some showdown value but highly vulnerable to Ax, 6x, better 4x, sets, or two-pair combos in Flash's BB calling range vs my button raise. Flash's check suggests weakness or trapping, but in position postflop, a continuation bet exploits fold equity against draws (hearts, straight draws like 25s, 57s) and marginal hands while getting value from worse like pure draws or underpairs. No strong draw myself, but position allows realizing equity if checked through. Pot 650, bet sizing ~60% pot (400) balances value and bluff, representing Ax or strong hands. Stack depths support (effective ~5.5k), and history shows aggression pays off. Checking risks giving free cards to draws/overcards.

I have top pair with a Nine kicker on an Ace-high board. Elon's continuation bet of 400 into a 650 pot is a standard play for a pre-flop aggressor on the button. Since I have a strong hand but not an unbeatable one, calling is the most balanced approach. It allows me to keep his bluffs in the hand and see how the turn develops while keeping the pot at a manageable size. Folding is too tight given my equity, and raising might isolate me against better Aces or sets while folding out the weaker part of his range that I currently beat.

On this turn, the 5 of hearts is a very coordinated card that completes several potential draws. It brings a possible flush (three hearts on board) and several straights (2-3 was already there, 7-8 or 3-7 are now possible). With top pair (Ace-Nine) and no heart in my hand, my hand has significantly decreased in relative strength. I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor. Checking is the most prudent move here to control the pot and see how Elon reacts to the board texture change. If he checks back, I get to see a river for free; if he bets, I will have to evaluate based on the sizing.

Turn 5h completes the straight for my 3h4d (3-4-5h-6c straight) and adds flush draw completion risks. This is a strong made hand (6-high straight), ahead of Ax, unpaired overcards, weaker pairs, and missed draws in Flash's BB defense range vs my button raise. Flash check-called flop (Ah4h6c) likely with Ax (weak kicker), 6x, heart draws, suited connectors. Check-turn suggests no flush yet (would often lead/bet) or trapping medium strength, but repeated checking favors weakness or showdown value without initiative. Pot 1450, effective stacks ~5k, bet ~2/3 pot (1000) extracts value from AQo+/AJx, 76s, 65s etc., while protecting vs river non-heart overcards/draws. Checking risks free card/check-through/check-raise, suboptimal for value. History shows aggression exploits passive lines.

I have top pair (Aces) with a Nine kicker. The turn card (5 of hearts) is highly coordinated, completing potential flushes and straights (like 2-3 or 7-8). However, Elon is the pre-flop aggressor on the button, and his range is wide, including many weaker Aces (A2-A8), semi-bluffs with a high heart (like the King of hearts), or total air. Given the pot odds and the fact that we are three-handed, folding top pair to a single bet on the turn is too conservative. I will call the 1000 to see the river and evaluate based on the final board card and Elon's subsequent action, as he has a significant portion of his stack already committed.

The river completes a potential flush with four hearts now on the board. I have top pair (Aces) but no heart in my hand, meaning any heart in Elon's range beats me. Given that I am out of position and Elon has been the aggressor on every street, checking is the most prudent play. This allows me to see how he reacts to the fourth heart; he might check back with a hand like a set or two pair that is now afraid of the flush, or he might attempt a bluff. Leading out would likely only get called by hands that have me beaten.

River Qh completes the flush draw prominently with four hearts on board (Ah,4h,5h,Qh). Holding 3h4d, I have a flush (Ah Qh 5h 4h 3h), which is strong but vulnerable to higher-kickered flushes (any opponent heart higher than 3h beats me; only 2h loses). Flash's line (check-call flop 400 into ~650 pot, check-call turn 1000 into ~2050 pot, check river) is passive, capping somewhat at top pair Ax (weak kicker, no second heart), 6x pairs, gutshots that missed, or Ax with clubs/backdoors. Less likely monster flush (would often bet turn/river for value/protection) or set (preflop defense range). Aggression has been profitable historically against passive check-call tendencies (e.g., recent hands Flash folds to continued pressure). Pot 3450, SPR low (~1.2 for me), value bet extracts from Ax, 76/65 missed straights, overpairs that called flop. Sizing 2/3 pot (~2300) balances thin value and protection vs bluffs/draws, leaves 1825 behind vs Flash's 4475 effective post-call. Checking forfeits value vs worse and risks free showdown loss to air; bet exploits position/range advantage.

I am holding Ace-Nine offsuit with the Ace of Clubs and Nine of Spades. The board is Ace-Four-Six-Five-Queen with four hearts. While I have top pair (Aces), I do not have a heart in my hand. Elon has been the aggressor on every street, and his large river bet (approximately 2/3 of the pot) on a four-flush board is extremely polarizing. In this situation, Elon's range is heavily weighted towards flushes, as any single heart in his hand beats my pair of Aces. Given that he has triple-barreled into a board that became increasingly coordinated, it is highly likely he has at least a small heart or a strong flush. Calling here without a heart is a low-probability play, and preserving my stack of 74 BBs is more important than making a risky hero call against a demonstrated aggressor.

Three-handed and I'm on the button with Ac5s — a hand I want to play aggressively in position. With only the blinds to beat I should open a wide stealing range: A5o has blocker value to Ax and reasonable postflop playability with deep stacks (~250bb). I’ll raise to 300 (about 3x the BB / a standard table open) to apply pressure, build fold equity, and take the initiative. Plan postflop: c-bet thin on dry favorable boards, use position to realize equity and control pot size, call small defenses and fold to large 3-bets or heavy action that indicates stronger ranges.

In a 3-handed game, King-Ten suited is a very strong hand. Minni, the button, has been opening frequently. Raising (3-betting) here allows me to take the initiative, potentially win the pot pre-flop, or isolate Minni if the Big Blind folds. Given my stack size of approximately 74 big blinds, a 3-bet to roughly 3.5x the initial raise is a standard aggressive play to protect my equity and define the hand strength.

QTo offsuit is a marginal hand at best in the BB facing a button open-raise to 300 and an SB 3-bet to 1050. The pot is 1450, and calling 950 OOP multiway is poor due to weak equity against likely strong 3-bet ranges from Flash (aggressive player based on history) and domination risks from Minni. Folding preserves my 7475 stack for better spots in this tournament phase with 59/150 hands played and active players at 3. No value in 4-betting this offsuit Broadway hand without position or blockers.

I opened to 300 on the button with Ac5s and Flash (SB) 3‑bet to 1050. I’ll call (750 more, 1050 total). Rationale: I have position heads‑up so calling preserves the postflop advantage and allows me to realize equity. Ac5o has an Ace blocker and reasonable playability versus a likely wide SB 3‑bet range. Calling gives decent pot odds (≈34%) and lets me see a flop cheaply; a 4‑bet bluff here is too thin (I’d be dominated by better Aces and face tough decisions or a shove). Plan: play the flop in position — c‑bet/continue when I hit or have good equity, keep the pot controlled when I miss and fold to heavy aggression.

As the pre-flop 3-better, I have the range advantage on this paired board. The flop of 9-2-9 is generally better for the aggressor's range, which contains more high pocket pairs. I will lead with a small continuation bet of approximately one-third of the pot to maintain initiative and put pressure on Minni's overcards and potential floats. I also have two overcards and backdoor straight and flush draws as potential improvement on later streets.

Flop: 9c 2c 9h — Flash (SB) c-bets small (750 into a 2950 pot). I have A♣5♠: an Ace blocker to a lot of Ax and a small backdoor club possibility, but right now I only have Ace-high. Calling 750 requires ~20.3% equity (750 / (2950+750)). Versus a wide SB 3‑bet+c‑bet range this hand likely has low‑to‑medium equity (I’m ahead of pure air and many bluffs, behind trips/9x/sets/strong pairs), so calling is comfortably within pot‑odds and preserves position. Raising here would bloat the pot with a marginal hand and run into many value hands (pockets, A9, club draws) that will call or shove — I don’t want to turn this into a bloated pot without a real made hand. Plan: call to keep the pot manageable, use position on the turn to continue only if I improve (pair the A or pick up a club) or if villain gives a free card/checks; fold or proceed cautiously to heavy turn aggression if I miss.

On this paired board of 9-2-9-3, my King-high still has some showdown value against her air, but it's unlikely to fold out a better hand if she has a pair or a nine. After she called my 3-bet pre-flop and my continuation bet on the flop, her range is narrowed to pocket pairs, occasional 9s, or strong floats. Checking allows me to control the pot size and potentially see a free river card to improve to a pair of Kings or Tens, which would likely be the best hand. If she bets, I will have to evaluate her sizing, but for now, checking is the most prudent way to realize my equity without over-committing my remaining stack.

Check. I hold A♣5♠ and did not improve on the 3♠ turn — I currently have only Ace‑high. There’s only one card to come and my only clean outs are three Aces and three fives (~13%), and because I already hold A♣ there’s no realistic club flush possibility on the river. Flash’s line (SB 3‑bet preflop, small flop c‑bet, then check turn) looks like a medium/wide range that can include made pairs (including 9x), small pocket pairs, and some turn‑checks/bluffs. Betting the turn would bloat the pot and will get called by many better hands or induce a raise I don’t want to face with air; checking controls the pot, preserves chips, and keeps me in position to call or exploit any river bluff. I’ll check and evaluate the river card.

On this board of 9-2-9-3-Q, my King-high has some showdown value but is unlikely to get a better hand to fold if I bet. Minni's check on the turn suggests she might be holding a medium-strength hand or a missed draw. While the Queen on the river is an overcard that could have hit some of her floating range (like QJ or KQ), betting now would likely only be called by hands that beat me (Pairs, Queens, or the occasional Nine). By checking, I hope to reach a cheap showdown against her missed draws or potentially induce a bluff if she was floating with lower cards. Given the pot size and the board texture, checking is the most controlled way to conclude the hand.

Final board 9c 2c 9h 3s Qh, I hold A♣5♠ and have only Ace‑high. Flash 3‑bet preflop, made a small c‑bet on the flop then checked turn and river — a line consistent with both medium/strong showdown hands and missed/bluff draws. There is essentially no thin value to extract with A‑high and any river bluff needs substantial fold equity to be +EV. For example, a 2,000 chip bet into 3,700 requires ~35% folds to break even, a full‑pot shove ~50%; given Flash’s 3‑bet/c‑bet line I expect him to fold less than that often. Preserving a healthy 23k stack and taking the showdown (where I will still beat pure air) is the lower‑variance, +EV line here. I follow my preplanned river check.

I have pocket nines on the button, which is a very strong hand in a three-handed game. Raising is the standard play here to build value and take control of the hand. A raise to 300 is consistent with previous sizing and exerts pressure on the blinds.

KQs is a premium hand that plays well multiway or heads-up. Facing a button raise from Flash, who has shown aggression in prior hands (e.g., frequent raises and post-flop betting), I want to 3-bet for value and fold equity. Button opens are wide, so KQs is ahead of much of his range. OOP as SB, but 3-betting isolates and builds the pot. Sizing to 900 (3x his raise) is standard, denies equity to worse hands, and leaves room for post-flop play. BB Minni is deep-stacked but unlikely to squeeze light here. Calling keeps pot small and plays OOP weakly; folding is too tight.

Flash opened to 300 and Elon 3‑bet to 900; pot is 1,300 and I must call 800. I hold Q♥3♦ — a weak, offsuit queen with a bad kicker that will be dominated by a large portion of both opponents’ ranges. Calling requires ~38% equity (800/(1300+800)) which Q3o rarely has multiway, and I’d be out of position facing postflop action from the 3‑better and the original raiser. A small reraise to 1,500 as a bluff is also unattractive — I have no useful blockers and risk getting called or 4‑bet. With a big stack I can wait for a better spot, so I fold and preserve chips for more favorable situations.

I have pocket nines in a three-handed game, which is a premium hand. Elon's 3-bet from the small blind is a standard aggressive move against a button open. While I could 4-bet, calling allows me to play a strong hand in position and evaluate the flop. Folding is out of the question given the hand strength and the pot odds offered. I will call the additional 600 to see the flop.

The flop of 8d5s6d completely misses my KQs; I have king-high with no pair, no strong draw (just backdoor diamond flush with Qd and overcards). This board is highly coordinated with open-ended straight draws (e.g., 4x7x, 7x9x), flush draws (diamonds), and pairs from Flash's wide button opening range that called my preflop 3-bet. OOP first to act into a pot of 1900, betting as a bluff risks too much stack (effective ~4775 with Flash) when villain can easily have better equity or continue profitably. Checking maintains pot control, realizes showdown value if checked behind, and allows folding to a bet without committing more chips. Folding equity from a bet isn't worth it here given the board texture and my lack of equity. Balance my 3-bet range by checking back some missed hands.

I have an overpair with pocket nines on a relatively low, connected board (8d-5s-6d). Elon's check after being the pre-flop aggressor is interesting; he could be trapping with a larger overpair like JJ-AA, or more likely, he missed with high cards like AK/AQ that don't want to bet into a board that favors my calling range. I also have a backdoor straight draw. I need to bet here for value and to protect my hand against overcards or diamond draws. A bet of roughly half the pot (1000) is appropriate to define where I stand and build the pot while I likely have the best hand.

Facing a half-pot bet from Flash on this highly coordinated flop (8d5s6d) that completely misses my KQo, with no pair, no strong draw (just backdoor diamond flush draw and overcards), and acting OOP after checking. Flash's button 3-bet calling range includes many strong hands that connect well here: pocket pairs 77+, suited connectors like 45s/67s/78s (straights/flush draws), diamond flush draws (AdXd), and broadway with equity. My king-high has poor showdown value and limited equity (~20-30% vs his range). Calling risks further investment into a pot where I'll be OOP and likely facing more aggression on turn/river, with effective stacks allowing him to continue profitably. Raising as a bluff is -EV given the board texture favors his value range and he has position/shorter stack (3775). Folding preserves my stack (6575) for better spots in this deep-stack tournament phase (hand 60/150), balancing my checking range which includes missed value hands. Consistent with preflop plan to control pot and realize equity selectively.

3-handed on the button with T7s, SB (Minni) has folded leaving 50 dead money in the pot with BB (Flash) 100 posted, pot 150, to call 100. This is an ideal spot to raise and attempt to steal the dead money + BB, as button has position heads-up and T7s has good playability with suited connectors (straight/flush potential). Flash same effective stack as me at 6575, but wide BB defense expected. Standard steal size around 3x BB, to 300 total, applies pressure while keeping SPR reasonable postflop.

6♦7♠ offsuit (6h7s) is a marginal hand to defend from the small blind facing a 3bb open from the button. I’m out of position postflop and would be up against a wide but still largely dominating raising range (Ax, Kx, pairs), and the big blind may come along to create a messy multiway pot. Calling 250 to play OOP with a weak, unsuited connector is negative EV here; a tiny squeeze to 500 is a poor 3-bet size with a weak hand and will get me into marginal spots. Preserve the large stack for better spots — fold.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Queen offsuit (KQo), which is a strong starting hand in a three-handed game. Elon raised to 300 from the button, and Minni folded from the small blind. I need to call 200 more to see the flop. KQo is well ahead of a button-raising range in many cases. I have several options: calling or three-betting (raising). Given that I have a solid stack and want to control the pot size or take initiative, a three-bet is a strong option. However, simply calling is also very reasonable to see a flop and realize my hand's equity without bloating the pot unnecessarily against Elon, who has shown aggression in previous hands. Hand history shows Elon can be aggressive but also folds to pressure (Hand #60). Given the pot odds (calling 200 to win a total pot of 450), a call is the minimum play. A raise would be better to take control, especially since I'm out of position for the rest of the hand. I will choose to raise to 900 to test Elon's button-raising range and potentially take the pot down now or set up a post-flop continuation bet.

Facing a 3-bet from BB to 900 after SB folded, pot 1250, to call 600 more (total 900 committed). T7d suited connectors play well in position heads-up with ~3:1 effective SPR postflop (my 6275, Flash 5775). Pot odds ~2:1 direct (33% needed), and T7d has 35-45% equity vs polarized BB 3-bet range (JJ+, AK value or Ax suited/KQs/bluffs). Great playability for straights/flushes/draws/implied odds. Folding too tight 3-handed after steal raise; 4-betting risks stack vs value calls while SPR too deep for jam. Calling realizes equity flexibly OOP for opponent.

I have Top Pair (Kings) with a Queen kicker on a board of Ks-Jh-Jc. Pre-flop, I 3-bet from the Big Blind and Elon called from the button. This board texture is generally good for my 3-bet range (which includes AA, KK, AK, and KQ), but the paired Jacks are a concern as Elon could have AJ, KJ, or QJ in his calling range. I will lead with a continuation bet of 750 (roughly 40% of the pot) to extract value from weaker Kings, pocket pairs like TT or 99, or Broadway hands that might call one street. This bet also helps define where I stand in the hand.

Preflop, T7dd is a playable button steal vs limpers/blinds 3-handed, and calling the BB 3-bet maintains position with suited connector playability and reasonable pot odds (~33% equity vs polarized range). Flop KsJhJc is a disaster: paired board favors Flash's strong 3-bet range (JJ+, KJ, AJs, sets possible), and I have zero equity (no pair, no flush draw active, no straight draw, only weak backdoors). Facing a donk bet of 750 into 2600 (pot odds ~22% needed), but vs value-heavy range my equity is <15%. Raising as bluff is suicidal with SPR ~1.2 post-call and reverse position dynamics; calling bloats pot OOP-equivalent with air. Instant fold preserves stack for better spots; villain can rep JJ/KJ effectively here.

Button with T5s suited is marginal but has good postflop playability and benefits hugely from position. The table’s standard open size is ~3xBB, so I’ll raise to 300 to seize initiative, generate fold equity against the blinds, and play in position if called. With deep stacks I can realize implied odds on spade/straight runs but I’ll avoid bloating the pot with a weak made hand — fold to a large, polarized 3-bet, consider calling small squeezes depending on sizing and opponent tendencies. Raising to 300 maximizes my long-term EV here.

I am holding Jack-Trey offsuit in the Small Blind. This is a very weak hand to defend against a standard open-raise from the Button, particularly when the raiser (Minni) is the chip leader and has been active. Since I would be out of position for the rest of the hand with a marginal-at-best holding, folding is the most disciplined and correct play to preserve my stack.

3s4h is a very weak offsuit hand with poor post-flop playability, low equity against a typical button opening range from Minni, who has been aggressive in raises throughout the history. Pot odds are 2.25:1 (calling 200 into 450), but equity is insufficient (~25-30% at best), and out of position heads-up. My stack is 55BB, no need to defend wide here. Folding preserves chips for better spots in a long tournament (hand 62/150).

I am on the button with 7s 2d, which is essentially the worst starting hand in poker. The pot is currently 150 and I am required to call 100 to enter. Given the low quality of my hand and the fact that I am in the tournament, it is not worth wasting any chips on such a weak holding. I will fold.

Preflop in SB with 3d5d, weak suited connectors. OOP against BB, who can defend wide. Flash folded, so heads-up if limp, but limping SB invites raises and position disadvantage postflop. Folding is standard and preserves stack for better spots. No fold equity from raise with this hand.

Preflop on the button with J6 suited hearts, a speculative hand with good playability for straights and flush draws. Both opponents have checked (SB limp, BB check), pot is 150, and I need to call 100 to see flop. Raising from the button steals blinds frequently against passive checks and puts me in position post-flop with initiative. Standard raise size of 3x BB (300 total) balances steal and value. My stack is 55BB effective vs Flash, deep enough for implied odds if called. Opponent tendencies show Minni aggressive but deep, Flash variable; raising builds pot or takes it down.

I'm in the small blind with A♦5♦ facing a standard button open to 300. I'll call (250 more, total 300). A5s is a defendable hand from the SB — it has top-pair potential, a nut-flush/backdoor straight chance and it blocks many A-high combinations. Raising here would bloat the pot and leave me out of position against the button and the big blind; with both opponents likely to defend or jam (Elon is relatively short-stacked), I prefer to keep the pot controlled and realize equity cheaply. Postflop plan: proceed aggressively on A-high or diamond-heavy flops (c-bet or continue with draws), but play cautiously and fold to heavy action on dry, unfavorable boards. Calling 300 keeps options open and is the +EV, lower-variance line from this seat.

I am in the Big Blind with Qh 7c. Elon raised from the button, and Minni (the chip leader) called from the Small Blind. While I am getting good pot odds (200 to call into a 700 pot), Q7o is a weak hand that plays very poorly out of position in a multi-way pot. Minni's call suggests she has a range that likely dominates my 7 or has my Queen out-kicked. Given my stack size and the fact that I'll be first to act on every street, I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I have A♦5♦ on A♠7♠8♦ — top pair with a weak kicker plus a backdoor diamond. Elon opened from the button with a wide range and is relatively short (≈5.2k), so this is a good spot to c‑bet for value and protection. Many of his calling hands (worse A‑x, Kx, small pocket pairs and some broadways) will pay a ~half‑pot price, while a bet also charges two‑spade and other backdoor draws. I want a size that extracts value but keeps the pot controllable if he jams, so I’ll bet ~400 (~55% pot). If he raises or shoves I’ll re‑evaluate his range (polarized vs bluffs/draws) and respond accordingly, but on this flop I prefer to get value and deny free cards.

Preflop button raise with J6hh was standard speculative play for position and playability. Flop 7s As 8d completely misses: no pair, no straight draw (J6 doesn't connect well to 7-8-A), no flush draw as no hearts on board. Pure air with backdoor potential at best. Minni (SB) called OOP preflop raise, then leads ~57% pot on A-high spade-heavy board, representing Ax, sets, or strong draws in her decent calling range. My range has some Ax/A-broadways but J6hh is bluff-heavy here. Pot odds 1100 to call 400 (~3:1, 25% equity needed), but realized equity low vs her value-led range (dominated, few live cards). Raising semi-bluff risky: board favors her range, fold equity low vs deep aggressive Minni (26k stack), minraise to 800 commits ~15% stack poorly with poor equity post-raise. Calling floats OOP bettor likely to continue; multiway potential weak. Deep stacks (52BB), better spots ahead. History shows Minni bets aggressively but value-oriented postflop. Standard fold preserves stack for +EV spots in 150-hand tournament.

On the button with 6♠2♣ — a weak hand, but I have position and a large stack. The pot is only 150 and a standard open from the button at this table is ~3xBB. I want to apply pressure on the blinds and pick up the pot preflop or play a small, positional pot. I will raise to a standard 3xBB (300 total). If I face a reraise I’ll fold this junk hand; if called I’ll use position to c-bet or proceed cautiously on the flop.

K6o is a marginal hand to defend from the Small Blind against a button raise, especially when playing out of position against the chip leader (Minni). While button opening ranges are generally wide in 3-handed play, K6o lacks sufficient post-flop playability to justify calling. 3-betting as a bluff is also risky given Minni's active playstyle and large stack. Folding is the most prudent play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

84 suited clubs is a speculative hand with straight and flush potential. Facing a button raise from Minni, who has been aggressive in prior hands (frequent raises and continuation bets), after SB folds. Pot is 450, calling 200 gives excellent implied odds due to Minni's deep stack (27k) and my ability to realize equity if I hit flops like flush draws or wraps. Direct pot odds are about 2.25:1, favorable for a suited connector. Folding is too tight in BB defense range vs button; raising OOP with this hand is overplay. Prefer calling to see flop and play postflop dynamics.

Flop missed entirely with 84cc on 3h-3c-2d board. No pair, no made flush draw (only three clubs total: 8c,4c,3c), no straight draw (paired treys prevent forming five consecutive ranks even on 5x turn, as 2-3-3-4-5 still only four distinct ranks). Pure air with weak backdoor flush/straight possibilities. Heads-up OOP vs Minni's button raise (wide range including Ax, broadway, pairs, suited connectors). She c-bets frequently per history (e.g., Hands 14,17,20,29,35,44,47,51+). Betting out risks calling station call or raise, poor SPR postflop with 4925 stack (~33bb effective into 650 pot). Check-fold optimal: pot control, exploit her aggression by folding to likely c-bet, preserve stack for better spots in 85 hands left. No value, low EV semi-bluff OOP on paired low board.

I raised preflop from the button and got called by the BB. Flop 3♥-3♣-2♦ gives me two pair (3s and 2s) with 6♠2♣ — a strong made hand but vulnerable to trips/quads (any 3) and to hands with a 2 and a stronger kicker. The board is very dry with almost no real draws, and Elon's calling range from the big blind will include lots of worse hands (overcards, A/Kx, weaker pairs) that will pay to see a bet. I want to extract value and deny free cards without bloating the pot against a likely stronger pocket pair. Bet for value around half the pot is best: it gets called by worse and keeps the pot manageable if he shows strength. If he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn; if he check-raises large I’ll assume trips/pocket pair and fold or proceed very cautiously.

Facing a 400 bet into 1050 pot on a paired 3h-3c-2d flop with 84cc, which completely misses: no pair, only backdoor flush draw (three clubs total) and negligible straight potential (gaps prevent realistic draws). OOP in BB vs aggressive button Minni who frequently c-bets wide ranges (evident in prior hands like 14,17,20,29,35,44,47,51,53,56). Pot odds require ~27% equity (400/1450), but hand has <10% equity vs her range (dominated by Ax, pairs, broadways overpairing board or having sets). Calling bloats pot OOP with poor postflop playability and low SPR (~3:1 effective), turning 49bb stack into awkward short stack spots. Raising as semi-bluff too thin on dry paired board vs calling station tendencies. Check-fold exploits her aggression, preserves stack for 85 hands remaining in tournament.

I am on the button with K5o in a 3-handed game. While K5o is a relatively weak hand, it is standard to open a wide range from the button to pick up the blinds or play a pot in position, especially against a short stack in the small blind (Elon) and a large stack in the big blind (Minni). I will make a standard 3x open to 300 chips.

Q3s is a marginal suited hand from the small blind facing a button raise. Button ranges are wide, but I'm OOP postflop with poor equity against typical calling or 3-betting ranges, especially with Minni's massive stack behind who can apply pressure. Pot odds are not sufficient (calling 250 into 450 for ~2.8:1), and playability is weak with a weak queen and low kicker. Folding preserves my stack of 4875 for better spots in this deep tournament phase.

Call. Q♥9♦ is a reasonable hand heads-up against a button open. The pot is 450 and I need to call 200 more (total committed 300) which gives me ~30.8% break-even equity (200/(450+200)). Versus a wide BTN opening range my queen-high has enough equity and playability (backdoor straight/flush possibilities) to continue. A tiny 3-bet to 500 is an option, but it’s a weak sizing that creates marginal OOP spots; I prefer to see a flop and play postflop where I can control the pot and leverage my stack depth.

I have Q♥9♦ on T♠-6♦-6♠ with no pair and no real draw. The button opened pre and I called; this paired, low flop hits a lot of the raiser’s value range (T/x, pocket 6s, overpairs) and also contains many hands he can barrel with as a c-bet or check back. I’m out of position, so I should control the pot and avoid committing chips with a weak hand. Checking keeps weaker hands and bluffs in his range, lets me fold to large aggression, and allows a free turn card if he checks back. Plan: check; call small probes (roughly ≤1/3 pot) to realize equity, fold to sizable bets that represent a made hand.

On a paired board of Ts-6d-6s, I have a range advantage as the pre-flop raiser. Although Kc-5d is only King-high with no direct draw, this is a board where a continuation bet is highly effective at folding out hands that missed the flop, such as smaller pairs or non-spade overcards. Minni's check indicates she likely doesn't have a strong piece of this board. I will place a small continuation bet of approximately one-third of the pot to test her interest and potentially take the pot down immediately. If she raises, I can comfortably fold knowing my hand has little equity to continue.

I checked the flop with Q♥9♦ and the button made a very small probe (225 into a 875 pot). Calling 225 gives me break-even equity of ~225/(875+225) ≈ 20.5%. My hand has reasonable showdown equity versus a wide BTN c‑bet range (I can pick up a pair on turn/river and I beat a lot of pure bluffs and missed overcards), so calling to realize equity is correct.
