On this flop of 9d-Jd-Th, I have flopped bottom two pair (Tens and Nines). While this is a strong hand, the board is extremely 'wet' and coordinated, with numerous straight and flush draws available. Minni checked to me, and since I am in position, I should bet for value and protection. I want to charge potential diamond draws or straight draws (any Queen, King, or Eight) and build the pot while my hand is likely ahead. A bet of 300 (75% pot) is a standard size on such a connected texture to gain information and protect equity.

Board J♦ 9♦ T♥ with 4♣6♦ — I have almost no showdown equity (only runner‑runner diamond/paired‑card outs). Pot is 700 and Flash bets 300, so calling requires ~30% equity (300/(700+300)). My raw chance to pair by the river is ~24% and runner‑runner flush ~4% (combined still a bit under break‑even), so a pure call to showdown is marginal. However I’m in position as the preflop raiser and Flash’s bet is a relatively small probe that will be made with a wide, often weak range. Calling keeps the pot small, allows me to float and attempt to take the pot away on the turn if he checks or shows weakness, and preserves the option to fold to real turn aggression. A raise as a bluff is unattractive — I don’t block strong diamond/value combos and would be giving up a lot of fold equity versus hands that continue. Plan: call the 300, try to take it away on the turn if he slows down, otherwise fold to significant turn pressure unless I improve.

Turn paired my 4♣ (4s) and Flash checked after a small flop stab — exactly the scenario I planned to punish. I started with air and called the flop to keep pots small; now I’ve improved to a very weak pair and have position. Pot is 1,000; a bet of ~600 (≈60% pot) applies pressure to missed hands/overcards, gets value from worse Ax/Kx/Qx and some T/9 holdings, and charges diamond/straight draws that might peel. If Flash responds with a sizeable check‑raise I’ll concede (pair of fours is too weak to brawl there); if he calls I’ll re‑evaluate the river and proceed cautiously on dangerous runouts. Betting here leverages his turn weakness and my position to either take the pot now or extract thin value.

On this turn (4s), Minni has led out with a 'donk bet' of 600 into the 1000 pot after check-calling the flop. This line is often polar; she either has a very strong hand like a straight (KQ, Q8) or better two pair (JT, J9), or she is leading with a draw or a top pair (like AJ/KJ) to 'set her own price' and prevent me from betting larger. My bottom two pair (Tens and Nines) remains a very strong hand, but it is extremely vulnerable to many river cards (any King, Queen, Eight, or Diamond). Raising here might isolate me against hands that have me beaten, whereas calling allows me to keep her potential bluffs or weaker value hands in the pot. I will call to maintain position and evaluate the river based on whether the board remains safe.

Check. The river (T♦) pairs the board and drastically improves many hands in Flash’s calling range: any Ten in hand now has trips, Jx/9x remain ahead of my pair, KQ already had a straight on the flop, and two-card diamond combos now make flushes. Flash’s line (calling pre, betting the flop, calling my 600 turn) is consistent with real showdown hands or strong draws that won’t fold to a river bluff very often. My 4♣6♦ is only a single pair of fours and has very poor showdown equity versus that range, so bluffing the river is too thin — I’d get called by almost everything that beats me and fold out only air. Checking keeps the pot small and lets me fold to aggression; if he checks back we go to showdown, if he bets I’ll fold.

On the river, the Ten of Diamonds pairs the board and completes the diamond flush draw, while improving my hand to a full house (Tens full of Nines). Minni donk-bet the turn but has now checked the river. This check could signal a desire to bluff-catch with a straight, a flush, or trips (Tx) that is now wary of the paired board. While Jack-Ten (a better full house) is a possibility, it represents a small portion of her range. I must bet for value against her likely calling range of trips or flushes. A bet of 1400 into the 2200 pot is a strong value sizing that is likely to be called by any Ten or a flush.

The river (Td) pairs the board and also completes many diamond/straight lines — the board is 9d Jd Th 4s Td. Flash’s line (call preflop, bet the flop, call my 600 on the turn, now bet the river) is heavily weighted toward value: trip Tens, Jx/9x/Tx, two-diamond combos that made a flush, and the few straight combos that were possible on the flop. Pot is 3600 and the call is 1400, so I’d need ~28% equity to break even (1400/(3600+1400)). My hand (4♣6♦) is only a weak pair of fours and has very little showdown equity against that continuing range — I mostly beat pure air or rare missed bluffs, which are unlikely given his line. A raise would be reckless (I don’t block relevant value/flush combos and would be crushed), and a call is -EV. I’ll fold and preserve my stack for a better spot.

On the button with 7♣5♦ (7h5d) and both blinds to act, this is a standard open-raise spot. 7-5 offsuit is marginal but well inside a wide 3-handed opening range — it has straight connectivity and backdoor diamond potential and often wins the blinds outright. I'll raise to 300 (3x the BB) to apply pressure, isolate one defender, and avoid playing multiway OOP. Plan: fold to a large 3-bet; if called, proceed cautiously — c-bet on dry, non-connected boards for fold equity, and play for pot control or pursue draws on coordinated boards.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace of Spades and Six of Clubs. Minni, on the button, has raised to 300. A6o is a marginal hand to play out of position, but against a button open, it is generally too strong to fold. I have 12,250 chips, and the cost to call is only 250 more. While 3-betting is an option to exert pressure and take the pot down pre-flop, calling allows me to see a flop with a hand that has decent high-card value. I will call the raise and evaluate the flop, being mindful that I will be out of position for the rest of the hand.

Preflop in the big blind with 9d Tc offsuit, facing a button raise to 300 by Minni and a small blind call by Flash. The pot is 700, and it costs 200 more to call (getting about 3.5:1 odds). However, 9To is a marginal speculative hand at best, offsuit, with poor playability out of position multiway against two opponents. Minni's button range is wide but strong enough often, and Flash's call suggests some strength. Raising would be a bluff with a bad hand OOP. Best to fold and wait for better spots to maximize stack long-term in the tournament.

On this King-Jack-Eight flop, I have Ace-high with a backdoor nut flush draw. This board texture heavily favors the pre-flop aggressor (Minni), who likely has many King-x and Jack-x hands in her range. As the player out of position who called pre-flop, my standard play is to check to the raiser. I will check and see how Minni acts; if she bets, I will likely have to fold given the lack of direct connectivity to the board, but for now, checking is the most sound tactical move.

I opened the button with 7♥5♦ and the flop is K♠ 8♥ J♠. I have no pair and almost no clean equity (only unlikely two-card straight outs), so this is a pure continuation-bet spot to seize fold equity and leverage my preflop range. Flash checked, so I'll make a small, standard c-bet to fold out worse Ax, Qx, small pairs and isolated Jx/Kx weak hands while keeping the pot controllable if called. Betting ~300 into the 700 pot (~40%) represents strength without bloating the pot; plan is to fold to a sizable raise (I have negligible showdown value), and if called I’ll re-evaluate the turn and continue with pot control or barrels only if I pick up real equity or a favorable read.

On this highly coordinated board of King-Jack-Eight, my Ace-high has very little equity against Minni's range. As the pre-flop aggressor on the button, her range contains many Kings, Jacks, and strong draws (like Queen-Ten or Ten-Nine) that have me soundly beaten. While I do hold the Ace of Spades for a backdoor nut flush draw, the board is too wet to justify a call with just one overcard that might not even be clean. Calling 300 into a 1000 pot requires roughly 23% equity, which I likely do not have here given the board texture. Preserving my stack for better spots is the optimal play.

I am on the button with K3o. While a King-high hand can sometimes be used to steal the blinds in a three-handed game, K3o is at the very bottom of the opening range and plays poorly post-flop. Given that Minni (the Big Blind) has been quite active and aggressive in defending her blinds, and Elon (the Small Blind) is also reasonably active, I will choose to fold this hand and wait for a more favorable spot.

Preflop from small blind with 68o offsuit, a weak marginal hand. Flash has folded, leaving heads-up vs Minni in BB. 68o plays poorly postflop, low equity vs calling range, and vulnerable to 3bets or postflop aggression from Minni who has shown frequent raising tendencies. Deep stacks, better to fold trash and wait for better spots to maximize stack long-term.

K4o is a marginal offsuit king-high hand with a poor kicker. On the button in a 3-handed pot preflop, with a pot of 150 and to call 100 (implying some action from blinds, likely a limp and check or small raise), the hand lacks playability postflop multiway or heads-up against likely calling ranges from deep-stacked blinds. Folding preserves our ~97BB stack for better opportunities in this tournament phase, avoiding speculative calls that risk our stack without strong equity or fold equity. Aggressive opening would be standard if unopened, but facing to-call suggests prior commitment, making raise risky without position advantage postflop. Prioritizing stack maximization over thin equity.

2♠5♥ is one of the weakest hands — very poor equity and terrible playability. Calling 50 into 150 is cheap and would give me position, but this hand rarely improves and is often dominated; raising to 200 would be a thin, high-variance bluff against a BB who has shown willingness to defend. I preserve my 18,225 stack and wait for a much better spot rather than investing more with junk. Fold.

I'm on the button with 7♠6♦ — a playable connected hand in position. With only two opponents and deep stacks, I want to take the initiative rather than limp and allow a multiway pot out of position. A standard 3xBB open (to 300) applies pressure to the small blind, isolates the big blind, and gives me fold equity and postflop leverage in position. Min-raising to 200 is too small and calling 100 is too passive versus these opponents who have shown frequent aggression. If I face a sizable 3-bet I'll re-evaluate (often folding to a large 3-bet, or continuing/4-betting selectively against light 3-bets). Raise to 300 to take control of the pot.

King-Six offsuit (K6o) is a marginal hand to play from the Small Blind against a Button raise, especially against an active player like Minni. Playing out of position with a weak kicker often leads to difficult post-flop scenarios. Given my healthy stack and the current 3-handed dynamic, I prefer to fold this hand and wait for a stronger range to 3-bet or a hand with better playability to call.

K9h is a strong suited king defender from the BB against a button raise, likely a wide range from Minni. Excellent pot odds (200 to call into 450 pot, over 4:1 effective). High equity, suited connectivity for flush/straight potential, and live king. Prefer calling OOP over folding or 3-betting given playability and position. History shows Minni raises frequently, supporting wide defense.

Flop Qh5d5h gives me the nut flush draw with Kh9h (two overs for flush), king-high over queen, and backdoor straight potential. Paired board caps villain's range somewhat, but button raise is wide, including Qx, Ax suited, draws, pocket pairs below 5s unlikely. OOP heads-up vs Minni's aggressive history, but deep stacks and huge equity (~40-50%+ vs range). Pot 650, betting ~2/3 pot (450) builds pot, charges villain's draws/one-pair/weak Qx, denies equity, and takes initiative. Checking risks free cards or tough spot if villain bets big; donk bet exploits wide range and my playability post-flop. Favorable vs Minni's frequent calling/betting tendencies from history.

I opened to 300 from the button with 7♠6♦ and Elon (BB) just defended. Flop Q♥5♦5♥ and he bets a small 450 into a 1100 pot — a sizing that indicates a wide c-bet range (Qx, A-high, various overcards, underpairs, occasional hearts or slow-played 55). I have no pair but I’m in position and get ~29% pot odds to call (450 to win ~1550), which is reasonable to float with my wide, connected button raising range. Raising to 900 here would be a pure bluff and risks bloating the pot out of position against hands that will call or shove for value; calling keeps the pot manageable, lets me realize position advantage, and gives me the option to fire a turn bluff or fold to strong action if I don’t improve. Plan: call the 450, evaluate the turn — fold to heavy turn pressure unless I pick up equity (pair, 7/6, or a heart), or seize the pot if Elon checks.

Turn 4h completes the nut King-high flush with Kh9h (board provides Qh,5h,4h). Previously flopped nut FD + K-high + backdoors, donk bet flop for value/protection and got called by Minni's wide button range (Qx, 5x, Ahxh, gutshots, bluffs). Now nuts on flush-completed, paired board. Minni's history shows frequent calling of bets (e.g., Hands 2,14,17,29,35,47,51,71,83,100+), even multi-street, suggesting station tendencies. OOP, betting extracts value from worse (inferior flushes like Qx hearts, sets, two-pair like Q5/54); checking risks free river or missed value if villain has showdown value. Bet ~2/3 pot (1100 into 1550) balances value vs draws/one-pair/sets, builds pot deep-stacked (my 8975, villain 17475), denies equity to rivers. Optimal sizing exploits Minni's calling patterns without overbetting nuts early.

I missed on the turn. I have 7♠6♦ on Q♥‑5♦‑5♥‑4♥ and Elon fires 1,100 into a 2,650 pot. Calling 1,100 risks chips for ≈29% required equity (1,100 / (2,650+1,100) ≈ 29%). My crude raw outs (pair a 7/6 or hit a 3/8 for a straight) are limited and borderline vs the pot odds, and many of those outs are less effective against the hands Elon’s line represents (Qx, A/K overcards, underpairs, and now heart‑heavy hands that the turn helps). I also have no hearts, so the turn made the board very dangerous and reduced the frequency my bluffs would work. Raising would be a pure bluff vs a range that contains real made hands and is too high-variance here. I followed my flop plan to float; on the turn I didn’t pick up equity or a nut draw, so I fold and preserve my deep stack for better spots in position.

I am on the button with Jack-Eight offsuit in a three-handed game. This hand is strong enough to open for a raise to put pressure on the blinds and take control of the pot. A standard 3x open is appropriate here.

KJ offsuit is a strong hand with good equity against a button opening range, which is likely wide given Flash's aggressive history of frequent raises from late position. Facing a standard 3x open to 300 into 450 pot, calling gives good implied odds (~3:1 direct, plus position disadvantage but deep stacks). However, as a pro, 3-betting isolates the BTN, builds a pot with a premium hand, and takes initiative OOP. Minni in BB may fold wide. Raising to ~3x the open (900 total) is balanced sizing, min raise is 500 but sizing up for value/fold equity. Avoids multiway pot OOP.

Facing a button open to 300 and a small blind 3-bet to 900, the pot is 1,300 and I must call 800. I’m on the big blind with A♦9♣ — a marginal, offsuit ace out of position. Calling 800 would require ~38% equity to break even (800 / (1,300+800) ≈ 38%), which A9o generally does not have vs a standard SB 3‑bet range (QQ+, AK, AQ, some suited broadways and pairs). If I call I’m OOP and likely to face difficult post‑flop decisions, and if the button comes along it becomes a multiway pot where A9o’s equity drops further. 4‑betting/isolation is unattractive — A9o is not a good 4‑bet candidate and I don’t want to bloat a pot OOP with a dominated hand. With deep stacks and better spots to pick, the +EV play here is to fold and wait for a clearer opportunity.

I opened from the button with Jack-Eight offsuit, which is a standard steal attempt. Elon has responded with a 3-bet from the small blind to 900. While I have position on Elon, J8o is too weak a hand to defend against a 3-bet. It has poor playability and is likely dominated by Elon's value range. Given the stack depths and the stage of the tournament, folding here is the most prudent move to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Preflop on the button with QTo offsuit, a decent speculative hand with good position. Pot is 150 from blinds (50/100), to call 100 suggests SB (Minni) likely folded, leaving heads-up vs BB (Flash). Raising from the button vs a single BB is standard to steal blinds or play in position postflop. QTo has decent equity and playability. Standard raise size around 3x BB to 300, given pot odds and history of 300 raises being common. My stack ~109BB effective, plenty of room. Builds pot when ahead or steals.

I’m in the small blind with 2♦6♠ — a very weak, unsuited hand with poor playability and almost no bluff/blocker value. Elon opened to 300 from the button (3x BB). Calling costs 250 more to play out of position against two opponents and likely commits me to multi-street decisions where this hand has low equity and large reverse implied odds. 3-betting as a bluff is theoretically possible vs a wide open, but with no blockers and deep stacks it’s too risky and I won’t have reliable fold equity. Preserve chips for better spots and fold.

In a 3-handed game, Queen-Jack suited (QJh) is a premium hand, especially when facing a button open. I am in the Big Blind and Elon has raised to 300 from the button. While calling is an option to see a flop cheaply, I prefer to 3-bet here to 1000 to take the initiative, define Elon's range, and potentially win the pot pre-flop. QJh flops very well even if called, and 3-betting helps protect my blind against frequent button steals.

Facing a 3bet from BB after my button raise to 300. QTo offsuit is playable in position heads-up with deep stacks (~100BB effective). Has decent equity (~35-40% vs typical BB 3bet range of QQ+, AK, suited broadways, some bluffs), straight potential, and blocks Q-high hands. Pot odds excellent: calling 700 more (total 1000) into 1350 pot for ~2:1 direct + strong implied odds. Folding too tight given position and history showing Flash active/aggressive in BB (multiple 3bets/re-raises in recent hands like 60,61,67,80). 4betting marginal as not premium strength, risks bloating pot OOP postflop. Prefer calling to realize equity, control pot size, and play postflop IP where QTo has good playability. Stack preservation balanced with aggression to build over 150 hands.

On this flop of Qd-Th-9s, I have top pair (Queens) and an open-ended straight draw (King or Eight). This is a very strong hand, but the board is extremely coordinated, meaning many cards on the turn could be problematic. Since I was the pre-flop 3-better, I have the range advantage and should lead out with a continuation bet to extract value from weaker queens, tens, nines, and various straight draws in Elon's calling range. A bet of around 40-50% of the pot is appropriate here to maintain initiative while not over-committing on such a wet board. I will bet 850.

Flop gives me top two pair with Queens and Tens (Qc pairs Qd, Td pairs Th), an extremely strong hand on a coordinated Q-T-9 rainbow board. Possible straight draws like J8o/suited, KT, or AT exist, plus sets (QQ, TT, 99) or better two pair (e.g., Q9, T9, QJ). Flash's preflop 3bet from BB (aggressive defender per history: Hands 60,61,67,80+ show frequent BB 3bets/raises) plus c-bet of 850 into ~2900 pot (~30% pot) polarizes his range: value (sets, two pair, overpairs) or bluffs/draws. Calling is fine given position and ~4.4:1 odds (pot 2900+850=3750), but raising extracts value from worse made hands (top pair, weaker pairs, stubborn draws) and charges/protects vs. floats/draws. Deep effective stacks (~10k) support multi-street play IP. Raising to 2500 (~3x his bet) balances value/protection without overcommitting; folds out pure air while getting calls from worse. Avoids check-raise trap or free cards. Stack preservation via fold equity if villain has monster.

On this highly coordinated flop of Qd-Th-9s, I have top pair (Queens) and an open-ended straight draw (any King or Eight). Elon's raise to 2500 is significant, indicating he likely has a strong hand like two pair (QT, T9), a set (TT, 99), or a made straight (KJ). However, my hand has a tremendous amount of equity; even if I am currently behind a set or two pair, I have 8 outs to a straight plus potential outs for trips or two pair. I am getting excellent pot odds (1650 to call into a total pot of 5400) to see a turn card. Calling allows me to realize my equity without over-committing against a hand that currently beats me, while keeping the pot manageable if the turn is a scare card.

On the turn, the eight of spades completes a straight for my Queen-Jack (8-9-10-J-Q). While the board is very coordinated and a King-Jack would make a higher straight, my hand is extremely strong. Elon showed significant aggression on the flop by raising my continuation bet to 2500, suggesting he has a strong range like sets (TT, 99), two pair (QT, T9), or a straight draw. By checking, I allow Elon to continue his aggression with those hands that I now beat or to potentially semi-bluff. Given that the pot is 7050 and Elon only has 3925 remaining, I am fully committed to the hand and will call any bet he makes.

Top two pair (Q-T) remains very strong on Q-T-9-8s board, beating most one-pair hands, overpairs, and unfinished draws in villain's polarized range (sets, str8s like J8, two-pair like Q9/T9, or bluffs/draws from aggressive BB 3bet/call). Turn 8s adds spade FD (two spades) and possible str8 completes (J8 now nuts str8). Villain's check OOP after hero flop raise/call suggests possible weakness (missed draw now checking back, air, or trapping monster), but betting extracts value from worse (TPx KQ/KJ/AQ, sets might raise). Protection needed vs. villainous draws getting there. Checking risks free river card to 10+ outs draws or cheap showdown. With position, deep stacks (~74BB eff.), initiative: bet 2/3 pot (~4700) for thin value + protection/denial. Size extracts from calls while preserving stack for river (leaves ~27BB behind). History shows Flash aggressive but checks here = exploit by betting strong hands.

On this highly coordinated board of Qd-Th-9s-8s, I have turned a straight (8-9-T-J-Q). While King-Jack (KJ) would make a higher straight (9-T-J-Q-K), my hand is still exceptionally strong and beats all sets (QQ, TT, 99, 88), two-pair combinations (QT, T9, Q8, etc.), and lower straights. Elon's significant aggression on the flop and this turn bet of 4700 represent a very narrow, polarized range. Given the pot odds and the strength of my hand in a 3-handed game, I cannot fold a straight here. I will call the bet, effectively committing my stack to the hand.

On the river 3h, which is a complete brick, the board remains highly coordinated (Qd-Th-9s-8s). I hold QJh, giving me a Queen-high straight (8-9-10-J-Q). The only hand that beats me is a King-high straight (KJ). Given Elon's aggression on the flop and turn, he likely has a very strong hand such as a set (QQ, TT, 99, 88), two pair (QT, T9), or a straight. While KJ is possible for him to have called a pre-flop 3-bet with, I cannot fold a straight in a 3-handed game with such favorable pot odds. With the pot at 16,450 and Elon having only 2,725 behind, we are effectively all-in. I will check to allow Elon to bet his remaining chips with his entire value range or any potential bluffs, and I will certainly call any shove.

River check to me holding top two pair (queens and tens) on a Qd-Th-9s-8s-3h board, an extremely strong hand. Beats all top pair hands (AQ, KQ, QJ, Q9 weaker kicker/two pair), pairs, draws, and most two pairs except specific better ones like Q9/T9 (still thin calls possible). Only loses to sets (QQ, TT, 99, 88 ~4 combos total in range) or completed straights (primarily J8 for nuts Q-J-T-9-8 ~4 combos, few J7/Jx). Villain's BB 3bet preflop polarizes to QQ+, AKo/s, broadway suited, some bluffs; flop c-bet then call large raise suggests hit top pair/set/draw; check-call massive turn bet (4700 into ~7k pot) on 8s completing spades and straights indicates strong holding or bluff-to-hero. Check river OOP continues weakness or trap. With stacks short (my 2725 into 16450 pot = 15% SPR, his 3500), shoving thin value from worse made hands (TPTK, weaker two pair, stubborn Ax) in his calling range while denying equity. Position allows bet; check loses value vs 20-40% range that calls (e.g., overpairs turned pairs now). History: Flash aggressive BB defender but check-calls big spots (e.g., Hand 51 river call). Maximizes stack by extracting max value late tournament.

I have a Queen-high straight (8-9-T-J-Q) on a board of Qd-Th-9s-8s-3h. This is an extremely strong hand in a 3-handed game. The only hand that currently beats me is a King-high straight (KJ). Elon has been aggressive since the flop, and his river bet of 2725 puts him all-in. The pot is offering massive odds (calling 2725 to win a total pot of over 21,000). I beat all sets (QQ, TT, 99, 88) and two-pair hands (QT, T9, etc.) that Elon could reasonably be value-betting here. Given the strength of my hand and the mathematical price, this is a mandatory call.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the small blind) with 5♠3♥. Pot is 150 and it only costs me 50 to complete, but in heads-up play I should be opening very wide from the button to seize initiative and apply fold equity. 5-3o is weak but playable with position — a small 2xBB raise (to 200) is a low-risk steal attempt that wins the blinds a decent percentage and still leaves me plenty of stack to navigate postflop. Plan: fold to a large 3-bet; if Flash calls I’ll c-bet small on dry boards, exercise pot control on coordinated boards, and proceed cautiously with marginal holdings. Limping surrenders initiative and gives Flash good odds, so I prefer to raise.

In this heads-up situation, Minni has raised to 200 from the small blind/button. I am in the big blind with Jack-Five offsuit. The cost to call is only 100 more into a pot of 300, giving me 3:1 odds. While J5o is a weak hand, in heads-up play, the defending range is much wider due to the pot odds and the fact that you only have one opponent to beat. I will call the minimum raise to see a flop and look to play fit-or-fold post-flop, especially since I currently hold a chip lead.

I have Jack-high on a Queen-high board with no draws. I am out of position. Checking is the standard play here to see how the opponent reacts. I'm looking to fold to most bet sizes as this board doesn't connect with my hand at all.

I raised preflop and got called — as the preflop aggressor on the button I should be c‑betting very frequently on this dry, rainbow Q‑2‑4 flop to generate fold equity. My hand (5♠‑3♥) has almost no showdown value and only a backdoor wheel (A outs) — it’s not worth building a big pot but is perfect for a small continuation bet. The pot is ~400; I’ll make a small c‑bet (~1/3 pot) to take it down often and to deny equity to worse holdings. If Flash folds I win the pot; if he calls I’ll proceed with pot control on the turn given my weak equity; if he raises I’ll fold and preserve my stack. Betting small also keeps my range wide and exploits Flash’s frequent checking on flops.

I have Jack-high with no draws on a Queen-high flop. Although Minni's bet of 150 into 400 is small, offering me roughly 3.6:1 odds, my hand has very little equity and no clear way to improve. Calling here would likely lead to me folding on the turn to any continued aggression. I will fold and preserve my chip lead for a better opportunity.
K7o is a strong enough hand to open from the small blind in a heads-up situation. I will raise to 300 to put pressure on the big blind and take control of the hand pre-flop.

Heads-up vs Button open to 300 from the SB. My hand (2♣8♦) is very weak and out of position, but the raise is small relative to stacks so calling 200 more is cheap. Pot is 400; calling 200 to make the pot 600 gives me ~33% break-even equity. Against a wide BTN opening range my raw equity is marginal but not zero, and defending some percentage prevents being too exploitable. I will call to see a flop, but plan to fold to meaningful continuation-barrel aggression unless I pair, make two pair/straights, or get a strong draw/backdoor diamond help. I will not 3-bet with this junk hand — keep the pot controllable and preserve stack for better spots.

Check. I have 2♣8♦ on a T♥ Q♥ 3♥ flop — no pair, no heart, no meaningful draw (only a very remote backdoor diamond). Heads‑up the SB’s open is wide and they will c‑bet a high frequency, but this board heavily favors their range (top pairs, heart draws, broadway combos). Pot is small and my hand has negligible equity and poor playability out of position. I’ll check to keep the pot controllable and fold to any significant continuation bet, preserving my 17k stack for better spots or real equity (pair, two‑pair, straight/flush draw).

On a board of Th-Qh-3h, I have King-high with no heart. This board texture is extremely favorable for the Big Blind's calling range, which contains many hearts, Queens, and Tens. Since Minni checked to me, I will check behind to see a free turn card. Betting into such a wet board with no made hand and no heart draw is low-frequency and risky, as I have very little equity if called or raised.

Out of position with 2♣8♦ on T♥ Q♥ 3♥ 3♣. I have no pair, no heart, and essentially zero showdown value. We already defended cheaply preflop and checked the flop to keep the pot small. The turn pairing the board reduces my already-nil equity and there’s little point building a pot with nothing — my fold equity here is low versus a wide SB opening range that can still call a bluff with any pair or a heart draw. Pot (600) is tiny relative to effective stacks (~17k), so preserve chips for real equity spots. I will check and concede this street; plan to fold to any meaningful bet on the turn and look for better opportunities later.

I have King-high with no heart on a board that is very coordinated with three hearts and a paired three. Minni has checked both the flop and the turn. While her checks could indicate weakness, my hand has very little equity to improve and checking the flop makes a turn bet look less credible. By checking back, I see a free river and maintain a small pot with a hand that has some marginal showdown value against her complete air, while avoiding a check-raise or a call from any heart, Ten, or Queen in her range.

Board is T♥ Q♥ 3♥ 3♣ 3♠ and I hold 2♣8♦ — no pair, no heart, no showdown value. We already defended preflop cheaply and checked flop/turn to keep the pot small; opponent’s check-check line could be weakness or a slowplay, but a large portion of their raising range (Qx, Tx, any heart, or a paired hand) still beats me. Pot is only 600 vs ~17k effective stacks, so betting here would be a thin, low-ROI bluff with poor blockers and high chance of being called. I’ll conserve chips and check to see a showdown; if Flash leads out I’ll fold to any meaningful river bet.

The board has run out with three Threes, meaning every player has at least three-of-a-kind. My best five-card hand is 3-3-3-Q-K. Minni has checked all three streets, suggesting she likely doesn't have a Queen, a Ten, or the case Three (which would be a full house or quads). While my King kicker is strong in this 'trips' scenario, betting serves little purpose: a worse hand will not call, and I only get action from an Ace (3-3-3-Q-A) or a full house. I will check back to take my showdown value.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the SB) — 3♣8♦ offsuit is weak, but in a heads-up match the button should be opening a wide range. Limping here surrenders initiative and invites the BB to raise or take free flops; folding throws away the 50 chip SB with no chance to win the pot. I want to take the initiative and use positional advantage postflop while keeping the pot manageable with a marginal holding. A standard 2.5–3x open works well here: it gives fold equity, is not too large given deep stacks (~170bb), and keeps my range wide. I’ll raise to 300 total (add 250 more), plan to c-bet favorable flops, and fold to heavy 3-bets if Flash applies serious pressure.
K-Q offsuit is a top-tier hand in heads-up play. I will 3-bet to 1000 to take the initiative and extract value from Minni's wide opening range from the Small Blind.

Flash 3-bet to 1,000 after my button open to 300 — I must call 700 to continue. My hand 3♥8♦ is a weak, unsuited low holding with poor equity vs a 3-bet range that will be heavy in pairs and broadways (plus some suited connectors). Even though I’d be in position postflop, this hand plays terribly multi-street against a sizable 3-bet and offers no good blocker value for a 4-bet bluff. With deep stacks this spot can balloon into a big, difficult pot for very little equity, so the correct, lowest-variance line is to fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with 5s 6s, which is a strong starting hand in a heads-up scenario. I will raise to 300 to take initiative and build the pot, as suited connectors have good playability and equity against a wide range of defending hands from the Big Blind.

Facing a 3bb open from the button (SB). Q♠6♥ is marginal but defendable heads-up from the big blind — I’m getting ~3:1 pot odds (200 to call into a 400 pot) and we’re very deep-stacked so there are postflop implied odds. I’ll avoid a light 3-bet with a weak kicker out of position; calling keeps a wide range and lets me realize equity. Postflop plan: play cautiously out of position, fold to strong aggression on dry boards, and extract value or apply pressure when I hit a Q or develop strong draws.

Flop is A♦ T♠ 2♦; I hold Q♠6♥ — no diamonds, no pair, only a few weak outs (3 queens, 3 sixes) and poor equity against a button’s wide but A/T-heavy c-bet range. Heads-up the preflop raiser will c-bet a large portion, so this is a pot-control spot out of position. I’ll check to see what Flash does: fold to strong aggression, call small bets occasionally to float and realize equity, and only start applying pressure if I pair the Q or pick up a meaningful draw. With stacks deep relative to the pot (pot 600; my stack 16625 vs his 22775) there’s no need to bluff here — checking preserves chips and keeps turn options open.

As the pre-flop raiser, an Ace-high board like Ad-Ts-2d is very favorable for my perceived range. Even though I have no equity with 5s-6s, a continuation bet is standard here to put pressure on Minni's checking range, which likely consists of many hands that missed this flop or are marginal. A bet of 300 (half pot) is sufficient to test her strength and take down the pot immediately if she doesn't have an Ace or a strong Ten.

Call 300. Heads-up on A♦ T♠ 2♦ my Q♠6♥ is unimproved with no diamonds and only six clean outs (three Qs and three 6s) — ~24% to hit by the river. I need ≈25% equity to justify a 300 call into the current pot (900 -> 1,200 after my call), so this is borderline but acceptable given the button’s wide preflop/c-bet range and deep stacks (implied odds). Raising out of position with no real draws or fold equity is too risky; folding is too nitty heads-up. Plan: call to see the turn, fold to heavy turn aggression on blanks, call small turn bets to float, and if I pair a Q/6 or pick up a meaningful draw switch to value/raise or continue aggression.

I have Q♠6♥ on A♦ T♠ 2♦ 5♦ — I completely missed. I only have six clean outs (3 queens, 3 sixes), ~13% to improve on the river, and the turn diamond (5♦) only makes the board wetter for opponents (flush draws/diamond combos, any A♦ hands). Flash is the preflop raiser and c-bet the flop, so his range contains many Ax, tens, broadways and diamond draws that continue here. The pot is still small relative to our stacks (pot 1,200; stacks 16k vs 22k) so there’s no reason to manufacture a bluff out of position that will get raised or called by better hands. Checking keeps the pot controlled, preserves fold equity on the river, and lets me fold to significant aggression if Flash leads (which will usually mean I’m behind). Plan: check back the turn; fold to a large bet; call small-to-medium bets occasionally to realize equity and see the river; if I pair my Q or hit a 6 on the river I can turn that into value.

I have picked up a pair of fives on the turn, but the board is now quite dangerous with three diamonds present, potentially completing a flush for my opponent if they were drawing. Having been the aggressor pre-flop and on the flop, checking back here allows me to control the pot size and utilize the showdown value of my small pair. Betting again is risky as it's unlikely to fold out an Ace or a Ten, and I would be vulnerable to a check-raise if Minni has completed a flush or has a strong Ace.

Check. I missed the river with Q♠6♥ on A♦ T♠ 2♦ 5♦ 7♥ — I have no pair and no meaningful showdown value. Flash (preflop raiser) bet the flop and checked the turn, which is consistent with either pot-control strong hands (Ax/Tx) or a missed/mediocre flop‑cbet range. I have almost no folding equity and a thin bluff here would be called by any pair or most broadways in his range. The pot (1,200) is small relative to our stacks, so preserving chips and seeing a free showdown is the right play. I’ll check and fold to a river bet.

I have a pair of fives which has some showdown value. After Minni checked the river following our check-check on the turn, it's unlikely a bet will fold out an Ace or a Ten. Checking back allows me to see the showdown and potentially win the pot against missed draws or lower equity hands without risking a check-raise on a flush-heavy board.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the small blind) with 9♣8♠ — an off‑suit connector that plays very well in position. Facing only a 50 chip call to see the flop, I want to seize the initiative, build a pot I can play post‑flop, and deny the BB cheap odds to float or check‑raise. A standard 2x BB open (raise to 200 total, which includes my 50 SB) is optimal: it applies pressure to a wide defending range, keeps the pot manageable with deep stacks (if called the pot will be ~400), and preserves flexibility to c‑bet or fold to a big 3‑bet. Calling 50 is too passive; folding is unnecessary with this hand and position. I’ll raise to 200 and re‑evaluate on the flop.

I am in the big blind with Ten-Deuce offsuit. Minni has raised to 200 from the small blind, which is a min-raise. This means I only need to call 100 more chips to see the flop in a pot that will be 400. While T2o is a very weak hand, the pot odds (3:1) are theoretically favorable for a defense in a heads-up scenario. However, T2o is at the very bottom of the range and playing it out of position can be difficult. Given my significant stack lead and the fact that we are approaching the final hands of the tournament, I will choose to fold this weak hand and wait for a more profitable spot rather than risking chips out of position with a range disadvantage.

In a heads-up situation, Ten-Seven offsuit is a hand that is well within the standard opening range from the button/small blind. I have a significant chip lead over Minni (23,525 vs 16,325). Raising here allows me to take control of the pot early and potentially win the blinds immediately, or see a flop with the advantage of position. I'll make a standard 3x raise.

I’m in the big blind with 6♦5♣ facing a SB open to 300. Pot is 400 and I need to call 200 more (total commit 300). Heads-up I should defend the BB fairly wide — 6-5 has decent connectivity and the call is offered good pot odds (~33% break-even). I won’t 3-bet (min raise to 500) because this hand is weak and easily dominated; raising out of position with this holding is unnecessary risk. I’ll flat-call to see a flop and proceed cautiously OOP: look to continue only with a pair or strong draw, otherwise fold to large postflop aggression.

I have top pair (6) with a weak 5-kicker on 6♠-3♠-7♣. Heads-up vs a SB opener this is often the best hand but is vulnerable to spade flush draws and the occasional 7/3 or better pair. I want to both get value from worse hands (overcards that will call, small pairs) and deny equity to spade/straight draws, so I’ll take the initiative and bet ~2/3 pot. A 400 bet gives good protection while keeping the pot controllable given my weak kicker. Plan: if he folds I take it down; if called I re-evaluate on the turn (proceed cautiously on spades/overcards and respect sizable aggression or a check-raise).

On a flop of 6s-3s-7c, I have top pair with a Ten kicker. Minni's lead of 400 into a 600 pot is a strong bet, suggesting she either has a piece of the board, a draw (like a flush draw or a straight draw), or is trying to take the pot away. Since I have top pair in a heads-up situation, folding is not an option. Raising might bloat the pot unnecessarily and potentially drive away her bluffs while keeping me in against hands that beat me (like sets or two pair). Calling allows me to keep the pot manageable, maintain my positional advantage, and see how the turn develops.

Turn: the 5♥ pairs my 5 and gives me two pair (6s & 5s). Heads-up this is a very strong improvement — I'm ahead of most of Flash's flop-calling range (single pairs, underpairs, spade draws, weak broadways). My objectives are to extract thin value from one-pair hands and charge spade draws while keeping the river decision manageable. Pot is 1400, so a ~2/3-pot value/protection bet makes sense. Betting 1000: (1) prices many marginal hands and weaker pairs into calling, (2) denies two-spade flush draws the pot odds they need to continue on the river (they need ~29% to call here; a 2-card spade draw has ~19% to finish), and (3) keeps the pot controlled against made straights/sets. If Flash folds I take it; if he calls I re-evaluate the river and get value or exercise pot control. If he check-raises a large amount, I will assume a very strong holding (4 or set) and proceed cautiously — call small/medium raises to control risk, fold to extreme overbets.

On a board of 6s-3s-7c-5h, I have top pair (Sevens) with a Ten kicker. The turn 5h is a significant card because it completes a straight for any hand containing a 4 (3-4-5-6-7). Minni's lead on the flop and subsequent large bet on the turn (1000 into 1400) indicates strong interest in the pot. While her range includes straights, sets (33, 55, 66), and two-pairs (76, 75, 65), it also contains many semi-bluffs like flush draws (AsXs) and open-ended straight draws (89). In a heads-up situation, folding top pair to a single turn bet is generally too conservative, but raising would likely force out her bluffs and only get called by hands that beat me. Calling allows me to maintain pot control, keep her bluffs in, and see a river card with the plan to evaluate her final action.

River is a blank 2♣ and I hold 6♦5♣ — two pair (6s & 5s). Flash check-called the flop and turn, a line that includes lots of single pairs, underpairs and missed spade draws. Very few hands in his calling range actually beat me (sets or 4-8 straights); those hands would more often have bet or check‑raised earlier. Given heads‑up and his weakness on the river, this is a clear value-bet spot to charge worse one‑pair hands and deny any remaining equity. I’ll size to about half the pot to keep worse pairs and some river calls in while not overcommitting if he unexpectedly has the nuts. Bet 1,700 into the 3,400 pot. If he folds I take it; if he calls he likely has a worse pair; I’ll re-evaluate only if he check‑raises large (fold to extreme overbets, call small/medium raises).

On a board of 6s-3s-7c-5h-2c, I have top pair (Sevens) with a Ten kicker. This is a very strong hand in a heads-up situation. Minni has led out on every street, which in heads-up play can represent a polarized range of either strong value hands (like a straight with a 4, two pair, or sets) or missed draws. The board has a completed straight possibility (3-4-5-6-7), but it also contains many missed spade flush draws (like AsXs, KsXs) and straight draws (like 8-9). Given the pot odds of 3-to-1 (calling 1700 to win a total pot of 6800), folding top pair is too exploitable. I must call to see if my pair of sevens is good against her range, which includes a significant number of potential bluffs.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the SB) — huge positional advantage. 5♥4♦ is a weak but playable, connected hand in heads-up; standard strategy is to open very wide from the button to generate fold equity and play post‑flop in position. I’ll make the minimum raise to 200 (total committed, which includes my 50 SB) to conserve chips while applying pressure. If Flash 3-bets big I’ll fold (this hand plays poorly vs a strong 3‑bet range); if called I’ll play the flop in position — c-bet and attack on low/connected boards, pot-control or check/float on A/K/Q heavy or very dry boards.

In this heads-up situation, I am in the Big Blind with 9s 5c. Minni has made a minimum raise to 200. While the pot odds are 3:1 to call (100 more into a 300 pot), 95o is a very weak hand to play out of position against an active opponent. I prefer to fold and wait for a more favorable hand or a better spot to utilize my stack advantage, rather than potentially losing more chips on later streets with a marginal holding.

I am holding King of Spades and Six of Spades on the button in a heads-up scenario. This is a strong suited King and well within my opening range for the Small Blind. I will raise to 300 to take control of the hand and build the pot, a size consistent with my previous opening raises.

Flash opened to 300 from the button. I'm in the big blind with Qh‑Td. With deep stacks and only 200 more to call, QTo is a very reasonable defend heads‑up: it has good equity and useful blockers. I prefer to call rather than 3‑bet OOP with a hand that is often dominated — 3‑betting here would bloat the pot and invite 4‑bets. Calling keeps the pot manageable, preserves post‑flop maneuverability, and lets me play aggressively if I hit top pair or strong draws while folding/controlling on dry boards against heavy action. I'll call to see a flop (300 total).

I have top pair (T) with a good queen kicker on a very dry flop (Ts 3h 2c). Heads‑up vs the preflop raiser, his range is wide (many overcards, broadways, some weaker pairs and occasional tens/sets). I want value and to deny free cards while keeping the pot under control with deep stacks. Leading for about half the pot should get value from worse tens and many Kx/Qx hands, fold out pure bluffs/overcards, and keep weaker hands from floating cheaply. If he raises big I’ll re‑evaluate (sets/two‑pair are possible and I’ll proceed cautiously); if he calls I’ll play the turn for value/control. Bet ~1/2 pot (300).

On this dry Ten-high flop, Minni has led out with a 'donk bet' of 300 into the 600 pot. My King of Spades provides one overcard to the board and a backdoor spade flush draw. Given that I have position and the price to call is reasonable (3:1 odds), I will call to see how the turn develops. This allows me to potentially realize my equity if a King or a spade appears, or potentially take the pot away on the turn if Minni shows weakness by checking.

I have top pair T with a strong queen kicker on a very dry board (T♠ 3♥ 2♣ 5♠). Villain called my small flop c-bet, so his range is wide (weaker tens, Kx/Qx, A‑high floats, occasional pocket pairs). I want thin value and to deny free cards while keeping the pot controllable with deep stacks. Betting ~1/2 pot (600 into the 1200) extracts value from worse tens and Qx, charges the rare straight/draw combos, and sets up a reasonable river decision if called. If he raises large I’ll give weight to sets/two‑pair/straights and re‑evaluate (likely fold to a big polarized raise); if he just calls I’ll proceed to evaluate the river for another value sizing or a check/backdown.

On the turn, the Five of Spades provides me with a nut flush draw to go along with my King overcard. Minni has continued her aggression by betting 600 into the 1200 pot. I have 9 outs for the flush and potentially 3 outs if a King is the best hand, giving me roughly 26% equity. With 600 to call for a total pot of 2400, I am getting exactly the 4:1 (25%) odds needed to make a profitable call based on card equity alone, not including potential implied odds if I hit the river or fold equity on later streets. I will call to see the river.

I have T♦Q♥ on T♠‑3♥‑2♣‑5♠‑5♦: top pair with a strong queen kicker. Villain cold‑opened pre and then passively called my ~1/2 pot bets on the flop and turn, so his continuing range is wide (weaker tens, Qx/Kx/A‑high floats, some small pairs) — but the river 5 creates the risk of trip fives. Those 5x combos in his line are relatively rare compared with the mass of worse hands that will still call a modest river bet. I want thin value while keeping the pot controllable and allowing an easy fold to a raise, so I’ll bet about half the pot to get called by worse tens and many Qx/Kx/Ax floats but not overcommit against the occasional trips/sets. Bet 1200 into 2400; if he raises large I’ll give credit to a very strong hand and fold.

On the river, the board has paired with a second Five, and my spade flush draw has failed to materialize. I am left with King-high. Minni has led into me on every single street (donk betting the flop and continuing on the turn and river) with consistent half-pot sizing (300 into 600, 600 into 1200, and now 1200 into 2400). This betting pattern strongly suggests a value hand, likely at least a Ten or a pocket pair that she is protecting. While it is possible she is bluffing with a missed straight draw like 4-6, King-high is too weak to act as a reliable bluff-catcher in a triple-barrel scenario. To maximize my stack over the long term, I must avoid calling down with marginal non-pair hands when the opponent shows consistent aggression. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Heads-up on the button (I’m SB) with K♣9♥. This is a hand I want to play for value and initiative in position rather than limp; deep stacks and positional leverage favor an open raise. I’ll raise to 300 total (≈3BB) to apply pressure, isolate the BB, and build a pot I can play postflop. Size gives good fold equity vs marginal hands but isn’t so large that I’m overcommitted if Flash 3-bets; plan is to fold to a large 3-bet, call a small/medium 3-bet and play cautiously postflop, and C-bet standard textures to exploit position.

In Hand #121, I am in the big blind with 7c 5h. Minni, on the button, has raised to 300. This is a heads-up situation. Calling for an additional 200 chips into a total pot that will be 600 offers 2:1 odds. However, 7-5 offsuit is a weak hand to play out of position against an opponent who has shown aggression in previous hands. While I have a healthy stack, there is little reason to defend this specific hand against a standard button raise. I will fold and wait for a better spot to engage.

J5o is a weak hand even for heads-up play from the button. Given Minni's recent aggression and my slight stack deficit, I prefer to fold this marginal hand and wait for a better opportunity to contest the pot in the remaining hands of the tournament.

Heads-up on the button with Js2c: ranges should be very wide and position postflop is valuable. I want to take the initiative and try to steal the big blind rather than limp a marginal hand that is easily dominated. A standard button open in this game is ~3x the big blind, so I’ll raise to 300 to apply pressure, deny equity to many of Flash’s calling hands, and play last on the flop. Plan: fold to a large, committed 3-bet with this weak kicker; if Flash calls I’ll c-bet favorable flops and proceed cautiously when the board is unkind to my hand.

I am in the Big Blind with K7s, a strong suited king that plays well heads-up. Minni, on the button, has raised to 3x (300 total). Given our deep stacks and the strength of K7s, I will defend by calling to see a flop rather than 3-betting, as I want to keep her range wide and play post-flop with position being a disadvantage, but my hand strength making it a standard call.

On a board of Qh-Ts-Kh, I have top pair with a King and a 7 kicker. This board is extremely wet, containing numerous straight possibilities (AJ, J9) and two-pair combinations (KQ, KT, QT) that hit a button-raising range hard. Since I am out of position against Minni, the pre-flop aggressor, checking is the standard play to see how she reacts and to keep the pot manageable with a vulnerable top pair.

Heads-up on the button I opened preflop and see K-Q-T on the flop with Js2c. I have an open-ended straight draw (A or 9 complete the broadway straight — 8 outs, roughly ~31–32% to hit by river) but almost no showdown value right now. Flash checked, showing some weakness, so I should take the initiative and apply pressure with a semi-bluff that has real equity. I’ll c-bet about 2/3 pot (400) to fold out pure air, charge worse pairs and smaller draws, and build the pot when I have ~32% equity. Plan: call-down or pot-control if called and I miss the turn; fold to a large raise (I’m not calling huge raise as a semi-bluff); if I hit an A or 9 I’ll extract value on later streets.

On a board of Qh-Ts-Kh, I have top pair with King of spades and a seven kicker. While top pair is a strong holding, this board is extremely dangerous as it connects heavily with a standard button-opening range, containing many two-pair combinations (KQ, KT, QT), sets (TT, QQ, KK), and straights (AJ, J9). Minni's 2/3 pot bet of 400 is a standard continuation. Raising here would likely only be called by better hands or very strong draws that have significant equity against me. By calling, I keep the pot manageable, allow Minni to continue with bluffs or weaker pairs, and give myself a chance to re-evaluate on the turn based on the card and her subsequent action.

On the turn, the board has become extremely dangerous with the arrival of the Jack of Hearts. This card completes several possible straights (any Ace or any Nine) and creates numerous two-pair combinations (QJ, JT) that hit a standard button-opening range. My pair of Kings with a seven kicker has plummeted in relative strength. Being first to act post-flop in this heads-up situation, I must check to control the pot and see how Minni reacts to this highly connected board. I am prepared to fold to significant aggression as my hand is now primarily a bluff-catcher at best.

Turn puts a Jack on the board giving me a pair of Jacks (Js2c) but this is a very hostile board: K–Q–J–T with three hearts. Any A or 9 already has a straight, any K/Q/T pair or J with a better kicker beats me, and any two hearts in Flash’s hand is a made flush. Flash’s line (call on the flop, check the turn) shows some passivity but doesn’t narrow his range enough — many hands that beat me will just check/call. I don’t want to bloat the pot with a one-pair hand and a 2 kicker into a range that contains many made hands. Pot-control is the best line: check back the turn, keep the pot manageable, and reevaluate on the river. Plan for the river: call small-to-medium bluffs/value bets, fold to very large bets that represent straights/flushes, and be prepared to show down if he checks behind.

The river brings a fourth heart to the board (5h), making any single heart in the opponent's hand a flush. While I have top pair with Kings, its relative strength has been significantly diminished by both the flush and straight possibilities (any Ace or Nine). Since I do not hold a heart, I have no reason to bet as a value play, and a bluff is unlikely to succeed against a wide range that includes many small hearts. I will check and expect to fold to a bet unless it is extremely small, as I am likely beaten by even a marginal heart or a straight.

I have second pair (J♠) with a 2 kicker on a very wet, high-card board: Q♥ T♠ K♥ J♥ 5♥. This board beats a lot of Flash’s calling range (Ks/Qs/Ts, two-pairs, sets, broadway straights with A/9, and now any heart on river makes a flush). Flash’s line (check-call flop, check turn) is passive and can represent either a medium-strength made hand or a trap, so a value bet here will get called by most hands that beat me and fold out only pure air. Pot-control is optimal: I don’t want to inflate the pot with a vulnerable one-pair hand and terrible kicker. I’ll check and take it to showdown, prepared to call small river bluffs but to fold to large aggressive value shoves representing straights/flushes.

In a heads-up situation, Ten-Nine offsuit is a strong enough hand to open for a raise from the button (Small Blind). Raising to 300 (3x the big blind) is a standard open that allows me to take initiative and play the rest of the hand in position if called. Folding is too conservative for this hand in this position.

Heads-up on the button and Flash opened to 300 — he has a wide range. I have pocket 7s, which is a strong hand preflop vs a wide opener but not worth bloating a pot out of position with a small 3-bet. Calling 200 more (total committed 300) gives good pot odds, preserves my deep stack (about 20k), and lets me realize equity (set-mining) or play postflop cautiously depending on texture and opponent actions. I’ll call and evaluate the flop before committing more chips.

Flop is 5c 3d 5s and I hold pocket 7s — an overpair to the board. Heads-up vs Flash's wide opening range (who checked this flop), I beat most of his checking range (overcards, single-pair hands like 6x/4x, floats) and only lose to 5x (or very rarely a slow-played 33). The board is relatively dry and there are few real draws, so I want to extract value and charge any turn floats while keeping the pot controlled. A half‑pot bet (~300 into 600) accomplishes that: gets value from worse pairs and some broadways, keeps the sizing small enough not to commit too much versus a set, and preserves flexibility for the turn. If he raises big I can put him on a set/two‑pair and fold or re-evaluate; if he calls I proceed cautiously on the turn.

On this paired, dry board of 5-3-5, Minni has led out with a donk bet of 300 into a pot of 600. While I have two overcards with Ten-Nine, I have no backdoor draws or reliable equity if she has even a small pair or a 5. Minni has shown aggression in previous hands, frequently following through on multiple streets. Floating with 10-high in this spot is risky as I would likely be forced to fold on the turn if she continues her aggression, and I have no real way to improve to a dominant hand. Given the tournament stage and our nearly equal stack sizes, I will fold and wait for a more favorable opportunity.

Heads-up on the button with A♣5♣ (Ah5c) and deep stacks (~200BB). This is a great spot to take the initiative — A5o has strong playability in position, blocks some A-high hands, and has good postflop equity. I don’t want to just limp and give free flops, so I’ll raise to apply pressure, isolate the BB, and build a pot I can play from position. A raise to 300 (~3x BB) is a clear, standard size: it gives fold equity, discourages cheap multiway calls, and keeps the pot manageable deep-stacked. If Flash 3-bets big I’ll re-evaluate (usually fold this hand to an isolating 3-bet); if he calls I’ll use position to c-bet favorable flops or control the pot when missed.

I am holding 63 offsuit in the big blind. Minni has raised to 3x from the button. While I have 2:1 odds to call, 63o is one of the weakest starting hands and plays very poorly post-flop, especially out of position. Given the tournament stage and the nearly even stacks, there is no reason to defend with junk. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with 4c 5c, which is a strong speculative hand in a heads-up situation. I will raise to 300 to take control of the hand and apply pressure to the big blind. This sizing is consistent with my previous play and allows me to play a pot with a hand that has good post-flop potential.

Heads-up in the big blind vs a small-blind open to 300. I have Ac6c — a suited Ace that plays very well heads-up and has good postflop equity and blocker value. Pot is 400 and I need to call 200. With deep stacks (~208bb) I prefer to defend a wide range from the BB rather than 3-bet small and build a big pot with a marginal hand that can be dominated by better Aces. Calling keeps their weaker hands and draws in, lets me realize equity, and keeps postflop decisionmaking flexible. Plan: call and evaluate the flop — continue aggressively on A or club flops, exercise pot control or fold to heavy aggression on dry / unfavorable flops.

Heads‑up vs a SB open — I defended wide with Ac6c. Flop 2h‑3c‑7d is very dry and misses a large portion of the opener’s range. I have Ace‑high (a strong blocker) and a backdoor club — a hand that wants to apply pressure rather than passively give up the pot. Betting about half‑pot (≈300) will fold out many unpaired hands and small cards, charge any straight/diamond/backdoor draws, and get value from worse A‑x hands and some pairs. If Flash raises I’ll re‑evaluate (likely fold to a large, polarized raise; consider calling small raises to realize equity given my blocker).

I have an open-ended straight draw with 4c 5c on a 2h-3c-7d board. Any Ace or Six will give me a straight. Minni has led out with a 'donk bet' of 300 into a pot of 600. This action often represents a marginal hand like a small pair or a 7. I am getting 3:1 odds on a call, and with 8 clean outs (approx 16% to hit on the turn), the call is mathematically justified when considering implied odds. I will call to see the turn card and keep the pot manageable while in position.

Turn: Ad pairs the board and gives me top pair with a weak 6-kicker (Ac6c). I c-bet the flop and Flash called, so his continuing range is fairly wide (Kx/Qx/small pairs and some Ax). He checks the turn, which can be pot-control or weakness. I want to extract value from worse hands and deny equity to any backdoor draws while keeping the pot manageable against stronger Aces/sets, so I’ll bet a medium size (~1/2 pot). If he raises big I’ll fold this marginal kicker; if he calls I’ll proceed cautiously on the river.

On the turn, the Ace of Diamonds completes a wheel straight (A-2-3-4-5) for my hand. Given the current board of 2h-3c-7d-Ad, this is the absolute nuts as no higher straight or flush is possible. Minni has led out on both the flop and the turn, which suggests she likely has a strong made hand like top pair, two pair, or a set. Since I have the best possible hand, I will raise to 1800 to build the pot and extract maximum value before the river. This sizing is a 3x raise of her turn bet, designed to look like a protection bet or a semi-bluff on the Ace, encouraging a call from her entire range.

Pot is 3,600 and I face a raise to 1,800 after I bet the turn 600. I have Ac6c on 2h-3c-7d-Ad — top pair with a weak kicker and a club blocker. Calling 1,200 is cheap (break-even equity ≈25%). Flash’s line (called my flop c‑bet, then raised the turn) is polarized: sets/two‑pair/Ax value plus some floats/semi‑bluffs. Because I hold the Ace blocker and have decent equity vs a wide continuing range (and I beat a lot of the small pairs and Kx hands that called the flop), a call is the correct, low‑risk play. A raise would commit too much with a marginal kicker; folding concedes equity for too little. Plan: call, see the river and fold to a large shove or continue vs small/medium river bets (or value bet a checked river if it’s safe).

Flash opened preflop, called my flop c‑bet, then check‑raised my turn bet very large (bet 600 → raised to 1,800). That line is polarized toward sets/two‑pair/strong Ax or a big bluff. The river 4♥ is a dangerous card — it completes the only realistic straight (A‑2‑3‑4‑5) for any opponent holding a 5 and adds a few two‑pair/paired‑board combos. I have A♣6♣: top pair but a very weak kicker; I block some Ax combinations but still lose to sets/two‑pair/any 5 that now has a straight. There is no bet to call, and betting risks getting raised and facing a costly decision with a marginal hand. The lowest‑variance, +EV line here is to check to showdown and avoid bloating the pot further. If Flash bets small I’ll consider a call for showdown; if he jams I’ll fold.

On the river, the board is 2h-3c-7d-Ad-4h. I hold 4c-5c, which gives me a wheel straight (A-2-3-4-5). While a higher straight is possible if the opponent holds 5-6 (making 3-4-5-6-7), Minni has checked the river after calling my significant raise on the turn. This check likely indicates she has a strong but non-nut hand, such as top pair, two pair, or a set, or perhaps she is holding a 5 for the same straight and is playing it cautiously. Given the strength of my hand, I must bet for value to extract chips from her calling range (Aces, sets, or a lower straight). I will place a bet of 3200 into the 4800 pot, making my total commitment for the hand 5000 (1800 from previous streets + 3200 river bet).

I will CALL 5,000.  Recap: I have A♣6♣ on 2♥-3♣-7♦-A♦-4♥. Line: I c‑bet the flop and was called; I bet the turn (600) and Flash check‑raised to 1,800 — I called. He now bets 5,000 into a 9,800 pot (~51%).

Reasoning: my A‑6 is top pair but a very weak kicker; the turn check‑raise is polarizing toward strong value (sets, two‑pair, strong Ax) but can also include turn bluffs/semi‑bluffs. The river 4♥ brings only a small extra danger set (A4 makes two‑pair) and completes the A‑2‑3‑4‑5 straight for any 5. My A♣ blocks some Ax value combos, and the river sizing gives me ~34% break‑even equity (5,000/(9,800+5,000) ≈ 33.8%). Given the bet size and blocker, I expect enough bluffs/weaker pairs in his range to justify a call to showdown. Raising would commit too much with a marginal kicker and folding concedes equity for cheap. I will call the 5,000 to see the showdown; if he had shoved a much larger raise I would fold this weak kicker.

Heads-up on the button with Ah5c — a hand I want to play aggressively preflop. I already have position and an Ace blocker, and the pot is only 150 with 50 to call. Opening is standard here to steal the blind and build pots in position. I’ll raise to 300 (3x BB): that gives good fold equity, discourages light 3-bets compared with a minimum to 200, and keeps postflop play manageable with a 13.5k stack. Note I’ve already posted the 50 SB, so total committed will be 300 (adding 250). If Flash calls I’ll use position to continue; if he 3-bets large I’ll re-evaluate based on sizing and range.

I am currently the chip leader in this heads-up match after winning a massive pot in the previous hand. I am dealt Jack-Deuce offsuit (Jh 2c) in the Big Blind. While I am getting 2:1 odds to call Minni's raise to 300, J2o is an extremely weak hand with very poor playability post-flop, especially out of position. Given my significant stack lead (26,325 vs 13,275), I have no reason to defend with trash hands. I will wait for better spots to utilize my stack pressure rather than giving Minni a chance to recoup chips in a marginalized situation.

In a heads-up situation, Queen-Eight suited (Qs8s) is a very strong starting hand, well above the average. I am currently on the button (Small Blind) with a significant chip lead. I will open with a standard raise to 300 to exert pressure and build a pot with a hand that has good post-flop playability and showdown value.

Heads-up on the button Flash opened to 300; I'm in the big blind with K♣8♦. This is a defendable hand in a 2‑player pot — folding is too nitty and a small 3‑bet to 500 has little fold equity and just bloats a pot I’ll be OOP in. Pot is ~400 and I need to call 200 more (calling 300 total) which gives ~3:1 immediate pot odds (need ≈25% equity) — K8o has reasonable equity vs a wide opening range. I'll call to see a flop, play cautiously OOP on coordinated/ace‑high boards, and look to bluff/raise on favorable runouts or fold to heavy aggression.

I'm out of position with K♣8♦ on 3♦-5♦-4♠. I have no pair and only a single diamond — this is essentially a weak K‑high with a negligible backdoor flush (need both turn and river). The flop heavily favors the preflop raiser’s range (small pairs, connected cards, many hands that will c‑bet). Betting OOP here to try to steal risks getting check‑raised or committed with very little equity. Pot control is the priority: check and see how Flash reacts. If he checks back I get a free card; if he fires a small bet I can call to see a turn but will fold to large aggression. If I improve (pair a K/8 or pick up meaningful draws) I can take a more aggressive line on the turn.

On a low, connected board of 3-5-4, the Big Blind's calling range is much more likely to have connected with the cards than my pre-flop raising range. Holding only Queen-high with some backdoor potential, I have very little showdown value and limited equity if called. While a continuation bet is an option, checking back here allows me to control the size of the pot and see a turn card for free. This is a safer play that avoids bloating the pot on a texture that heavily favors the defender.

I check. I have K♣8♦ and my backdoor diamond flush has been killed by the turn (T♥), so I have essentially K‑high with only 6 outs (three Ks and three 8s) to pair on the river — ~6/46 ≈ 13.0% to improve. The pot is 600 and the minimum turn bet is 100, which would make calling 100 into 700 needing ~14.3% equity to break even. My equity (≈13%) is below that, so calling a minimum bet is -EV and larger bets are even worse. I want pot control OOP and to see a free river for a possible pair; if Flash bets the turn I will fold (unless he posts an absurdly small bet below breakeven, which rules out here). Checking keeps options open and preserves my stack.

Following my check on the flop, Minni's second check on the turn (Ten of Hearts) signals significant weakness. Although the board is connected, the Ten is unlikely to have improved her range significantly if she didn't lead out. I will execute a delayed continuation bet of 300 (half-pot) to fold out her air and weak overcards. Given that I have zero showdown value with Queen-high, betting now is the most effective way to realize equity and take down the pot.

Board: 3♦-5♦-4♠-T♥. I hold K♣8♦. After I checked the turn Flash bets 300 into a 900 pot (I must call 300). I have only 6 clean outs (three Ks + three 8s) to pair on the river — ~6/46 ≈ 13% equity. The break-even equity to call is 300/(900+300) = 25%, so a call is strongly -EV. Raising to the min (to 600) would be a pure bluff with limited fold equity against Flash’s opening/turn-betting range (pairs, tens, some broadways) and would bloat a pot I’m unlikely to win; stacks are deep enough he can call or shove. Given the math and table dynamics, preserve my stack and fold.

Heads-up on the button (I am SB) with 2c9s — a marginal, non‑suited hand but I have position post‑flop and a very cheap price. The pot is 150 and it only costs me 50 more to see the flop (100 total committed including my SB). Rather than inflate the pot with a raise with a weak hand against an active big stack, I’ll call to realize equity and use position to fold to strong action unless I connect. If I hit a pair, a 9/2, or get a decent draw I can proceed; otherwise I’ll control the pot and fold to heavy aggression.

I am in the Big Blind with J5s. Minni, on the button, has just completed the small blind (limped). My stack is significantly larger than Minni's (26,525 vs 13,275). J5 offsuit is a weak hand, but since it is a limped pot and I am already in the big blind, I have the option to check and see a flop for free. Raising here would be unnecessary risk with such a marginal hand, and folding is not an option when I can check.

The flop is 8c Ah As, and I hold Jh 5s. In a limped pot, this board is very dry. Since I am first to act and have no piece of the board, checking is the standard play. I want to see if Minni shows interest in the pot or if we can see a cheap turn. Leading out here doesn't accomplish much against her range in a small pot.

Flop A♥ A♠ 8♣ — both aces are on the board, so no one can hold an Ace; the only real made hands are pocket pairs (giving two pair with the board) or an 8. I hold 9♠2♣: essentially air with only very slim backdoor equity. Pot is tiny (200) and I have position. Flash’s check is ambiguous (weak or slowplay), but I don’t want to bloat the pot with no hand and risk a check‑raise or a large turn bet from a better made hand. Plan: control the pot, realize equity, and fold to meaningful aggression. Check and re‑evaluate on the turn — if Flash checks again I can take a free card or probe later; if he bets I’ll fold most sizes.

On a board of 8c Ah As Th, I hold Jack high (Jh 5s). The pot is tiny (200 chips) because Minni limped pre-flop and we both checked the flop. My hand has no showdown value, but in a limped pot where Minni has checked twice, her range is very wide and likely weak. However, leading out now into a very small pot doesn't achieve much value or fold equity against any marginal piece she might have. I will check and see if she checks behind or bets, and I can re-evaluate on the river.

Heads‑up, small pot (200) and I have 9♠2♣ — no pair, no real draws and essentially no showdown equity. Flash checked flop and turn; that can be weakness or a slowplay, but I don’t want to bloat the pot with air against a much deeper stack. With position I’ll control the pot and realize equity: check and fold to meaningful aggression. If Flash checks the river as well I can reconsider a small bluff on the river, but for now avoid committing chips with no hand.

We are at the river in a limped, multi-checked pot with Jack-high. The board is 8c Ah As Th 4s. My hand has very little showdown value, but in a pot this small where neither player has shown any aggression, there is no reason to turn my hand into a bluff. I will check and hope for a free showdown to see if my Jack-high can beat a worse high-card hand, and I will fold to any significant bet.

I have 9♠2♣ — no pair, no meaningful showdown equity on A♥ A♠ 8♣ T♥ 4♠. Flash has checked flop, turn and river; that line can be either weak or a slowplayed very strong hand (pocket 8s, TT turned a set/full house, or a small pair that improved). The pot is tiny (200) relative to our stack and villain's stack, and I hold no blockers to Aces/Tens that would make a bluff reliably fold better hands. With tournament life and long‑term chip preservation in mind, the +EV line is to avoid bluffing here: check and fold to meaningful aggression rather than risking chips with essentially air.

I am currently the chip leader with a significant 2:1 advantage over Minni following a large pot win in Hand #126. While 7-5 offsuit is a weak hand, in a heads-up situation from the Small Blind, it is often profitable to maintain aggression and put pressure on the shorter stack. I will raise to 300 to try and take the pot pre-flop, but I am prepared to fold if Minni shows significant strength through a 3-bet.

Heads-up vs the button who open-raised to 300. I have A♠8♣ — a reasonably strong heads-up hand that blocks a lot of the opponent’s A‑x combos and has good showdown and postflop playability. Stacks are deep (~132bb effective) but I’m out of position, so I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal 3‑bet. Calling 200 more (300 total committed) keeps the pot manageable, lets me realize equity against what is likely a wide BTN opening range, and gives me the chance to play flops where I hit top pair or good draws. I’ll proceed cautiously on dry boards and look for value when I connect.

I have A♠8♣ on 3♦-8♥-7♠ — top pair with the best possible kicker on a relatively dry, moderately connected board. We’re OOP versus a BTN open-raiser who will c-bet a wide range heads-up. With deep stacks (~130bb effective) I don’t want to bloat the pot with only one pair. Checking here controls the pot, induces the button to fire with a wide range (bluffs, floats, worse pairs), and lets me call reasonable c-bets to realize equity and re-evaluate on the turn. Plan: check and call standard c-bets (~1/3–1/2 pot); consider a delayed value bet on a blank turn or a check-raise only if Hero reads a clear bluff. I’ll avoid leading or over-committing OOP with a single pair.

On a board of 3d-8h-7s, I have flopped middle pair (Sevens) with a 5 kicker. Minni has checked to me. Since I am the chip leader and the aggressor pre-flop, a continuation bet here serves to protect my hand against overcards (like QJ, KQ, or AJ) and to extract value from weaker draws or lower pairs (like a 3 or pocket 4s/5s/6s). A bet of 300 (half the pot) is a standard size that allows me to control the action while denying free equity to Minni's range.

I have A♠8♣ on 3♦-8♥-7♠ — top pair with the best possible kicker, but I’m out of position and stacks are deep (~130bb). Flash opened wide on the button and c-bet a small ~1/3 pot (300 into the current pot), so his range contains a lot of bluffs and worse pairs. I want to keep the pot controlled and let him bluff with worse hands or float; a raise here bloats the pot OOP with a marginal one‑pair hand and is unnecessarily committal. I’ll call the 300, realize my equity, and re‑evaluate on the turn (look to value-bet blanks, call reasonable barrels, fold to very large/clear-strength aggression on scary turns).

I opened the hand preflop with a wide BTN open and defended in the BB with A♠8♣. I checked the flop and called a small c-bet — I have top pair with the best kicker and wanted to keep the pot under control OOP. The 6♣ on the turn is largely a “blank” vs the BTN’s wide continuing range (there are very few single-card straights; two-card combos exist but are rare). With ~126bb effective and a pot of 1,200, this is a good spot to extract thin value without bloating the pot out of position. A half‑pot bet (~600) will get called by worse pairs, Kx/Qx/Jx type hands and some floats, while charging the few draws and folding out some pure air. If Flash check‑raises very large (polarized or shove), I’ll assume heavy value (sets/two‑pair) and fold; I’ll be willing to call a small/standard raise and re‑evaluate on the river. Bet 600 to take value and keep options on the river.

On the turn, the board is 3d-8h-7s-6c. I have second pair (7s) and an open-ended straight draw (needing a 4 or a 9). Minni has led for 600 into a 1200 pot. This lead often indicates she has hit a piece of the board, perhaps two pair (8-7, 7-6) or a straight (9-5, 5-4), or is semi-bluffing with a draw. Given I have both showdown value with my pair and strong drawing equity to a straight, I have the required pot odds to call. Calling keeps the pot controlled and allows me to see the river, where I can potentially improve to a winning hand or evaluate a fold if the board becomes too dangerous and I miss my draws.

I have A♠8♣ — top pair with the best kicker on a relatively dry board (3♦-8♥-7♠-6♣-2♥).
