Flash opened wide, c‑bet a small flop and called my 600 turn bet; his check on the river looks like weakness or a marginal pair/draw. Checking behind surrenders thin value to worse pairs and floats. A half‑pot probe (≈1/2 pot = 1,200 into 2,400) is the right balance: it charges busted draws, gets called by worse pairs (7x/6x/3x), A‑x floats and some reluctant hands, and avoids bloating the pot where I’m vulnerable to sets/straights. If he raises big I’ll treat it as a polarized value line (sets/two‑pair/rare straights) and fold; call small raises and re‑evaluate. Bet 1,200 for thin value.

The river is a 2h, which is a brick. The final board is 3d-8h-7s-6c-2h. I have a pair of Sevens with a Five kicker. Minni has bet 1200 into a 2400 pot (offering me 3-to-1 odds). While the 6c on the turn opened up straight possibilities (9-5 or 5-4), those are relatively narrow. Minni's lead on the turn and follow-through on the river could represent an 8, two pair, or a missed straight draw/semi-bluff. In heads-up play, a pair of Sevens is often enough to call a half-pot bet, especially since I am the significant chip leader and can afford the call to keep her from bluffing me out of pots. Her line is somewhat suspicious, as she checked the flop and then took over on the turn. I will call to see the showdown.

Heads-up on the button with A♣4♣ — a playable, suited ace with good blockers and postflop playability. Pot is 150 and it’s only the big blind left to act; calling 50 would be fine but gives Flash cheap odds to see a flop and miss position. I want to take the initiative, apply pressure, and simplify postflop play by opening my range. I’ll raise to 300 (≈3x the BB). This size gets fold equity versus a wide BB defense, keeps stacks deep enough to maneuver postflop, and is a standard heads-up open. Plan: c‑bet many flops to capitalize on position; if Flash 3‑bets large I’ll proceed cautiously (likely fold to a large polarized 3‑bet, but consider calling small 3‑bets depending on sizing). Total committed amount = 300.

In a heads-up situation, King-Six offsuit (K6o) is a relatively strong hand for defending the Big Blind against a Small Blind open. I have a significant stack lead over Minni, and the price to call is only 200 chips into a 400-chip pot, giving me 2:1 odds. K6o has sufficient equity against a standard button-opening range to justify seeing a flop. I will call to keep the pot size controlled and look to improve on the flop.

On this dry Ace-high board, the advantage lies with the pre-flop raiser. Holding King-high with no draw, I have very little showdown value and no reason to lead out. Checking is the standard play here to see how my opponent reacts. If Minni bets, I will likely fold given the lack of connectivity and the presence of the Ace, which hits her opening range frequently.

Heads‑up, in position on a very dry A♠7♣3♥ flop with A♣4♣ — top pair but a weak kicker. Flash checked, so I can take the initiative: c‑betting extracts value from worse Ax and medium pairs, folds out pure floats, and protects my hand against unlikely turn improvements. Pot is 600; a half‑pot c‑bet (300) is a balanced size — it gets value without overcommitting with the weak kicker and leaves room to fold or call a raise depending on sizing. Plan: bet 300; if Flash makes a large, polarized raise I’ll strongly consider folding; a small raise I may call and re‑evaluate on the turn.

Minni's 1/3 pot bet on an Ace-high board is a standard continuation bet. I have King-high with no pair and no draw. This board texture favors her pre-flop raising range significantly. Since I have no showdown value and very little equity to improve, folding is the most logical choice to preserve my stack.

In a heads-up situation, QTh is a very strong hand to open from the Button (Small Blind). I want to build a pot and maintain my aggressive image while exercising my positional advantage throughout the rest of the hand. A standard 3x raise is appropriate here.

Facing a 3x open to 300 from the button (pot 400, 200 to call). I have 7♣3♣ — one of the weakest playable hands. Out of position postflop this hand has very poor playability and low realizable equity vs a button opening range, and the call requires ~33% equity to break even preflop. A small 3‑bet bluff to 500 is technically possible, but with such poor blockers and deep stacks it’s a high-variance play I don’t want to take here. Preserve chips and wait for a better spot in heads-up play — fold.

Heads-up on the button with QcJh — a strong, in-position hand that plays well both for value and as a bluff-catcher. The pot is 150 and calling only 50 is cheap, but opening for a standard 3x BB (to 300 total) is the right play: it takes the initiative, isolates the big blind, denies cheap equity to speculative hands, and builds value when we're ahead of a wide BB calling range. Size is consistent with the table dynamics (lots of 300 opens) and keeps postflop decisions straightforward. Plan: if called, continuation-bet on dry Q/J/A-type flops and probe-turn; if faced with a large 3-bet, fold QJo most of the time (re-evaluate vs small 3-bets), and exploit Flash’s calling tendencies. Raise to 300 to apply pressure and play the hand in position.

In a heads-up situation, an Ace-high hand like Ace-Four offsuit is well above the average starting hand strength. Minni has been active from the button, and I have a significant chip lead (24,025 vs 15,575). While I am out of position, the pot odds (calling 200 to win a total pot of 400) make this a mandatory call. Raising is an option to take initiative, but calling allows me to keep the pot controlled and see how the flop develops without over-committing to a marginal kicker.

I have Ace-high on a moderately connected board (7-9-5). As the Big Blind who called a pre-flop raise, checking to the aggressor is the standard and most prudent play. This allows me to see how Minni reacts to the flop. Leading out here would likely only be called or raised by better hands, and checking keeps the pot manageable while I evaluate the strength of my Ace-high against a potential continuation bet.

I opened preflop to 300 and took the initiative. The flop (7d 9h 5c) is dry and misses many hands in a BB’s calling range, so a continuation bet is standard — QcJh has two overcards and reasonable equity versus lots of his calls, plus fold equity. However my effective stack (15,575) vs pot (600) gives a high SPR (~26), so I don’t want to blo﻿at the pot with a marginal hand. I’ll make a pot-manageable c‑bet of about half the pot to gain fold equity and extract value from worse hands while keeping options open for the turn. Plan: if Flash folds we take it; if he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn (follow-up on Q/J or back off on blanks); if he check‑raises big I’ll fold QJo most of the time, only calling small, noncommittal raises with a strong read that he’s light.

On a board of 7d-9h-5c, I have Ace-high. While the board texture is somewhat connected and likely hits a button opening range, my hand still has significant showdown value in a heads-up pot against a continuation bet. The pot odds are 3:1 (calling 300 to win 900), which makes a call with Ace-high standard here. Raising would likely only be called by better hands or draws that have good equity against me, so calling is the best way to keep the pot manageable and see a turn card.

The turn card is the Eight of Hearts, which creates a four-to-a-straight on the board (5-7-8-9). This is an extremely dangerous card for my Ace-high, as any Six in Minni's range now has a straight. Given that Minni raised pre-flop and bet the flop, her range is quite wide and includes many hands that have connected with this board. Out of position and with only Ace-high, leading into the aggressor on this texture is ill-advised. Checking is the standard and safest play, allowing me to see how Minni reacts to the board becoming so coordinated.

Check. The turn 8h is a blank for QcJh — I still have two overcards but no pair or realistic draw (even a heart on the river can only get me to four hearts; a flush is impossible). Flash checked-call the flop, showing a sticky calling range (pairs, small/medium made hands, some draws), so my turn barrel would have poor fold equity and would bloat the pot with deep stacks (effective ≈15,275 vs pot 1,200, SPR ≈12–13). I want to control the pot, realize equity with my overcards, and keep options for the river (call small bets as a bluff-catcher, fold to large aggression, or bluff/value on favorable rivers). Checking keeps the weakest part of my range intact and avoids committing with a marginal hand.

The river card is a Queen of Diamonds, which doesn't improve my Ace-Four. On the turn, the Eight of Hearts completed a possible four-card straight (5-6-7-8-9), and both Minni and I checked. Minni's check behind on the turn suggests she likely doesn't have a Six or a very strong hand, or she is playing cautiously. My Ace-high still has decent showdown value in this heads-up pot. If I bet, I'm unlikely to be called by anything worse than an Ace, and better hands are unlikely to fold. By checking, I can see a showdown for free and potentially win against her missed draws or lower high-card hands.

Heads‑up on the river I have QcJh and the river Qd gives me top pair with a decent kicker. Flash called my flop c‑bet and then checked the turn and river, a line consistent with medium pairs (9x/7x/5x), slow‑played or weak Qx, or marginal hands that will call a small value bet. Pot is 1,200 — I want to extract thin value from worse pairs while keeping the pot controlled and avoiding big confrontations if he check‑raises. A small value bet (~1/3 pot = 400) accomplishes that: it prices in calls from 9/7/5 type hands and many second‑pair holdings, but is small enough that a check‑raise would credibly represent a stronger Q/two‑pair and I can fold without losing a big portion of my stack. Plan vs responses: if called, I take down the extra value at showdown; if Flash check‑raises large I fold (he usually shows strength with that line); if he raises small/light I can call depending on sizing and read. Bet 400 to extract thin value and keep the initiative.

On the river Qd, I have Ace-high (As 4d). The board texture (7-9-5-8-Q) is quite coordinated, but the turn check-back by Minni is a key piece of information. Typically, a player with a straight (any 6) or a very strong hand would bet the turn for value and protection on such a wet board. Her check suggests she likely has a marginal made hand or a draw that didn't materialize. The Queen on the river is an overcard that might have hit her, but her small bet of 400 into a 1200 pot offers me excellent pot odds (4:1). Given that I only need to be right 20% of the time to break even, and my Ace-high beats her potential missed draws or lower high-card bluffs, I will make the call.

I am on the button with 8d 3s, which is a very weak hand. I have a significant stack lead of 23,075 to 16,775 against Minni. While I've been aggressive in many previous hands, 83o is near the bottom of my range. However, in heads-up play, maintaining pressure is important. Given the current stack depths and the fact that I only need to put in 50 more to see a flop, folding is an option, but often folding the small blind is too tight. Nevertheless, 83o is essentially trash and doesn't play well post-flop. I will fold this hand and wait for a better spot to utilize my chip lead.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the small blind) with K♣3♣ — marginal but playable because it’s suited and I have position postflop. Against a single big blind who will call wide, I want to take the initiative and avoid playing OOP. A standard open to ~3x the big blind applies here: it gets folds from the weakest hands, keeps the pot controllable with deep stacks, and lets me leverage position if called. I’ll raise to 300 total (not a min-raise), preserving fold equity and a playable SPR. If Flash 3-bets I can reevaluate based on sizing and fold equity.

With a stack of nearly 23,000 against Minni's 16,625, and only 15 hands remaining in the tournament, I am in a comfortable position. Holding 5s 2c in the Big Blind, I am facing a standard 3x raise from Minni. This hand is extremely weak and does not play well post-flop, especially out of position. Given the tournament context and the need to protect my lead while avoiding unnecessary marginal spots, I will fold this hand and wait for a better opportunity to commit chips.

I am in the Small Blind/Button position with 3h 5c. While I have a significant chip lead (22,925 to 16,925) and am in position post-flop, 3-5 offsuit is a very weak hand. Given that we are at hand 136 of 150, I want to avoid unnecessary risks with bottom-tier hands against an opponent who has shown aggressive tendencies. I will fold and wait for a better spot to maintain my lead.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) with 6♥3♥ — a weak but playable suited connector in position. Pot is 150 and it only costs 50 to see the flop, but heads-up I prefer to open-raise a wide range to seize initiative and exploit the BB. A minimum raise to 200 total keeps the pot manageable, applies pressure to BB’s wide defending range, and preserves stack depth for postflop play in position. Plan: take the lead — if Flash folds we pick up the blinds, if he calls we play flops in position; if he 3-bets large we can release the hand rather than bloating the pot with marginal holdings.

I am in the Big Blind with a very weak hand, Ten of Spades and Deuce of Clubs. Minni has raised to 200 from the Button/Small Blind. While the price to call is small (100 more into a pot of 300), T2o is one of the weakest possible hands and performs poorly post-flop, especially out of position. Given that I have a substantial lead with only 14 hands remaining in the tournament, there is no need to defend with junk hands and risk losing more on later streets. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity to use my chip lead.

I hold Ace-Eight offsuit (A8o) on the button in a heads-up situation. This is a very strong starting hand in this format. I will open with a standard raise to 300 to take control of the pot and put pressure on Minni in the Big Blind.

Heads-up vs a button open to 300, I’m in the big blind with T9s — a very playable, suited connector with good equity and postflop playability against a wide opening range. The pot is 400 and it only costs me 200 more (pot odds ~3:1), so a call requires ~25% equity; T9s easily meets that vs a standard button opening. A tiny 3-bet to 500 would have little fold equity and just bloats the pot out of position; a larger 3-bet would commit too much with a marginal but speculative hand. I’ll call, keep the pot reasonable, and plan to play the flop based on texture (look to continue aggressively on spade/connected flops, and fold or control size on dry/overcards if pressured).

I have T9s on Qc Qh 9d — that’s two pair (Qs and 9s). Heads‑up vs a button open my hand is strong vs a lot of the button’s non‑Q holdings (AK, AJ, medium broadways, some small pocket pairs) but very vulnerable to any queen (trips/full house/quads), pocket pairs above 9 (TT/JJ/etc. make two‑pair that often beats my Q9), or Q9/QQ combos. I’m out of position with deep stacks, so I don’t want to bloat the pot and get raised off my hand by the many hands that dominate me. Flash has shown aggression — checking allows him to barrel with air and worse hands, which I can then call, while also controlling the pot if he has a strong made hand and wants to inflate. Plan: check and evaluate — call small/medium c‑bets to extract from worse hands and fold to extreme overbets/line‑raises that polarize to full houses/quads. If Flash checks back I’ll take the initiative on the turn and re‑assess.

On a paired board of 9d-Qc-Qh, I hold Ace-high. As the pre-flop aggressor, this board texture generally favors my range, which contains more high cards and strong pairs. Minni has checked to me, which is standard. I will place a small continuation bet of 225 chips (slightly over 1/3 pot) to take down the pot now if she missed or to value-protect my Ace-high against her random air. If she calls, I have decent showdown value and can check back many turns.

Board: Qc Qh 9d, my hand Ts9s = two pair (Qs and 9s). Flash (button) has shown aggression and his flop stab is very small (225 into a ~825 pot), which looks like a continuation-bet that can contain lots of bluffs and thin value hands (Kx, Ax, missed broadways) as well as the strong but less frequent hands that crush me (any Q, 99, TT+). I’m out of position, so I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal raise that invites a shove or a big 3‑bet from the polarized part of his range. The price to continue is reasonable (call 225 into ~825 — ~25–30% pot odds), and two pair heads‑up has good showdown value against a wide button range. I’ll call to keep the pot manageable, extract from worse hands, and re‑evaluate on the turn: shove/large raise or fold to heavy action will depend on whether the turn brings a queen/full‑house card or a safe low card and on the sizing of his turn aggression.

I have T9s on Qc Qh 9d 6h — two pair (Queens and Nines). Heads‑up vs the button’s wide open range and a very small flop stab (225 into ~600), Flash’s flop bet looks like a wide mix of bluffs and thin value hands rather than a locked‑in heavy value range. The 6h is a harmless low card: it doesn’t bring a queen or nine or complete any realistic draw, so my two‑pair strength is largely intact. Out of position I prefer to take the initiative and extract value from worse one‑pair hands (Ax, Kx, Tx, small pairs) and charge any turn bluffs rather than just checking and letting him see a free card. I’ll make a modest value bet (~½ pot) to keep the pot controlled but get called by worse; plan is to call small resistance but fold to a polarizing large overbet/all‑in that almost always represents Qx/99/full houses.

I currently hold Ace-high (As 8d). On a board of 9d-Qc-Qh-6h, the texture is quite coordinated. Minni checked-called my small flop continuation bet and has now led out for 525 (half pot) on the turn. This 'donk lead' on the turn after calling the flop usually indicates a made hand that wants to protect against draws or a hand that has improved (like a 9, a pocket pair, or even a slow-played Queen). While Ace-high has some showdown value in heads-up play, calling here likely commits me to a difficult decision on the river if she continues her aggression. Given that I have a significant stack lead and there are only a few hands left in the tournament, I will choose the disciplined path and fold this marginal hand rather than calling down with no pair and no real draw.

Heads-up on the button (SB) with 6♦4♦ — a speculative but playable suited hand. In heads-up play I should be opening very wide; I want initiative and the post-flop positional advantage. Calling 50 is cheap but cedes initiative; the min-raise (200) is allowed but table norms here lean to ~3x opens and a 300 total raise gives cleaner fold equity and keeps the pot manageable with a speculative holding. With deep stacks I’m not committing a lot and can fold to a big 3‑bet, but I’ll take the lead against a call and try to realize equity (flush/straight/backdoor plays) and use position. Raise to 300 total (3x BB).

I am holding Ten of Diamonds and Deuce of Diamonds in the Big Blind. Minni has raised to 300 from the Small Blind. While the hand is suited, T2s is very weak. Looking at the recent hand history, Minni has been quite aggressive and successful in the last several hands. With only 12 hands remaining in the tournament and a significant chip lead (22,200 to 17,400), I want to avoid playing marginal hands out of position against an aggressive opponent. Folding here preserves my stack and allows me to wait for better spots to maintain my lead.

I am currently the chip leader with 22,150 chips against Minni's 17,700 with only 11 hands remaining in the tournament. Holding Eight of Diamonds and Five of Hearts on the button is a weak starting hand. While heads-up play typically encourages a wide opening range, protecting my lead and avoiding unnecessary post-flop complications with a marginal hand is the more prudent strategy at this stage. Folding costs me only 50 chips and keeps my stack safely ahead.

Heads-up on the button with QQ and 178bb effective — this is a premium hand and I want to build the pot and isolate the big blind. I’ll open-raise (not minimum) to a size that gets value from worse hands and reduces the frequency of free flops for speculative holdings. A 4x BB total (400) is a solid, table-consistent sizing here: it builds the pot without committing the stack and avoids inviting cheap multiway action. Postflop I’ll proceed for value on many boards and re-evaluate if Flash 3-bets (call and play postflop against very large 3-bets; consider 4-bet versus small/standard 3-bets depending on sizing).

I am in the Big Blind with 8h 6c. Minni, on the Button, has raised to 400. In a heads-up situation, 86o is a hand that can be played defensively, but given we are at Hand 141 of 150 and I have a significant chip lead (22050 to 17450), I can afford to play somewhat tight. However, calling 300 more into a 500 pot (offering 2.6:1 odds) is generally acceptable to see a flop. Looking at Minni's recent aggression, they have been opening frequently. I will call here to defend my blind and see if I can connect with the flop, keeping the pot manageable unless I hit a strong draw or pair.

On this flop of Qh 2h 2d, I have 8-high with no direct draws. I am out of position as the Big Blind in this heads-up match. Given the paired board and the presence of a Queen, this board is more likely to have hit the pre-flop aggressor's range or to be checked through. I will check to see how Minni reacts, as I have no reason to build a pot with air at this stage of the tournament while holding a chip lead.

I hold QcQs on Qh 2h 2d — that’s a full house (Queens full of deuces), essentially the near-nut on this flop (only quads of deuces beat me). Flash checked, so this is a clear value-spot: I want to charge worse hands (pairs, two-pair, sets, and any two-heart combo draws) and build the pot. I’ll lead out rather than check (checking gives free cards and forgoes value). I size to extract but not scare off too many callers — 2000 into an 800 pot (~2.5x) should get called by most of Flash’s continuing range while still leaving room to call/raise later if necessary. If Flash check-raises very large or jams, that strongly polarizes to quads or a miracle hand; I’ll re-evaluate but my default is to call reasonable aggression with this full house and keep extracting value on turn/river.

Minni has made a massive overbet on the flop, betting 2000 into an 800 chip pot (2.5x pot). On a paired board like Qh 2h 2d, this bet either represents extreme strength (a Deuce or a strong Queen) or a high-variance attempt to buy the pot. Holding only 8-high with no draws and no showdown value, there is no justification for calling this bet. I currently have a chip lead with only 9 hands remaining in the tournament. My priority is to avoid unnecessary risks and preserve my stack to secure the win. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with Qs2s, which is a playable suited hand in a heads-up situation. My stack is larger than Minni's (21,700 vs 18,150). Raising pre-flop allows me to take control of the hand and potentially win the pot immediately or set up a post-flop play. A standard 3x raise is appropriate here to put pressure on the Big Blind.

Heads-up in the big blind with 7♦8♥ (offsuit). Flash opened to 300 from the button/SB. This is a marginal but playable heads-up hand — good equity and playability to see a flop. With stacks deep relative to the pot and being out of position, I prefer to defend cheaply rather than 3-bet light. Calling keeps the pot controlled, realizes equity, and lets me proceed based on the flop texture and opponent’s tendencies. I need to put in 200 more, which is 300 total committed on this street.

Heads-up out of position on a Qc Td 6d flop with 7♦8♥. I called preflop and this board heavily favors the button’s raising range (Qx, Tx, broadways), while my hand has only modest equity: four clean 9s to make a 6-7-8-9-10 straight and a few runner-runner/backdoor possibilities (pairing a 7/8 or two-card 4/5 combos, and a very small backdoor flush chance with my 7♦). Stacks are deep relative to the pot, so I want to keep the pot under control and realize equity rather than bloating the pot with a weak hand. Standard play is to check and respond to Flash’s action: call small/medium c-bets to continue with my draws, fold to heavy pressure or large-sized bets, and consider bluffing or barreling only if the turn gives improved equity or a clear fold equity spot. Checking preserves options and avoids committing chips with only ~15–20% effective equity on this texture.

I hit top pair on the flop with Qs2s. While my kicker is weak, top pair is a significant hand in heads-up play. Minni checked, which is an invitation to bet. I will place a continuation bet of 400 to extract value from marginal hands like a Ten or a Six, and to charge straight draws (like J9 or KJ) to see the next card.

Heads-up out of position with 7♦8♥ on Q♣ T♦ 6♦. I checked the flop and Flash C-bet 400 into the ~600 pot; calling 400 to win ~1400 (pot after my call) requires ~28.6% equity. My hand has only four clean outs to a straight (the 9s) — ~8.5% to hit on the turn and ~17% by the river — plus a very small runner-runner diamond flush possibility, so pure immediate equity is well below the break-even number. However, this is a deep-stacked heads-up spot against a wide button range that C-bets lightly; if I hit a 9 (or pair a 7/8 or pick up a diamond) there are strong implied odds to extract from many of his holdings (Qx, Tx, Kx). The bet size is standard rather than polarizing, so I prefer to realize equity and see a turn rather than folding and letting him profitably C-bet me off many flops. Plan: call the 400 to see the turn; if I miss and face heavy pressure (large turn shove/big sizing) I’ll fold, while I’ll proceed/raise when I actually improve (9/7/8/diamond) or if the turn gives a very favorable fold-equity spot.

Board on the turn: 6♦ Q♣ T♦ J♦. I hold 7♦8♥ — a gutshot to a 9 and a one-card-away diamond flush on the river. Unique outs = 4 nines + 9 remaining diamonds − 1 (9♦ double-counted) = 12 outs, roughly 26% to improve on the river. Pot is only 1400 and stacks are deep. Flash bet the flop and I called; there is no turn bet to me now, so I should not bloat the pot with a weak semi-bluff out of position. Checking preserves my equity, keeps a lot of worse hands in that I can potentially extract from when I hit, and lets me fold to large river aggression if I miss. Plan: check the turn, see the river; if checked to again I may bet a realized hand or call a small river bet with these outs, but will fold to heavy pressure unless I improve. Given my current equity and the line I took, checking is the disciplined, +EV line.

The Jack of Diamonds is a very coordinated card. It completes potential straights (like K9 or 98) and puts a third diamond on the board. While I still have top pair, my kicker is extremely weak (Deuce), and Minni calling the flop suggests she has a piece of this board or a draw. Checking behind here allows me to control the pot size on a dangerous board and potentially see a showdown or a safer river card without facing a check-raise.

Heads‑up, pot is 1400 and it’s checked to me on the river holding 7♦8♥ on 6♦ Q♣ T♦ J♦ 2♣. I have essentially no showdown value (7‑high) — only beat rare busted high‑card hands. Flash’s line (open pre, small c‑bet flop, check turn) is consistent with a range weighted toward at least one pair (Qx/Tx/Jx) or pot‑control with a medium hand; two‑diamond pocket combos that have a made flush are possible but rare. To bluff profitably I need meaningful fold equity: e.g. a half‑pot bet (≈700) needs >33% folds, a full‑pot needs >50%, a small probe (≈400) needs >22%. I don’t hold strong blockers (no A♦/K♦) and I expect many hands in his range to call small river bets for showdown, so my fold equity is likely below the threshold. Betting risks spewing chips when called; checking preserves stack and lets me fold to a river bet if he suddenly barrels. With the information and stack sizes, the disciplined +EV play is to check and see the showdown (fold to any river bet).

The river 2c improves my hand to two pair (Queens and Deuces). However, the board is quite dangerous with three diamonds and potential straights (any King or 98). Minni's check on the river after calling the flop and checking the turn suggests she may have a medium-strength hand like a Jack, a Ten, or perhaps a weak Queen that is afraid of the board. While my two pair is strong in heads-up play, the coordination of the board (straight and flush possibilities) makes it risky to value bet. Checking behind allows me to realize my equity and win the pot at showdown without risking a check-raise from a hidden straight or flush.

Raise to 300. Heads-up on the button with K♠5♠ this is well inside my open-raise range — it has suit, a King blocker and good postflop playability in position. With stacks deep (~175bb) I want to take the initiative and use fold equity rather than limp and give a free flop. A standard 3xBB-sized open (to 300) matches table norms, pressures wide BB defense, and keeps the pot controllable if called. Postflop plan: play aggressively on dry flops (c-bet to take it down), evaluate texture and equities on coordinated boards, and exercise pot control or fold to heavy aggression when dominated. If Flash 3-bets big, I’ll release K5s — no need to get stacked off with a marginal holding.

I have Ace-Jack offsuit in the Big Blind. This is a premium hand in heads-up play. Minni has been quite active with button raises. I will put in a 3-bet to 1000 for value and to gain initiative, as I will be out of position post-flop. This forces Minni to either fold or play a bloated pot against a range that dominates many of her opening hands.

Flash has 3-bet to 1,000 after my 3x open to 300. Stacks are deep (~172bb effective) and I'm in position. K♠5♠ is marginal but has a King blocker, is suited and plays very well in position postflop. Flash’s 3-bet size is fairly small (1000 total) and from his history he shows an aggressive/wide 3-bet tendency — this makes calling reasonable. Calling 700 more (1000 total committed) gets me good postflop playability and implied odds; a 4-bet bluff here is too thin and folding would be overly tight given position. Plan: call, see flop; c-bet dry textures, proceed cautiously on coordinated boards, fold to heavy 4/5-bet aggression or big resistance when clearly behind.

On a low, connected board like 6d-4s-7s, my 3-betting range (which consists mostly of high cards and pairs) is at a slight disadvantage compared to Minni's calling range, which contains more middling connectors and small pairs. Having missed the flop with Ace-Jack, I will check to see how Minni reacts. This allows me to keep the pot small and potentially catch a pair on the turn or see if she checks back with her own air. Betting into this specific board texture out of position is risky since she is unlikely to fold any piece of the board or even a decent draw.

Flop: 6♦ 4♠ 7♠ — I have K♠5♠: the nut-ish spade draw (K blocker) plus a 4-card straight draw (4-5-6-7 needs a 3 or 8). That’s ~15 unique outs (~54% to improve by the river). Flash’s line (small preflop 3-bet, then checking the flop) looks wide/pot-control or weak — good spot to take the initiative in position. With strong drawing equity and deep stacks I want to build the pot and charge worse draws/overcards while retaining fold equity. A half-pot c-bet (~1,000 into 2,000) accomplishes that: folds some showdown hands, gets called by hands I dominate or can outdraw, and keeps the pot manageable if I face aggression. Plan: bet 1,000; if called I proceed cautiously on turn (continue with equity or fold to large resistance if the turn bricks and villain shows strength). If Flash check-raises big, I’ll give up the hand.

I 3-bet pre-flop with Ace-Jack offsuit to take control, but the flop of 6d-4s-7s is very poor for my range. This board texture heavily favors Minni's calling range, which is likely to contain pocket pairs, suited connectors, and one-pair hands that connect with these middle cards. After I checked, Minni's bet of 1,000 into a 2,000 pot is a standard sizing that could represent value or a semi-bluff with a draw. Although I have two overcards and decent pot odds (4:1), I am out of position on a highly coordinated board. Since we are approaching the final hands of the tournament and I currently hold the chip lead, I will prioritize stack preservation and avoid a high-variance spot where I am likely behind. Folding here allows me to maintain my lead without committing more chips to a hand where I have little more than high-card value.

I am on the button with a pocket pair of treys (3c 3s). In a heads-up situation, any pocket pair is a strong starting hand. I will follow my established strategy of raising to 300 to build the pot and take control of the hand pre-flop.

Heads-up against an aggressive Button who opened to 300 from the SB, I’ll defend the big blind with 9♦2♦ by calling. The pot is 400 and I need to call 200 (total commitment to 300) — break-even equity ≈ 200/(400+200) = 33%. 9♦2♦ is flimsy but suited and has backdoor flush/straight potential heads‑up; folding too many hands from the BB to frequent SB opens would be exploitable. A 3‑bet bluff to the min (500) with this hand is too thin — it lacks blockers and I don’t want to invest more out of position. I’ll call, see the flop, and proceed cautiously OOP: look to continue only with meaningful improvement or favorable board texture, otherwise fold to significant aggression.

I check. I have 9♦2♦ and I flopped a weak pair (pair of 2s with a 9 kicker) — a hand with some showdown value but vulnerability out of position. Flash opened preflop with a wide, aggressive range, so many of his c‑bet hands (Qx, big overcards, some draws) beat me. Betting for thin value is unattractive and check‑raising as a bluff is risky without blockers or fold equity. Checking controls the pot, lets me call small continuation bets to realize equity (and exploit over‑frequent cbets), and fold to large, polarizing pressure. Plan: check and call modest bets (roughly ≤1/3 pot ≈ 200) to see the turn; fold to sizable aggression unless I pick up a clear improvement or a specific read that Flash is bluffing.

I have a pocket pair of treys on a relatively dry board of 2c-Qd-8h. While the Queen is an overcard, my pre-flop raise allows me to represent a wider range of strong hands. Minni checked to me, which often indicates weakness or a marginal hand. I will place a continuation bet of 300, which is half the pot, to put pressure on her air and non-pairing cards and potentially take the pot down now before an overcard falls on the turn.

Flop: 2♣ Q♦ 8♥. I hold 9♦2♦ — a small pair (pair of 2s) with a weak kicker and a very long backdoor diamond draw. Flash (SB) opened wide preflop and is an aggressive c-bettor, so his flop betting range will include a lot of overcards, bluffs and some vulnerable one-pair hands. He bet 300 into ~600 (a 0.5‑pot c‑bet). Calling 300 gives me ~25% break‑even odds (300/(900+300)=25%), and versus a wide c‑bet range my hand has reasonable showdown equity. I’m out of position so I won’t inflate the pot by bluff‑raising; instead I call to realize equity, see the turn and re‑evaluate. If I spike trips/2‑pair or pick up a diamond I can proceed more aggressively; if the turn brings a clean overcard and he shows heavy action I’ll fold. Deep stacks make this a cheap look.

I hold 9♦2♦ and the turn (T♠) paired the board: 2♣ Q♦ 8♥ T♠. My 2♦ makes only a small one-pair (pair of deuces) with a weak 9 kicker. The backdoor diamond possibility is now dead. Flash is a wide, aggressive opener who c-bet the flop (I called); his check on the turn looks like pot control or weakness rather than strength. Out of position with a marginal made hand I should avoid bloating the pot. Checking controls the size, keeps worse hands and bluffs in, and lets me see the river for a relatively cheap showdown. Plan: check and call reasonable river bets (or fold to large polarizing aggression); if I improve to trips/two-pair on the river I can start extracting value.

On the turn, the board is 2c-Qd-8h-Ts. I still only have my pocket pair of treys. The Ten of Spades is another overcard, and it connects with several straight draws (J9, 97). Minni has checked again. While I could bet to try and take it down, Minni called my flop continuation bet, suggesting she has at least a piece of the board or a draw she isn't ready to fold yet. Checking behind allows me to control the size of the pot and see a river for free, potentially realizing my showdown value if the river is safe, or folding if she bets big on a scary river card.

Check. I have only a weak pair of deuces (9♦2♦) and the river paired the board with tens, making my hand very vulnerable to any Ten, Queen, or better two-pair/sets/straights that could beat me. Both players checked the turn, which looks like pot‑control/weakness rather than a clear giveaway; betting here for thin value is unlikely to get called by worse very often and will get me raised by the hands that already beat me. With 1,200 in the pot and deep stacks, I should avoid bloating the pot with a marginal hand — checking preserves showdown and lets me fold to polarizing aggression (or call a small river stab). I’ll check to see a showdown; if Flash now bets a small, non‑committal amount I can consider a call, but I won’t lead out here with a bluff or thin value bet.

On the river, the board has paired with Tens (Qd-8h-2c-Ts-Th). My pocket Treys have now become two pair: Tens and Treys (Th-Ts-3c-3s-Qd). Minni called my flop continuation bet on a Q-8-2 board and has checked through the turn and river. Her range for calling the flop likely includes a Queen, an Eight, a pocket pair, or a straight draw like J9. If she has an 8 or any pocket pair higher than Treys (like 44-77), she has a better two pair than me. If she has a Queen, she also wins. Given that she checked, I have some showdown value against missed draws or high-card hands (like Ace-high), but I'm unlikely to get a better hand to fold or a worse hand to call if I bet. Checking is the most prudent action to reach showdown cheaply.

Heads-up on the button with AhJh suited and deep stacks (~179bb effective). This is a strong raising hand — I want to take the initiative and play last postflop. A min-raise (to 200) is allowed but too small; opening to a standard ~3x (300) isolates the big blind, builds value against Flash’s likely wide defending range, and preserves postflop maneuverability with position. Plan: c-bet favorable flops for value, control the pot on marginal boards, and be prepared to call a reasonable 3-bet given the deep stacks or 4-bet selectively depending on sizing/reads.

In a heads-up match with a stack lead, 9d 7d is a very strong hand to defend from the Big Blind against a standard 3x open. It has excellent post-flop playability and is well ahead of Minni's likely wide opening range. With only a few hands left in the tournament, calling allows me to see a flop and control the pot size without unnecessary risk.

I have 9-high on a board that significantly favors Minni's pre-flop raising range (containing many King and Jack combinations). As the Big Blind acting first, the standard play is to check to the aggressor. With no immediate draws and only runner-runner potential, I will check and likely fold to any significant bet to preserve my stack lead with only 5 hands remaining in the tournament.

I raised preflop and hold AhJh on Kh Jc 5c — second pair with the best Ace kicker but vulnerable to Kx and club combinations. Flash checked, so I want to both extract value from worse jacks/Ax and deny free equity to club draws. I’ll take the initiative with a standard-sized continuation bet ~1/2 pot (600 pot → bet 300). This size charges draws and gets value from worse pairs while keeping the pot manageable given deep stacks. Plan: if Flash calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and play cautiously (check-control or thin value on safe turns); if he check-raises or raises big, I’ll fold most of the time as that line strongly represents Kx/strong made hands or large draws.

I have 9-high on a board that heavily favors my opponent's pre-flop raising range (containing Kings, Jacks, and high-card combinations). With only 5 hands remaining in the tournament and a substantial stack lead of 4,400 chips, my primary objective is to minimize unnecessary risk. Since I have no direct draws and very little showdown value, calling even a small bet is an unprofitable use of chips. I will fold to preserve my lead.

With a comfortable lead of over 3,500 chips and only 5 hands remaining in the tournament, my primary objective is to protect my stack and minimize risk. 4s 6c is a very weak starting hand. While I am on the button, the marginal benefit of trying to steal the blinds with such a poor hand does not outweigh the risk of getting involved in a pot against an aggressive opponent like Minni. Folding here preserves my lead and moves us closer to the end of the tournament.

Heads-up on the button with Ah8c — a strong, playable ace with a good blocker and position. Pot is 150 and it’s only 50 to call, but I want the initiative and to build a pot in position while keeping worse aces and many dominated hands in. I’ll take the standard aggressive line and raise to the min allowed (200): preserves postflop maneuverability, keeps fold equity vs the big blind, and lets me continuation-bet a wide range on many flops. If Flash 3-bets large I’ll re-evaluate (fold to extreme sizing, consider calling smaller 3-bets to play postflop in position).

In a heads-up situation with Hand #147 of 150, I am currently the chip leader with 21,600 against Minni's 18,100. Minni has min-raised to 200 from the button. I am in the big blind holding 9c 5c. I need to call only 100 more to see a flop in a pot that will be 400. This gives me 3:1 pot odds. 95 suited is a hand that has reasonable playability post-flop, and since we are nearing the end of the tournament, I want to defend my big blind efficiently without over-committing to a marginal hand. Folding is too weak given the price, and raising is unnecessary as I want to keep the pot controlled while maintaining my lead.

I have called pre-flop with 9c 5c to defend my big blind. The flop comes Qs 4s 4d, which is a paired board and does not significantly help my hand beyond a very thin backdoor flush draw. In a heads-up situation against the pre-flop aggressor, the standard play is to check to the raiser and evaluate their action. I have no reason to lead out here with 9-high.

I raised preflop and Flash checked the flop — this is a classic heads-up continuation-bet spot. Board: Qs 4s 4d is relatively dry (paired small card with a Q high) and will fold out a lot of the BB caller’s weaker Kx, Jx, Tx and small pocket pairs. With Ah8c I have Ace-high and a key blocker to some strong Ax/AQ combos, so I’m ahead of a large portion of his checking range and have decent fold equity. I’ll bet for value and protection but keep the sizing manageable to allow folding to a big raise: bet ~60% of the pot (250 into 400). Plan: if he folds I take the pot; if he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn (barrel selectively on safe cards, check back vs obvious draw/runner spades or if a Q/4 pairs the board further); if he raises big I’ll assume strong made hands (trips/Qx) and will fold most of the time given I have only Ace-high.

I am the chip leader with 21,500 to Minni's 17,850 and there are only four hands remaining in the tournament. My goal is to protect this lead. On a board of Qs 4s 4d, my 9c 5c has no showdown value and no significant draws. Minni's bet of 250 into a 400 pot represents a strong range or at least a hand with better equity than mine. There is no reason to risk chips with 9-high when I can easily fold and maintain a comfortable lead heading into the final three hands.

Holding KTh on the button in a heads-up situation near the end of the tournament, I have a very strong hand. With only three hands left including this one, and holding a chip lead of about 3,000, I want to apply pressure and build the pot with my premium holdings. A standard 3x raise to 300 is consistent with my previous play and allows me to maintain control of the hand with position.

Heads-up vs a 3x button open (300). J♠9♣ is a reasonable defending hand in the big blind — it has good equity vs a wide button range (hits top pair, two-pair, straights and useful backdoor draws). Calling keeps the pot controlled and lets me realize equity out of position; a small reraise (to 500) is unattractive because I'll be out of position in a big deep-stacked pot and could face a larger 4-bet. Pot odds: I must put in 200 more to win ~600 (≈33% equity required) — J9o comfortably justifies a call heads-up. Plan: call to see a flop; proceed aggressively if I hit (pair, good draw), otherwise play cautiously OOP and fold to heavy action when appropriate.

I hold Js9c on JcJh9s — that’s a full house (Jacks full of Nines) on the flop. Heads‑up versus a button open, Flash’s range is wide and contains plenty of worse hands that will call for value (KJ/QJ/AJ, 99, 9x, some pocket pairs and single‑pair Jx hands). Only an extremely narrow subset of hands (the lone remaining Jack in his hand giving quads) can beat me. I should be extracting value rather than slowplaying. I’ll make a sizable value bet to charge all worse hands and build the pot: bet 6000 into the 600 chip pot (~10× pot, ~1/3 of my stack). If he jams/raises very large I’ll be willing to call because almost everything that raises me here is still behind except quads.

Minni has made a massive overbet of 10x the pot (6000 into 600). I am holding an open-ended straight draw with overcards (Kh Th), but the price is extremely poor. To call, I need roughly 48% equity. If Minni is holding a Jack (trips), I only have about 32% equity with two cards to come. If she has a pocket pair or a 9, I'm roughly 50/50. Given that there are only three hands left in the tournament and I have a significant chip lead of approximately 9,000, there is no need to take a high-variance risk that could lose me the lead. Folding is the safest and most professional play to secure the tournament victory.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) with J♦Q♣ — a strong, aggressive-opening hand in heads-up play. Pot is 150 and it only costs 50 to call, but I want to use position and apply pressure to Flash's wide big-blind defending range. A standard 3xBB open (raise to 300 total) builds the pot, gains fold equity, and leaves plenty of stack depth to maneuver postflop (I still have ~18750). Plan: if Flash calls we see a flop in position and c-bet most dry/high-card flops; if he 3-bets small I'll consider calling to realize equity and play postflop; if he 3-bets large or jams I can fold/reassess based on sizing and range. Raising is the +EV play here to steal the blind and play the hand on my terms.

I hold Ace-seven offsuit in the Big Blind. This is a strong hand heads-up. Given I have a slight lead and the tournament is ending in two hands, I will call the raise to see a flop and maintain pot control, rather than risking a large portion of my stack with a 3-bet.

I have hit top pair with an Ace kicker on a relatively low, coordinated board. In heads-up play, the Big Blind acts first post-flop. I will check here to the pre-flop aggressor. This allows Minni to continue with her bluffs or semi-bluffs (like club draws or straight draws) while keeping the pot manageable as I enter the final two hands of the tournament with a slight lead.

Heads-up I opened preflop and hold QcJd on 3c-6s-7c: two clean overcards plus a single‑club backdoor. This is a very standard c-bet spot — the flop is relatively dry and favors my raising range, and Flash in the BB defends widely so a half‑pot stab will pick up a lot of hands (unpaired air, weaker overcards, many floats) while keeping the pot controllable if called. I’ll bet ~1/2 pot (300) to apply pressure and realize equity; if he jams or gives a large check‑raise I’ll fold, if he calls I’ll continue on turns that improve my equity (Q/J/club) and re‑evaluate on blank turns.

I have top pair with an Ace kicker on a 3-6-7 board. This is a very strong hand in heads-up play. However, the board is coordinated with straight and flush possibilities. Given that I have a chip lead and there are only two hands remaining in the tournament, my primary goal is to protect that lead while still extracting value. Calling the flop bet allows me to keep the pot size manageable and evaluate the turn. This avoids a potential high-variance stack-off if Minni is holding a set or two pair, while still keeping her wide betting range in play.

I have a pair of 7s with an Ace kicker. The 9 on the turn is an overcard to my pair, which makes the board slightly more dangerous as it connects with potential straight draws (8-10, 5-8) and gives Minni's 9x hands the lead. As I am currently holding a chip lead of 2,400 with only two hands left in the tournament, my priority is pot control and protecting my stack. Checking is the most prudent play to keep the pot manageable and see how Minni proceeds without overcommitting.

Flop: I raised pre and c-bet the dry 3♣-6♠-7♣ board with Q♣J♦ and was called. Turn 9♦ is a complete brick for my hand — it doesn’t pair me, it kills the slim backdoor club runner-runner, and leaves me with just two overcards. Flash checks the turn, showing either weakness/ pot control with a marginal pair or a slow/float. Pot is only 1,200 and both stacks are deep relative to the pot. Betting the turn here buys little: worse hands (small pairs) will often call, better hands are never folding, and if I get called I’m likely in a tough spot on the river with no clean improvements. Checking keeps the pot small, preserves fold equity for a later river bluff, and lets me realize my equity cheaply — I can barrel strong rivers (Q/J/club) for value or bluff on a scare card. If Flash leads into me I can fold and avoid bloating the pot with only overcards. Standard, position-first pot control — check.

On the river, the Ace of Diamonds has given me top pair (Aces) with a Seven kicker. The board is relatively coordinated (3-6-7-9-A), but there are no completed flushes. While I likely have the best hand, the tournament is at hand 149 of 150, and I currently hold a chip lead of 2,400. My primary objective is to secure the tournament win by avoiding any high-variance situations that could jeopardize my stack. By checking, I control the size of the pot and allow Minni to potentially bluff with missed draws or value bet weaker hands. If Minni checks behind, I win a decent pot and maintain a safe lead going into the final hand. If she bets, I will evaluate the sizing, but a check is the most strategically sound move to minimize risk while ahead.

I checked the turn per my plan to keep the pot small with two overcards. River A♦ changes the board but Flash checked it — he check‑called the flop, then pot‑controlled the turn and river, which looks like a missed hand or a weak pair rather than strength (if he had an Ace he’d often bet for value). This is a classic river bluff spot heads‑up: my Q♣J♦ has showdown equity only vs air but I can credibly represent an Ace or a 9/7/6 that improved. The pot is 1,200 and my stack is deep, so I’ll apply pressure with a sizing that folds out most weak pairs/missed hands but doesn’t commit a large portion of my stack — ~2/3 pot (800). With a bet of 800 I only need ≈40% fold equity to make this +EV (800/(1200+800)=0.4). Plan: bet 800 to try to steal the pot; if Flash raises I’ll fold (I have no showdown value), if he calls I lose the pot and muck.

I have improved to Two Pair (Aces and Sevens) on the river. This is an exceptionally strong hand in a heads-up situation. While there are straight possibilities on the board (8-10 or 5-8), Minni's check on the turn makes these hands less likely, as she likely would have bet for value or protection. Her river bet of 800 into a 1200 pot looks like a value bet with a single pair of Aces or perhaps a bluff. Given that this is hand 149 of 150 and I currently hold the chip lead, my main priority is to reach the final hand with that lead intact. Calling is the safest play; it allows me to win a significant pot if I'm ahead while ensuring that I remain the chip leader even in the unlikely event that I am beat. Raising would introduce unnecessary risk of a re-raise or a shove from a superior hand, which could jeopardize my overall tournament position.

This is the final hand of the tournament. I hold 9s 8d on the button, which is a solid hand to open in heads-up play. I have a comfortable lead of over 5,000 chips. I will put in a standard raise to 300 to exert pressure and attempt to win the blinds. If Minni plays back aggressively, I can evaluate based on the flop, but the primary goal is to maintain my lead while looking for a final opportunity to add to my stack.

Heads-up in the big blind with A♥5♣ facing a standard SB open to 300. I have to put in 200 more (300 total) to see the flop. A5o is a strong defensive hand heads-up — it dominates worse A-x and has decent playability. I'm out of position post-flop, so 3-betting OOP with a marginal hand isn't appealing: it gives fold equity but also creates difficult post-flop spots versus a wide but competent opener. With deep stacks there's good implied equity to realize by calling and seeing a flop. Plan: call the 200 (300 total) to keep the pot manageable, then evaluate on the flop — value/raise when I hit an ace or strong draw, fold or exercise pot control if I miss and face significant aggression OOP.

Out of position on the flop with A♥5♣ against a wide button opener. I only have Ace-high (single heart backdoor) — no pair, no real draw — but my Ace blocks a bunch of the opponent’s Ax combos and has decent showdown value heads-up. Flash is a frequent opener/c-bettor, so leading out OOP with a marginal hand would build a pot I don’t want to play big out of position; betting here will get called by many worse hands (Jx, Tx, Kx, flush/straight draws) and put me in tough spots on turn/river. I’ll check to control the pot and allow myself to call reasonable c-bets to realize my equity; if Flash fires a small-to-medium continuation bet I’ll usually call and re-evaluate on the turn, folding to large, polarized pressure if I haven’t improved. If Flash checks back I get a free turn to try and pair my Ace or pick up backdoor equity. Checking preserves options and keeps the pot manageable OOP.

I have flopped middle pair (8s) and an open-ended straight draw (any 7 or Q). Minni checked to me on a very connected board. I have significant equity with 8 outs to a straight plus my middle pair. I will place a standard continuation bet of 400 (two-thirds pot) to take the initiative, protect my hand from overcards, and extract value. If called, I have many favorable turn cards.

Heads-up out of position with A♥5♣ on T♠ J♣ 8♥. I checked the flop to control the pot and now face a standard 400 c-bet into a ~1000 pot (to call 400). Against Flash’s wide SB opening/c-betting range my Ace has decent showdown value and blocks lots of Ax combos — calling is inexpensive versus my stack (400 of 17,100) and the pot odds (~28.6%) are reasonable to realize equity. Raising OOP with naked Ace-high is too thin and will create difficult spots; folding surrenders too much vs a wide c-bettor. Plan: call 400, see a turn (raise or value bet only if I pair/clearly improve; fold to large polarized pressure if I don’t improve and face strong aggression).

I paired my Ace on the turn (A5 vs T♠ 8♥ J♣ A♣). Out of position vs Flash — a wide opener and frequent c-bettor — I now have top pair but a very weak kicker and several parts of his range still beat me (sets, two-pair, and flopped straights). I want to extract value from worse A-x, Jx and Tx hands and protect against any rare backdoor club draws, but I don’t want to bloat the pot out of position given how many hands dominate my kicker. A modest half‑pot value bet achieves that: it charges worse hands and gives me information while keeping discomfort against a large raise manageable. Bet ~700 into the 1400 pot; call/reevaluate on a call, and respect large, polarized raises (fold unless I have strong evidence otherwise).

On the turn, an Ace appears, which is a significant card that likely hits Minni's range. However, I have a pair of eights and an open-ended straight draw (any 7 or Q completes the straight). Minni has led out for 700 into a pot of 1400, giving me 3:1 pot odds. Given my equity with the straight draw and the possibility that my pair of eights is still ahead of a semi-bluff or a lower pair, calling is the most mathematically sound decision. There is no need to raise and risk a large portion of my chip lead on the final hand of the tournament, as calling allows me to see the river and potentially hit a powerhouse hand.

I have top pair (A♥) with a weak 5 kicker on a T♠-8♥-J♣-A♣-2♥ runout. Villain is a wide, frequent opener and called both my flop and my modest turn bet, so his calling range is full of worse A-x, Jx and Tx hands plus some Kx/Qx. Betting a small value bet here extracts calls from those worse hands while still controlling the pot out of position. Pot is ~2800 — a ~35% pot bet pressures pure air and gets value from Kx/Qx/Jx/TX and worse aces, while allowing me to fold to a large raise (which would usually represent sets/two‑pair/strong A‑kinds). Checking risks missing thin value or giving villain a free card to pair up and beat my weak kicker. Plan: make a small value bet to be called by worse and avoid bloating the pot. If he raises big I will fold.

This is the final hand of the tournament, and I have a significant chip lead (21,200 to 15,000). On a board of Ts-8h-Jc-Ac-2h, my hand is only a pair of eights with a nine kicker. The board is extremely connected, with several straights possible (KQ, Q9) and the Ace appearing on the turn, which is a card Minni led into. Minni's line of calling the flop and then leading the turn and river strongly suggests she has at least a top pair of Aces, two pair (like J-T or A-J), or a straight. A pair of eights is essentially a bluff-catcher here, and it is very unlikely to be the best hand given the action. By folding, I preserve my substantial lead and guarantee a first-place finish in the tournament without risking an unnecessary call on the final hand.
